## Agent 5 response — adversarial Gate 1 counterexample hunt

I am using the Agent 5 assignment: try to falsify Gate 1 by building progressively more realistic source-flat models, then identify which constraint first kills the model. 

## Executive verdict

I can break Gate 1 in the following models:

\[
\text{local positive packet}
\rightarrow
\text{quartet-symmetric packet}
\rightarrow
\text{finite zero-counting compatible packet}
\rightarrow
\text{packet plus smooth }B_2
\rightarrow
\text{packet plus flexible AFE-like dual/background terms}
\rightarrow
\text{packet plus finite }Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\text{ constraint}.
\]

I **cannot** yet break Gate 1 after imposing full actual-zeta zero–prime compatibility:

\[
q''_{\can}
=
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

with fixed actual coefficients and a controlled zero-side/AFE-side agreement error.

So the first genuinely lethal constraint is not quartet symmetry, zero-counting, \(B_2\), a smooth \(D_2\), or \(Z_\alpha\). It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed-coefficient actual-zeta AFE / explicit-formula compatibility.}
}
\]

No stop-rule trigger yet. I do **not** have a realistic source-flat family surviving

\[
B_2,\quad D_2,\quad E_2,\quad Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,\quad
\text{and zero-side/AFE agreement}.
\]

But all weaker/local constraints are too soft to prove Gate 1.

---

# 1. Base adversary: positive same-scale quadrature

Use the normalized packet kernel

\[
K_\theta(x)=
\frac{\sin\theta}{(x-\cos\theta)^2+\sin^2\theta},
\qquad 0<\theta<\pi.
\]

Its Taylor coefficients at \(x=0\) satisfy

\[
K_\theta^{(n)}(0)=n!\sin((n+1)\theta).
\]

For a positive packet

\[
Q(x)=\sum_{j=1}^N w_jK_{\theta_j}(x),
\qquad w_j>0,
\]

source-curvature flattening through order \(R\) is equivalent to

\[
Q''(0)=Q^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=Q^{(R+2)}(0)=0,
\]

i.e.

\[
\sum_{j=1}^N w_j\sin(k\theta_j)=0,
\qquad 3\le k\le R+3.
\]

The deterministic quadrature construction

\[
\theta_j=\frac{j\pi}{N+1},
\qquad
w_j=\frac{2}{N+1}\sin\theta_j
\]

gives

\[
\sum_{j=1}^N w_j\sin(k\theta_j)=\delta_{k1},
\qquad 1\le k\le N.
\]

Therefore, for \(N\ge R+3\),

\[
Q''(0)=Q^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=Q^{(R+2)}(0)=0.
\]

Because \(Q''\) is analytic on a fixed compact interval \(|x|\le X<1\), Taylor remainder gives

\[
\sup_{|x|\le X}|Q''(x)|
\le C_R X^{R+1}.
\]

Hence

\[
\int_{-X}^{X}|Q''(x)|^2\,dx
\le C_R^2 X^{2R+3}.
\]

With \(R\sim C\log Q\), this can beat any prescribed polynomial threshold \(Q^{-A}\) by choosing \(C\) large enough. With \(R\gg\log Q\), it is genuinely super-polynomial in \(Q\).

So the pure local packet model decisively breaks Gate 1.

---

# 2. Quartet symmetry does not kill the adversary

The deterministic nodes are already symmetric:

\[
\theta_{N+1-j}=\pi-\theta_j,
\qquad
w_{N+1-j}=w_j.
\]

For paired angles,

\[
\sin(k(\pi-\theta))
=
(-1)^{k+1}\sin(k\theta).
\]

Thus equal-weight pairing automatically enforces the expected left/right parity structure:

\[
\sum_j w_j\sin(k\theta_j)
=
0
\quad \text{for the parity-forbidden modes.}
\]

The remaining modes are killed by the same sine quadrature identity.

So quartet symmetry is not an obstruction. The adversary survives.

---

# 3. Finite zero-counting does not kill it

The construction needs only

\[
N\ge R+3
\]

same-scale source points, or \(O(R)\) points after quartet completion.

For the contemplated depths

\[
R(Q)\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}
\quad\text{or}\quad
R(Q)=O(\log Q),
\]

this is too small to contradict crude local zero-counting allowances. Standard local counting constraints of the form

\[
N(T+H)-N(T-H)
\lesssim H\log T+\log T
\]

do not prohibit \(O(R)\) same-scale points unless one already has a much sharper local repulsion or anti-design theorem. Pair correlation, simplicity, and quartet symmetry are still compatible with this kind of finite packet design.

So finite zero-counting does not kill the adversary.

---

# 4. Smooth \(B_2\) does not kill it

Now replace the pure flattening condition by cancellation against a smooth background:

\[
F(x)=Q''(x)+\lambda^3B_2(m_0+\lambda x).
\]

Let

\[
b_r
=
-\partial_x^r\left[\lambda^3B_2(m_0+\lambda x)\right]_{x=0}.
\]

We need

\[
\partial_x^rQ''(0)=b_r,
\qquad 0\le r\le R.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\sum_j w_j\sin((r+3)\theta_j)
=
c_r,
\qquad 0\le r\le R,
\]

for explicit constants \(c_r\) proportional to \(b_r/(r+2)!\).

This is a finite moment-matching problem. It is still breakable.

Reason: choose more nodes than constraints. The sine moment matrix

\[
M_{rj}=\sin((r+3)\theta_j),
\qquad 0\le r\le R,
\]

has full row rank for generic distinct \(\theta_j\). Therefore signed solutions exist. Positivity can be restored by adding a large positive vector from the homogeneous flattening cone:

\[
M w^{(0)}=0,
\qquad w^{(0)}_j>0,
\]

provided by the deterministic quadrature construction. Thus

\[
w=w^{(1)}+A w^{(0)}
\]

solves the same moment equations and is positive for \(A\gg1\).

So \(B_2\), being finite-jet smooth data, only changes the right-hand side. It does not kill source flattening.

---

# 5. Flexible \(D_2\) / AFE-like terms do not kill it

If the dual term is treated as another smooth or finite-band analytic function

\[
D_2^{\mathrm{flex}}(m),
\]

then it simply modifies the target moments:

\[
c_r
=
-\partial_x^r
\left[
\lambda^3(B_2+D_2^{\mathrm{flex}}+E_2)(m_0+\lambda x)
\right]_{x=0}.
\]

The same full-rank moment argument applies.

Thus the adversary survives any model in which \(D_2\) is merely “AFE-like” but not tied to fixed actual-zeta coefficients.

This is important: a relaxed main/dual surrogate is not enough to prove Gate 1. The coefficients must be actual, fixed, and globally coupled.

---

# 6. The off-line center condition \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) does not kill finite-source flattening by itself

Treat \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) as one additional real constraint, or two real constraints if complex-valued before taking the relevant real condition.

In the local packet model, this has the schematic form

\[
\mathcal Z(m_0,\lambda,\theta,w)=0.
\]

If its finite-jet linearization is

\[
L_0+\sum_j w_jL(\theta_j)=0,
\]

then it is just another row appended to the moment system:

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
M\\
L
\end{pmatrix}w
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c\\
-z_0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

For generic nodes, increasing \(N\) by \(1\) or \(2\) preserves full row rank. Positivity is again restored by adding a large positive null-quadrature vector, assuming that vector also lies in the enlarged homogeneous nullspace; if not, add two surplus nodes and solve inside the positive cone directly.

If \(Z_\alpha\) is nonlinear in \(m_0,\lambda,\theta,w\), then the same conclusion holds generically by the implicit function theorem, provided the derivative in at least one free parameter is nonzero.

So \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) is not by itself a killer constraint. It is finite-codimension.

The adversary survives.

---

# 7. Where the adversary first genuinely fails

The first non-finite-codimension constraint is full actual-zeta compatibility:

\[
q''_{\can}
=
B_2
+
P_2
+
D_2
+
E_2,
\]

where

\[
P_2(m)=\Re\sum_{n\le X}a_ne^{-im\log n}
\]

has fixed actual coefficients, \(D_2\) is the genuine functional-equation dual, and \(E_2\) has a certified error budget.

A source-flat packet would force

\[
\partial_m^r q''_{\can}(m_0)\approx0,
\qquad 0\le r\le R.
\]

On the AFE side this becomes

\[
\partial_m^r
\left[
B_2+P_2+D_2
\right](m_0)
\approx0,
\qquad 0\le r\le R.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\Re\sum_{n\le X}a_n(-i\log n)^r e^{-im_0\log n}
\approx
-\partial_m^r(B_2+D_2)(m_0).
\]

This is a fixed-coefficient recurrence / small-ball problem. The adversarial packet has no free weights left here. The coefficients \(a_n\), the dual term, and the zero packet must all be mutually compatible.

That is the first place I cannot currently construct a surviving counterexample.

So the sharp diagnosis is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 1 cannot be proven from local zero geometry or finite constraints.}
}
\]

But also:

\[
\boxed{
\text{I do not yet have a source-flat model compatible with the full fixed actual-zeta AFE.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Most dangerous relaxed counterexample mechanism

The strongest adversarial mechanism is:

\[
\text{positive same-scale packet}
+
\text{finite moment correction}
+
\text{smooth background cancellation}.
\]

Explicitly, choose \(N\gg R\), generic symmetric nodes \(\theta_j\), and solve

\[
\sum_jw_j\sin((r+3)\theta_j)
=
c_r,
\qquad 0\le r\le R,
\]

where \(c_r\) cancels \(B_2+D_2+E_2\) and any finite \(Z_\alpha\)-derived condition. Then

\[
F^{(r)}(0)=0,
\qquad 0\le r\le R.
\]

For \(|x|\le X<1\),

\[
|F(x)|
\lesssim
\frac{\sup_{|z|\le \rho}|F^{(R+1)}(z)|}{(R+1)!}|x|^{R+1}.
\]

Thus

\[
\int_X|F(x)|^2dx
\lesssim
X^{2R+3}\cdot \operatorname{Comp}(F,R)^2.
\]

This makes Gate 1 fail unless actual arithmetic prevents the finite moment design.

The mechanism is robust because it uses the positivity of Poisson packets rather than signed cancellation.

---

# 9. Constraint ladder result table

| Model | Constraint added | Source-flat packet survives? | Reason |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| M0 | Positive same-scale quadrature | Yes | Exact sine quadrature kills jets |
| M1 | Quartet symmetry | Yes | Deterministic nodes already symmetric |
| M2 | Finite zero-counting | Yes | Needs only \(O(R)\) source points |
| M3 | Smooth \(B_2\) | Yes | Finite moment RHS shift |
| M4 | Flexible \(D_2,E_2\) | Yes | Still finite analytic moment matching |
| M5 | \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) | Yes, generically | Extra finite-codimension constraint |
| M6 | Fixed actual \(P_2+D_2\) AFE | Unknown / not broken | Becomes fixed-coefficient nonrecurrence |
| M7 | Zero-side/AFE agreement with error budget | Unknown / not broken | Infinite arithmetic compatibility |

---

# 10. Concrete adversarial numerical test to run next

The right break-test is not another pure zero-kernel proxy. It is a constrained optimization ladder.

For fixed \(m_0,\lambda,R,X\), optimize over symmetric packet variables \((\theta_j,w_j)\), \(w_j>0\), with

\[
F_{\mathrm{zero}}(x)
=
Q''(x)
+
\lambda^3B_2(m_0+\lambda x).
\]

Then progressively add terms.

## Stage A: local + \(B_2\)

Minimize

\[
\mathcal J_A
=
\int_X
\left|
Q''(x)+B_{2,\lambda}(x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

Expected: collapse.

## Stage B: add smooth dual surrogate

\[
\mathcal J_B
=
\int_X
\left|
Q''(x)+B_{2,\lambda}(x)+D_{2,\lambda}^{\mathrm{sur}}(x)
\right|^2dx.
\]

Expected: collapse unless \(D_2^{\mathrm{sur}}\) has actual fixed-coefficient rigidity.

## Stage C: add center condition

\[
\mathcal J_C
=
\mathcal J_B
+
\mu |Z_\alpha(m_0,\lambda,\theta,w)|^2.
\]

Expected: collapse if \(N\) has enough slack.

## Stage D: add actual AFE residual

Define

\[
R_{\mathrm{AFE}}^{(r)}(m_0)
=
\partial_m^r
\left[
q''_{\mathrm{zero}}-
(B_2+P_2+D_2)
\right](m_0).
\]

Minimize

\[
\mathcal J_D
=
\mathcal J_C
+
\nu\sum_{r=0}^{R_{\mathrm{AFE}}}
|R_{\mathrm{AFE}}^{(r)}(m_0)|^2.
\]

This is the first serious test.

Expected adversarial outcome: collapse probably stops here.

If collapse continues through Stage D with certified \(E_2\), pause the proof sprint.

---

# 11. What this says about Gate 1 proof strategy

Agent 5 conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any Gate 1 proof using only local packet geometry is dead.}
}
\]

More specifically, the following are insufficient:

\[
\text{positivity},\quad
\text{quartet symmetry},\quad
\text{finite zero-counting},\quad
\text{smooth background control},\quad
\text{finite center constraints}.
\]

The proof must use one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{fixed actual-prime nonrecurrence}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-prime anti-design via explicit formula}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a genuinely coercive global trace / Weil-positive functional.}
}
\]

The current adversarial status is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Continue the Gate 1 proof sprint, but reject any proof that does not use actual zeta arithmetic.}
}
\]

No stop rule triggered yet.

## Agent 5 / E — Linear explicit-formula statistic for Gate 1

I am following the Agent 5 instruction from the coordinator message: search for a **linear** packet-adapted explicit-formula statistic that would imply Gate 1 by Cauchy–Schwarz, and determine whether it gives a new lever or collapses to finite-jet anti-design. 

## Verdict

I do **not** find a genuinely new linear statistic that is both:

\[
\text{localized to } I_\rho
\]

and

\[
\text{insensitive to same-scale source-flattening quadrature.}
\]

Every local linear probe I can construct falls into one of three categories:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite-jet / packet-mode probe}
}
\]

which collapses to finite-jet anti-design;

\[
\boxed{
\text{prime-block matched probe}
}
\]

which collapses to fixed-coefficient prime nonrecurrence;

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{mass-recovery probe}
}
\]

which can beat quadrature but is necessarily nonlocal, so it does not imply the local Gate 1 estimate by Cauchy–Schwarz on \(I_\rho\).

So the linear-statistic route is useful as a **coordinate reformulation**, but I do not see a standalone new proof mechanism.

---

# 1. Normalized setup

Let

\[
I_\rho=m_0+\lambda X,
\qquad
m=m_0+\lambda x,
\]

and

\[
F_\lambda(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m_0+\lambda x).
\]

Use a normalized test

\[
\phi\in C_c^\infty(X),
\qquad
\|\phi\|_{L^2(X)}\le 1,
\]

and define the physical test

\[
\psi_{m_0,\lambda}(m)
=
\lambda^2\phi\!\left(\frac{m-m_0}{\lambda}\right).
\]

Then

\[
\int_{I_\rho}\psi_{m_0,\lambda}(m)q''_{\can}(m)\,dm
=
\int_X\phi(x)F_\lambda(x)\,dx.
\]

Therefore, if one could prove

\[
\left|
\int_X\phi(x)F_\lambda(x)\,dx
\right|
\ge Q^{-B}\lambda^3 |X|^{1/2},
\]

then Cauchy–Schwarz gives

\[
\int_X |F_\lambda(x)|^2\,dx
\ge
Q^{-2B}\lambda^6 |X|.
\]

That is the normalized Gate 1 estimate, up to exponent loss.

So the route is formally valid.

The hard part is finding \(\phi\) for which the lower bound is provable for the actual canonical object.

---

# 2. Zero-side expression

For a packet modeled by normalized kernels

\[
K_\theta(x)=
\frac{\sin\theta}{(x-\cos\theta)^2+\sin^2\theta},
\]

we have locally

\[
F_\lambda(x)
\approx
\sum_j w_j K_{\theta_j}''(x)
+
F_{\mathrm{tail}}(x)
+
F_{B_2}(x).
\]

The linear statistic is

\[
S_\phi
=
\int_X\phi(x)F_\lambda(x)\,dx.
\]

On the packet part,

\[
S_{\phi,\mathrm{pkt}}
=
\sum_jw_jL_\phi(\theta_j),
\]

where

\[
L_\phi(\theta)
=
\int_X\phi(x)K_\theta''(x)\,dx.
\]

If \(\phi\) is smooth and compactly supported in \(X\), then integration by parts gives

\[
L_\phi(\theta)
=
\int_X\phi''(x)K_\theta(x)\,dx.
\]

Thus each local linear statistic is just another packet moment.

For analytic or Gevrey \(\phi\), \(L_\phi(\theta)\) has a rapidly convergent sine expansion:

\[
L_\phi(\theta)
=
\sum_{k\ge 1} c_k(\phi)\sin(k\theta).
\]

The same-scale quadrature adversary kills finitely many packet moments:

\[
\sum_jw_j\sin(k\theta_j)=0,
\qquad
3\le k\le R+3.
\]

Therefore, any \(\phi\) whose \(L_\phi\)-mass is concentrated in those modes is killed. If \(\phi\) has high-mode leakage, the adversary can push \(R\) high enough that the residual is exponentially small in \(R\), hence polynomially small in \(Q\) for \(R\asymp C\log Q\).

So ordinary local packet probes are not quadrature-insensitive.

---

# 3. Prime/AFE expression

Write the canonical AFE-side decomposition as

\[
q''_{\can}(m)=B_2(m)+P_2(m)+D_2(m)+E_2(m),
\]

with

\[
P_2(m)=
\Re\sum_{n\le N}a_n e^{-im\log n}.
\]

Then

\[
S_\phi
=
S_{B_2,\phi}
+
S_{P_2,\phi}
+
S_{D_2,\phi}
+
S_{E_2,\phi}.
\]

For the main term,

\[
S_{P_2,\phi}
=
\lambda^3
\Re\sum_{n\le N}
a_n
e^{-im_0\log n}
\widehat\phi(\lambda\log n).
\]

Similarly, the dual term has the schematic form

\[
S_{D_2,\phi}
=
\lambda^3
\Re\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}
\widetilde a_n
e^{+im_0\log n}
\widehat\phi(-\lambda\log n)
+
\text{slow-amplitude derivative corrections}.
\]

The background term is

\[
S_{B_2,\phi}
=
\lambda^3
\int_X
\phi(x)B_2(m_0+\lambda x)\,dx.
\]

The error term must satisfy

\[
|S_{E_2,\phi}|
\le
\frac12 Q^{-B}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2}
\]

for the statistic to be useful.

Thus any prime-side proof of a lower bound for \(S_\phi\) becomes a fixed-coefficient inequality:

\[
\left|
\lambda^3
\Re\sum_{n}
a_n e^{-im_0\log n}\widehat\phi(\lambda\log n)
+
\lambda^3
\Re\sum_n
\widetilde a_n e^{+im_0\log n}\widehat\phi(-\lambda\log n)
+
S_{B_2,\phi}
\right|
\ge
Q^{-B}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2}.
\]

This is not easier than prime nonrecurrence. It is a one-dimensional projection of the same obstruction.

---

# 4. Candidate probes and their failure modes

## Candidate A: finite-jet dual probes

Take

\[
\phi_r(x)=(-1)^r \partial_x^r\rho(x)
\]

for a compact bump \(\rho\). Then

\[
S_{\phi_r}
=
\int_X \phi_r(x)F_\lambda(x)\,dx
=
\int_X \rho(x)\partial_x^rF_\lambda(x)\,dx.
\]

This probes finite jets or averaged finite derivatives. On the packet side it corresponds to moments

\[
\sum_jw_j\sin(k\theta_j)
\]

for \(k\) in a finite range.

Failure mode:

\[
\boxed{
\text{same-scale quadrature kills it.}
}
\]

This is exactly finite-jet anti-design, not a new lever.

---

## Candidate B: prime-block matched filter

Choose \(\phi\) so that \(\widehat\phi(\lambda \log n)\) concentrates on one prime or one short prime block.

Then

\[
S_{P_2,\phi}
\approx
\lambda^3
\Re
\sum_{n\in \mathcal P}
a_n e^{-im_0\log n}.
\]

This could work if one proves that at least one such block has polynomial-size projection after adding \(B_2+D_2\).

Failure mode:

\[
\boxed{
\text{this is fixed-coefficient prime recurrence in disguise.}
}
\]

The phase \(m_0\mapsto e^{-im_0\log n}\) can make any one block small. To force a lower bound, one needs a large-sieve, transversality, Wronskian, or nonrecurrence theorem across many blocks. That is Agent 1/2/4 territory, not an independent linear statistic.

---

## Candidate C: packet mass-recovery probe

A tempting nonlocal probe is

\[
\phi(x)=x^2.
\]

Over the full line,

\[
\int_{\mathbb R} x^2 K_\theta''(x)\,dx
=
2\int_{\mathbb R}K_\theta(x)\,dx
=
2\pi,
\]

so

\[
\int_{\mathbb R}x^2Q''(x)\,dx
=
2\pi\sum_jw_j.
\]

This is excellent: it detects positive packet mass and is insensitive to same-scale quadrature.

But it is nonlocal and not \(L^2\)-localized on \(X\). If we cut it off to \(X\),

\[
\phi(x)=x^2\chi_X(x),
\]

boundary terms appear, and same-scale quadrature can again make

\[
\int_X\phi(x)Q''(x)\,dx
\]

small whenever \(Q''\) is small on \(X\).

Failure mode:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the only quadrature-insensitive mass probe is nonlocal.}
}
\]

It can at best prove source mass on a larger region. It does not imply Gate 1 on \(I_\rho\) unless one adds a Remez/analytic-continuation step, which returns to finite-jet anti-design.

---

# 5. Direct adversarial test against any local linear statistic

Assume \(\phi\) is supported in \(X\) and

\[
\|\phi\|_{L^2(X)}\le Q^C.
\]

If the same-scale quadrature construction gives

\[
\|F_\lambda\|_{L^2(X)}
\le Q^{-A}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2},
\]

then automatically

\[
|S_\phi|
=
\left|
\int_X\phi F_\lambda
\right|
\le
Q^{C-A}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2}.
\]

For any proposed polynomial lower bound, choose \(A\) larger.

This is not merely heuristic. The deterministic sine quadrature gives, for \(X<1\),

\[
\sup_{|x|\le X}|Q''(x)|
\le C_R X^{R+1},
\]

so

\[
\|Q''\|_{L^2(X)}
\le C_R X^{R+1}|X|^{1/2}.
\]

Taking

\[
R\asymp C'\log Q
\]

makes every bounded local linear statistic polynomially small.

With smooth background \(G=B_2+D_2+E_2\), finite-jet cancellation gives

\[
F_\lambda(x)=Q''(x)+G(x),
\]

with

\[
F_\lambda^{(r)}(0)=0,
\qquad
0\le r\le R.
\]

Analytic complexity bounds then again yield

\[
\|F_\lambda\|_{L^2(X)}
\le Q^{-A}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2}
\]

for suitable \(R\). Therefore every local \(\phi\) is defeated.

So no local linear statistic can be intrinsically insensitive to source flattening.

---

# 6. Conditional theorem that would be enough

A useful but tautological theorem shape is:

**Conditional linear-probe theorem.**  
Suppose that for every admissible off-line center \(m_0\) and packet scale \(\lambda\), there exists \(\phi_{m_0,\lambda}\in C_c^\infty(X)\) such that

\[
\|\phi_{m_0,\lambda}\|_{L^2(X)}\le Q^C
\]

and

\[
\left|
\int_X\phi_{m_0,\lambda}(x)F_\lambda(x)\,dx
\right|
\ge
Q^{-B}\lambda^3|X|^{1/2}.
\]

Then

\[
\int_X|F_\lambda(x)|^2dx
\ge
Q^{-2B-2C}\lambda^6|X|.
\]

But proving the hypothesis is equivalent to proving that \(F_\lambda\) is not source-flat on \(X\). In other words, this theorem does not bypass Gate 1. It restates it in dual form.

---

# 7. Where the route could still help

The linear-statistic route may still be useful if one changes from a single probe to a structured **bank** of probes:

\[
\{\phi_k\}_{k=3}^{R+3}.
\]

If one can prove

\[
\sum_{k=3}^{R+3}
\left|
\int_X\phi_kF_\lambda
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-B}\lambda^6|X|,
\]

and also has a frame bound

\[
\sum_k
|\langle \phi_k,F\rangle|^2
\le
Q^C\|F\|_{L^2(X)}^2,
\]

then Gate 1 follows.

But this is no longer a single linear statistic. It is exactly the packet spectral leakage / finite-mode anti-design problem.

So the viable refinement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{linear probe bank + large-sieve/nonrecurrence lower bound.}
}
\]

A single \(\psi\) does not appear sufficient.

---

# 8. Final diagnosis

The linear explicit-formula statistic route does **not** currently provide a new proof lever.

Precise classification:

| Probe type | Localized to \(I_\rho\)? | Beats same-scale quadrature? | Gives Gate 1? | Diagnosis |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Finite-jet bump probes | Yes | No | Only if anti-design already known | Collapses to finite-jet anti-design |
| Prime-block matched filter | Yes | Not by itself | Only with prime nonrecurrence | Collapses to fixed-coefficient recurrence |
| Quadratic mass probe \(x^2\) | No | Yes | No local Gate 1 | Nonlocal trace/global route |
| Probe bank/frame | Yes | Maybe | Yes if spectral leakage proved | Agent 4-style theorem |
| Adaptive \(\phi=F/\|F\|\) | Yes | Trivial | Tautological | Not explicit-formula usable |

Final answer:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No Green linear statistic found.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any successful local linear statistic must secretly prove finite-jet anti-design or prime nonrecurrence.}
}
\]

The only genuinely quadrature-insensitive linear idea is the nonlocal mass probe, but that proves mass outside the desired local interval and therefore cannot by itself close Gate 1.

## Assistant (2026-04-26 18:59:28)

## Agent 5 / E — Adversarial Wronskian countermodel

I am following the Agent 5 instruction: try to make the Wronskian route fail by constructing models where

\[
\mathcal W_R(m,\alpha)
\]

is very small while

\[
Z_\alpha(m)\approx0,
\qquad
\Delta^r\partial_m^r q''_{\can}(m)\approx0,
\quad 0\le r\le R.
\]

The coordinator’s prompt identifies the Wronskian route as the current bottleneck. 

## Verdict

The Wronskian route **fails in every relaxed finite-phase model** I can construct. It only survives when one imposes the full actual-zeta arithmetic restriction:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

with fixed coefficients, genuine main/dual coupling, and certified error control.

The core adversarial point is simple:

\[
\boxed{
G_j(m_0,\alpha)=0\ \text{for all rows}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathcal W_R(m_0,\alpha)=0.
}
\]

Indeed, the Wronskian matrix has first column

\[
(G_0,G_1,\ldots,G_{R+1})^T.
\]

So at an exact bad point, that column vanishes.

Thus a Wronskian lower bound is not a structural identity. It is itself a fixed-coefficient nonrecurrence theorem saying the bad locus is never even approximately reached.

The first model level at which I cannot break the Wronskian route is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{full actual AFE / explicit-formula compatibility with fixed coefficients.}
}
\]

Everything weaker admits countermodels or near-countermodels.

---

# 1. Setup

Let

\[
G_0(m,\alpha)=Z_\alpha(m),
\]

and

\[
G_{j+1}(m,\alpha)
=
\Delta^j\partial_m^j q''_{\can}(m),
\qquad 0\le j\le R.
\]

So there are

\[
K=R+2
\]

rows:

\[
G_0,G_1,\ldots,G_{R+1}.
\]

The proposed Wronskian is

\[
\mathcal W_R(m,\alpha)
=
\det
\left(
\partial_m^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_
{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}.
\]

The bad Gate 1 finite-jet locus is

\[
G_j(m_0,\alpha)\approx0,
\qquad 0\le j\le R+1.
\]

At an exact bad point,

\[
G_j(m_0,\alpha)=0
\quad\forall j,
\]

the zeroth derivative column of the Wronskian matrix is zero:

\[
\left(
\partial_m^0 G_j(m_0,\alpha)
\right)_j
=
(G_j(m_0,\alpha))_j
=
0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal W_R(m_0,\alpha)=0.
\]

More quantitatively, if

\[
\max_j |G_j(m_0,\alpha)|\le \varepsilon
\]

and all derivative columns satisfy

\[
\max_{j,\ell\le R+1}
|\partial_m^\ell G_j(m_0,\alpha)|
\le Q^C,
\]

then by multilinearity of the determinant,

\[
|\mathcal W_R(m_0,\alpha)|
\le
C_R\,\varepsilon\, Q^{C(R+1)}.
\]

So a lower bound

\[
|\mathcal W_R(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

would imply

\[
\max_j |G_j(m_0,\alpha)|
\ge
C_R^{-1}Q^{-A-C(R+1)}.
\]

That is the desired anti-design implication.

But adversarially, this also means:

\[
\boxed{
\text{any model that realizes the bad locus automatically kills the Wronskian.}
}
\]

So the Wronskian route is only as strong as the theorem forbidding the bad locus.

---

# 2. Phase-relaxed prime model

Start with a relaxed AFE main-term model:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j(\alpha)
+
\sum_{\nu=1}^{M} A_{j\nu}(\alpha)u_\nu,
\qquad
|u_\nu|=1,
\]

where

\[
u_\nu
\]

represents an independent prime phase

\[
e^{-im\log n_\nu}.
\]

The bad equations are

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le K-1.
\]

This is a system of \(K\) complex equations, or \(2K\) real equations, on the torus

\[
\mathbb T^M
\]

plus the transverse parameter \(\alpha\).

If

\[
M\gg 2K,
\]

then the dimension count strongly favors solutions, unless the coefficient vectors have a special separating hyperplane.

Equivalently, the reachable set is the Minkowski sum of circles

\[
\mathcal R
=
b(\alpha)
+
\sum_{\nu=1}^{M}
A_\nu(\alpha)\mathbb S^1
\subset \mathbb C^K.
\]

If \(0\) lies in the interior of this zonotope-like body, there are phase choices with

\[
G(u,\alpha)=0.
\]

At such a point,

\[
\mathcal W_R=0.
\]

So the phase-relaxed model breaks the Wronskian route.

This model is not exotic. It is exactly what one expects before imposing the one-parameter Kronecker orbit

\[
u_\nu=e^{-im\omega_\nu}
\]

with fixed \(\omega_\nu=\log n_\nu\).

---

# 3. Adding \(Z_\alpha=0\) does not rescue the Wronskian

Including

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0
\]

as \(G_0=0\) adds one equation, but \(\alpha\) also supplies a free transverse parameter.

In the phase-relaxed model, this changes the codimension by roughly

\[
+1 \ \text{or}\ +2
\]

real constraints, depending on whether \(Z_\alpha\) is treated as real or complex, while \(\alpha\) returns one or more real degrees of freedom.

Thus the phase count remains favorable as soon as

\[
M \gg 2(R+2).
\]

The Wronskian determinant still vanishes at any exact solution because \(Z_\alpha\) is included in the zeroth column.

So \(Z_\alpha\) may add symbolic rank, but symbolic rank is not enough. One needs quantitative nonrecurrence.

---

# 4. Fixed coefficients but independent phases

Now freeze the actual coefficients \(A_{j\nu}\), but keep the phases independent.

Then the equations remain

\[
b_j+\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu=0.
\]

For a sufficiently large active block, this is still solvable or nearly solvable under generic convexity assumptions.

A useful adversarial criterion is:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
-b,
\sum_{\nu=1}^M A_\nu \mathbb S^1
\right)
\le \varepsilon.
\]

If this distance is \(\varepsilon\), then

\[
\max_j |G_j|\le\varepsilon
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathcal W_R|
\le
C_R\varepsilon Q^{C(R+1)}.
\]

Thus, in the fixed-coefficient but phase-independent model, the Wronskian route fails unless one proves a quantitative separation:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
-b,
\sum_{\nu=1}^M A_\nu \mathbb S^1
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

That is already a nontrivial arithmetic/geometry theorem about the actual coefficient vectors.

I do not see a general reason for such a separation in this relaxed model.

---

# 5. One-parameter Kronecker orbit

Now impose

\[
u_\nu=e^{-im\omega_\nu},
\qquad
\omega_\nu=\log p_\nu.
\]

The phase vector lies on the Kronecker orbit

\[
m\mapsto
(e^{-im\log p_\nu})_{\nu=1}^M
\in \mathbb T^M.
\]

Since the \(\log p_\nu\) are rationally independent over \(\mathbb Q\), the orbit is dense in the \(M\)-torus.

Therefore, any open bad tube in the relaxed phase torus is eventually hit.

If the bad tube radius is

\[
\delta=Q^{-C},
\]

and the real codimension is roughly

\[
2K=2(R+2),
\]

then the volume heuristic gives recurrence time

\[
\tau_{\mathrm{rec}}
\sim
\delta^{-2K}
=
Q^{O(R)}.
\]

So under the same heuristic already identified in the previous sprint, recurrence is not expensive enough by itself to rule out near-bad points.

At such near-bad points,

\[
\max_j|G_j|\lesssim Q^{-C},
\]

and therefore

\[
|\mathcal W_R|
\lesssim
Q^{-C+C'(R+1)}.
\]

Taking \(C\) larger than \(C'(R+1)+A\), the Wronskian is smaller than \(Q^{-A}\).

Thus the one-parameter fixed-frequency model still has an adversarial near-countermechanism unless one proves a stronger determinant nonrecurrence theorem.

---

# 6. Explicit toy Wronskian-killer

A clean toy model shows the mechanism.

Let

\[
f(m)=
\left(e^{-i\omega(m-m_0)}-1\right)^{R+1}.
\]

Then

\[
f^{(r)}(m_0)=0,
\qquad
0\le r\le R.
\]

If we model

\[
q''_{\mathrm{toy}}=f
\]

and choose

\[
Z_{\alpha,\mathrm{toy}}(m)
=
e^{-i\eta(m-m_0)}-1,
\]

then

\[
Z_{\alpha,\mathrm{toy}}(m_0)=0
\]

and all source jets vanish through order \(R\).

The associated Wronskian has a zero first column at \(m_0\), hence

\[
\mathcal W_R(m_0,\alpha)=0.
\]

Expanded out, \(f\) is an exponential polynomial:

\[
f(m)
=
\sum_{a=0}^{R+1}
(-1)^{R+1-a}
\binom{R+1}{a}
e^{-ia\omega(m-m_0)}.
\]

This uses tunable binomial coefficients and arithmetic-progression frequencies, so it is not an actual-zeta model. But it proves the Wronskian mechanism is not formally protected by differentiation or Vandermonde structure. Exponential sums can have high-order flat points.

The actual proof must forbid this specific phenomenon for the actual prime-log coefficient system.

---

# 7. Adding main/dual structure

Now include the folded AFE structure:

\[
q''_{\can}
=
B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

A schematic row becomes

\[
G_j(m,\alpha)
=
b_j(m,\alpha)
+
\sum_{n\le N} a_{j,n}e^{-im\log n}
+
\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}
\widetilde a_{j,n}e^{+im\log n}
+
e_j(m).
\]

In phase variables,

\[
u_p=e^{-im\log p},
\]

both main and dual terms are functions of the same prime phases:

\[
n^{-im}
=
\prod_{p^a\parallel n}u_p^a,
\qquad
n^{+im}
=
\prod_{p^a\parallel n}u_p^{-a}.
\]

So main/dual structure does not simply double the number of independent variables. It creates a Laurent polynomial system on the same prime torus.

The adversarial question becomes:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)=0
\quad
\text{for Laurent polynomials }G_j.
\]

For \(M\) active primes and \(K=R+2\) equations, this system is still underdetermined in the phase-relaxed torus when

\[
M\gg 2K.
\]

Thus main/dual structure alone does not kill the countermodel.

It could kill the countermodel only if the actual Laurent coefficients have a special positivity, monotonicity, or algebraic obstruction. I do not see such an obstruction from the formal main/dual shape alone.

So the adversary survives “main/dual structure” as a formal constraint.

---

# 8. Adding \(B_2,D_2,E_2\)

## \(B_2\)

The background term \(B_2\) contributes smooth inhomogeneous rows:

\[
b_j(m,\alpha).
\]

In the finite system, this only shifts the target:

\[
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu
=
-b_j.
\]

It does not remove degrees of freedom.

So \(B_2\) does not rescue the Wronskian route.

## \(D_2\)

If \(D_2\) is treated as a flexible analytic term or a formal opposite-frequency block, it still only changes the Laurent polynomial system. It does not prevent solutions by dimension count.

Only the **actual** dual coefficients, tied to the same functional equation and gamma factors as \(P_2\), may help.

So flexible \(D_2\) does not rescue the route.

## \(E_2\)

If

\[
|E_2^{(r)}|\le \varepsilon_E
\]

for all required derivative orders, then near-bad source jets only need be achieved to tolerance \(\varepsilon_E\). This makes the adversarial construction easier, not harder.

To rescue the Wronskian route, the determinant lower bound must exceed the Wronskian error produced by \(E_2\):

\[
|\mathcal W_{\mathrm{actual}}-\mathcal W_{\mathrm{truncated}}|
\ll
Q^{-A-1}.
\]

This is a canonical audit issue, not an adversarial obstruction.

---

# 9. Cauchy–Binet determinant expansion is itself vulnerable

The Wronskian main term expands as

\[
\mathcal W_R(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_S C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S},
\]

where \(S\) ranges over subsets of active frequencies and

\[
N_S=\prod_{\nu\in S}n_\nu.
\]

The Vandermonde factors inside \(C_S\) may show

\[
\mathcal W_R\not\equiv0.
\]

But nonzero is far weaker than a pointwise lower bound.

An exponential sum

\[
\sum_S C_S e^{-im\log N_S}
\]

can have small values by phase cancellation. Without a dominant coefficient or positivity, there is no general lower bound

\[
|\mathcal W_R(m,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The determinant route therefore faces the same adversary one level higher:

\[
\boxed{
\text{subset-product phase recurrence.}
}
\]

Even if \(Z_\alpha\) adds symbolic rank, the Wronskian determinant can be small unless the subset-product exponential sum has a quantitative zero-avoidance theorem at admissible centers.

---

# 10. Model ladder

| Model level | Constraint imposed | Can I make bad jets and small Wronskian? | Diagnosis |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| M0 | Arbitrary exponential polynomial | Yes | High-order flat exponential polynomial |
| M1 | Independent prime phases, flexible coefficients | Yes | Solve finite linear system |
| M2 | Independent prime phases, fixed coefficients | Yes/generic | Torus zonotope likely contains target |
| M3 | One-parameter Kronecker orbit | Near-yes by recurrence | Bad tube hit in time \(Q^{O(R)}\) heuristically |
| M4 | Add \(Z_\alpha=0\) | Yes/generic | Extra finite-codimension constraint |
| M5 | Add smooth \(B_2\) | Yes | Inhomogeneous RHS shift |
| M6 | Add formal main/dual Laurent structure | Yes/generic | Still underdetermined on large prime torus |
| M7 | Add actual fixed \(P_2+D_2\), gamma factors, \(E_2\) | Unknown | First level I cannot break |
| M8 | Add proven subset-product determinant lower bound | No | This is essentially the needed theorem |

---

# 11. First level where the Wronskian route survives

The Wronskian route survives only after imposing an actual-zeta theorem of the following kind:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\det
\left(
\partial_m^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for the genuine canonical rows

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\qquad
G_{j+1}=\Delta^j\partial_m^j q''_{\can},
\]

at all admissible centers.

Equivalently, after Cauchy–Binet expansion, one needs:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\sum_S C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S}
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is not supplied by:

\[
\text{Vandermonde nonvanishing},
\]

\[
\text{formal rank},
\]

\[
\text{main/dual decomposition},
\]

or

\[
\text{ordinary Kronecker density}.
\]

It requires a genuine fixed-coefficient determinant nonrecurrence theorem.

---

# 12. Persistent countermechanism

The persistent countermechanism is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite-dimensional phase recurrence into the bad jet tube.}
}
\]

At the level of \(G\), it makes

\[
G(m,\alpha)\approx0.
\]

At the level of \(\mathcal W_R\), it automatically makes

\[
\mathcal W_R(m,\alpha)\approx0
\]

because the zeroth column is small.

At the determinant-expansion level, it appears as subset-product cancellation:

\[
\sum_S C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S}\approx0.
\]

Thus the Wronskian route does not avoid nonrecurrence. It amplifies and repackages it.

---

# 13. Final adversarial diagnosis

The Wronskian route is valid only as a conditional implication:

\[
|\mathcal W_R|\ge Q^{-A}
+
\text{derivative upper bounds}
\Longrightarrow
\text{finite-jet anti-design}.
\]

But I can make the route fail in every model where the bad finite-jet locus is allowed by finite-dimensional phase freedom.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Wronskian rank is not enough.}
}
\]

The missing theorem must be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-zeta subset-product determinant nonrecurrence at admissible }Z_\alpha\text{ centers.}
}
\]

No stop-rule-level counterexample for actual zeta has been found. But the Wronskian route is **not** protected against the same adversary unless that arithmetic determinant lower bound is proved.

## Agent 5 / E — Adversarial canonical Wronskian stress test

I am following the Agent 5 instruction: stress-test the **normalized canonical Wronskian** route through the ladder

\[
\text{phase-relaxed}
\to
\text{fixed coefficients}
\to
\text{one-parameter prime-log orbit}
\to
+B_2
\to
+D_2
\to
+Z_\alpha
\to
+E_2.
\]

## Verdict

The normalized Wronskian route still fails in every relaxed or semi-realistic model I can construct. The first constraint I cannot break is again the full actual-zeta compatibility package:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]

with fixed actual coefficients, genuine main/dual coupling, \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), and a certified \(E_2\) budget.

So the stop-rule verdict is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Continue the Wronskian route, but only as a hard arithmetic nonrecurrence theorem.}
}
\]

Do **not** treat normalized Wronskian rank or \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol transversality as enough. They are necessary, not sufficient.

---

# 1. Normalized Wronskian setup

Use

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathcal D=\Delta\partial_m.
\]

Define

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\qquad
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Then

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)
=
\det
\left(
\mathcal D^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}.
\]

The bad finite-jet locus is

\[
G_0(m,\alpha)=0,
\qquad
G_{j+1}(m,\alpha)=0,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0,
\qquad
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m)=0,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

At an exact bad point, the zeroth Wronskian column is zero:

\[
\left(G_j(m,\alpha)\right)_{j=0}^{R+1}=0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)=0.
\]

Quantitatively, if

\[
\max_j |G_j(m,\alpha)|\le \varepsilon
\]

and normalized derivative columns satisfy

\[
|\mathcal D^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)|\le Q^C,
\qquad
1\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

then by determinant multilinearity,

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)|
\le
C_R\,\varepsilon\, Q^{C(R+1)}.
\]

Thus any near-bad point automatically makes the normalized Wronskian small.

This remains true after normalization. Normalization controls derivative growth; it does not protect the determinant from a small first column.

---

# 2. Ladder stress test

## Level 0: phase-relaxed model

Let the active prime phases be independent variables

\[
u_\nu\in \mathbb T,
\qquad
u_\nu=e^{-im\log n_\nu}
\]

but temporarily drop the one-parameter relation.

Model the rows as

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j(\alpha)
+
\sum_{\nu=1}^{M} A_{j\nu}(\alpha)u_\nu,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

The bad equations are

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

There are \(R+2\) complex rows, or at most \(2R+4\) real equations, plus the free \(\alpha\)-parameter. With

\[
M\gg R,
\]

the phase-relaxed torus has far more variables than constraints. Generically the target point lies in the reachable zonotope-like body

\[
b(\alpha)+\sum_{\nu=1}^M A_\nu(\alpha)\mathbb S^1.
\]

So the bad locus is generically solvable.

At such a solution,

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R=0.
\]

**Result:** Wronskian route fails.

---

## Level 1: fixed coefficients, independent phases

Now freeze the coefficients \(A_{j\nu}\) to actual or actual-like values, but keep independent phases.

The problem becomes

\[
-b(\alpha)
\in
\sum_{\nu=1}^{M} A_\nu(\alpha)\mathbb S^1.
\]

This is still a high-dimensional vector-balancing problem. Unless there is a special separation theorem for the actual coefficient vectors, one expects approximate solutions with error

\[
\varepsilon\ll Q^{-A}
\]

when \(M\) is sufficiently larger than \(R\).

If such an approximate solution exists, then

\[
\max_j |G_j|\le \varepsilon
\]

and hence

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R|
\le
C_R\varepsilon Q^{C(R+1)}.
\]

Taking \(\varepsilon\) small enough breaks any proposed polynomial Wronskian lower bound.

**Result:** fixed coefficients alone do not rescue the Wronskian route in the independent-phase model.

---

## Level 2: one-parameter prime-log orbit

Now impose

\[
u_\nu=e^{-im\omega_\nu},
\qquad
\omega_\nu=\log n_\nu.
\]

For prime-supported phases, the \(\log p\) are rationally independent, so the orbit

\[
m\mapsto(e^{-im\log p_\nu})_{\nu=1}^{M}
\]

is dense in the \(M\)-torus.

If the bad tube has radius

\[
\delta=Q^{-C},
\]

and effective real codimension

\[
K_{\rm real}\sim 2(R+2)-\dim(\alpha),
\]

then the crude recurrence heuristic gives hitting time

\[
\tau_{\rm rec}
\sim
\delta^{-K_{\rm real}}
=
Q^{O(R)}.
\]

For

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is

\[
\exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)\right).
\]

This is not obviously too large relative to the global height available in the RH contradiction setup.

So plain one-parameter recurrence still plausibly hits near-bad tubes.

**Result:** one-parameter prime-log structure does not by itself kill the bad locus.

---

## Level 3: add \(B_2\)

Adding \(B_2\) changes

\[
G_j
\]

by a smooth inhomogeneous term:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b^{(B)}_j(m,\alpha)
+
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu.
\]

This only shifts the target vector:

\[
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu
=
-b^{(B)}_j.
\]

It does not increase codimension. It does not force positivity. It does not prevent phase recurrence.

**Result:** \(B_2\) does not kill the bad locus.

---

## Level 4: add formal \(D_2\)

If \(D_2\) is treated as a formal dual block,

\[
D_2(m)
\sim
\sum_{\nu}
\widetilde A_{\nu}e^{+im\log n_\nu},
\]

then in prime phase variables it contributes Laurent monomials:

\[
u_\nu^{-1},\quad u_\nu^{-2},\quad \prod_p u_p^{-a_p}.
\]

The rows become Laurent polynomials:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j
+
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu
+
\sum_\nu \widetilde A_{j\nu}u_\nu^{-1}
+
\cdots.
\]

But this remains a finite system of \(R+2\) equations on a high-dimensional torus. Formal main/dual structure does not remove enough degrees of freedom.

**Result:** formal \(D_2\) does not kill the bad locus.

Only the exact actual dual coefficients, tied to the true gamma phase and smoothing convention, might matter.

---

## Level 5: add \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank

Assume Agent 1’s favorable conclusion: \(Z_\alpha\) genuinely adds symbol rank. That is,

\[
\frac{z_n(\alpha)}{c_n}
\]

is not well approximated by a degree-\(R\) polynomial in \(\log n\) on a thick active block.

This prevents the \(Z_\alpha\) row from being algebraically redundant with the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet rows.

But it does **not** prevent simultaneous smallness.

Finite-dimensional transversality says the rows are independent. It does not say the one-parameter orbit avoids their common small tube.

In the phase-relaxed model, independent rank merely increases the codimension from roughly \(2R+2\) to \(2R+4\). With enough active phases, the bad tube remains hittable.

At a near-hit,

\[
G_j\approx0
\quad\forall j,
\]

so the Wronskian is still small.

**Result:** \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank is necessary but not sufficient.

---

## Level 6: add \(E_2\) budget

The error term imposes a tolerance issue.

Suppose the truncated canonical model is

\[
q''_{\mathrm{trunc}}
=
B_2+P_2+D_2
\]

and

\[
q''_{\can}
=
q''_{\mathrm{trunc}}+E_2.
\]

If

\[
|\mathcal D^k E_2|\le \eta
\quad
0\le k\le 2R+1,
\]

then an adversarial near-bad point for the truncated system only needs accuracy

\[
\varepsilon\lesssim \eta.
\]

The \(E_2\) budget does not kill the adversary unless the actual finite model is rigid at a scale larger than \(\eta\).

**Result:** \(E_2\) control is needed for transfer, but it is not itself an anti-recurrence mechanism.

---

# 3. Realistic surrogate with \(Z_\alpha\) rank but small Wronskian

Here is the strongest surrogate countermodel.

Let \(u_p=e^{-im\log p}\) for \(p\le P\), but initially allow independent \(u_p\in\mathbb T\).

Define

\[
q''_{\mathrm{sur}}(u)
=
B_2
+
\sum_{n\le N}a_n u^n
+
\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}\widetilde a_n u^{-n},
\]

schematically as a Laurent polynomial in prime phases.

Define

\[
Z_{\alpha,\mathrm{sur}}(u)
=
\sum_{n\le N}z_n(\alpha)u^n
+
\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}\widetilde z_n(\alpha)u^{-n}.
\]

Choose the symbols so that

\[
\frac{z_n(\alpha)}{c_n}
\notin
\mathcal P_{\le R}(\log n)
\]

on the active block. Thus \(Z_\alpha\) genuinely adds rank.

Now solve the finite system

\[
Z_{\alpha,\mathrm{sur}}(u)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal D^j q''_{\mathrm{sur}}(u)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

Because the number of active prime phases is much larger than the number of constraints, a solution or near-solution is expected in the independent-phase torus.

At that solution,

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(u,\alpha)=0.
\]

Now restrict to the one-parameter orbit

\[
u_p=e^{-im\log p}.
\]

By Kronecker recurrence, the orbit returns near the independent-phase solution at scale \(\delta\) with time roughly

\[
\delta^{-M}
\]

for full \(M\)-torus accuracy, or more optimistically

\[
\delta^{-K_{\rm real}}
\]

for hitting the bad tube directly.

Either way, for finite \(R\) and polynomial \(\delta^{-1}=Q^C\), no immediate contradiction appears.

This surrogate has:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha\text{ rank} + \text{main/dual Laurent structure} + \text{small Wronskian}.
}
\]

It lacks only exact actual-zeta coefficients and certified AFE agreement.

So this is a serious warning: **symbol rank alone cannot be the claimed breakthrough.**

---

# 4. Subset-product determinant cancellation remains the same adversary

The normalized Wronskian expands as

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_S \widetilde C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S}.
\]

Even if every coefficient \(\widetilde C_S\) is well-scaled and nonzero, this is still a fixed-coefficient exponential sum.

The adversarial cancellation mechanism is:

\[
\sum_S \widetilde C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S}\approx0.
\]

The subset frequencies are

\[
\log N_S=\sum_{n\in S}\log n.
\]

Unless there is coefficient dominance, positivity, sign rigidity, or an admissible-center exclusion principle, pointwise lower bounds are not available.

Thus the determinant lower bound

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

is itself the hard arithmetic theorem.

---

# 5. First constraint that actually kills the bad locus

The ladder result is:

| Level | Constraint | Bad locus survives? | Wronskian small? |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | Phase-relaxed rows | Yes | Yes |
| 1 | Fixed coefficients, independent phases | Yes / generically | Yes |
| 2 | One-parameter prime-log orbit | Plausibly recurrent | Yes near hits |
| 3 | Add \(B_2\) | Yes | Yes |
| 4 | Add formal \(D_2\) | Yes | Yes |
| 5 | Add \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank | Yes in relaxed model | Yes |
| 6 | Add \(E_2\) tolerance | Yes unless actual rigidity exceeds \(E_2\) | Yes |
| 7 | Full actual AFE / explicit-formula compatibility | Unknown | First unbroken level |

So the first constraint that might actually kill the bad locus is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{full actual-zeta fixed-coefficient compatibility.}
}
\]

More specifically, one needs a theorem saying the actual one-parameter orbit cannot enter the common small tube:

\[
\left\{
Z_\alpha\approx0,
\quad
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}\approx0,\ 0\le j\le R
\right\}.
\]

Equivalently, one needs:

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for admissible centers, but this must be proved using actual arithmetic, not formal rank.

---

# 6. Stop-rule verdict

No stop-rule counterexample for actual zeta has been found.

But the stress test says the route needs stronger arithmetic input than currently available:

\[
\boxed{
\text{continue Wronskian route, but require a genuine determinant nonrecurrence theorem.}
}
\]

Do not pivot yet, because I cannot produce an actual-zeta source-flat family.

Do not declare success either, because I can produce realistic surrogates with:

\[
Z_\alpha\text{ rank},
\qquad
\text{main/dual Laurent structure},
\qquad
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R\approx0.
\]

The next proof obligation is therefore exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual normalized subset-product determinant nonrecurrence at admissible }Z_\alpha\text{ centers.}
}
\]

Without that theorem, the Wronskian route remains Yellow.

## Agent 5 / E — Adversarial canonical Wronskian stress test

Instruction applied: continue adversarially against the normalized canonical Wronskian route, using the ladder from phase-relaxed models up to full actual AFE compatibility. 

## Bottom line

\[
\boxed{
\text{The normalized Wronskian route remains Yellow.}
}
\]

I can make the normalized Wronskian small in every relaxed model up to:

\[
\text{fixed coefficients}
\to
\text{one-parameter prime-log orbit}
\to
+B_2
\to
\text{formal }D_2
\to
+Z_\alpha\text{ symbol rank}
\to
+E_2\text{ tolerance}.
\]

The first level I cannot break is the full actual-zeta package:

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
}
\]

with fixed actual coefficients, true AFE main/dual coupling, exact \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), and certified error transfer.

So the route should continue, but only if the next theorem is explicitly arithmetic:

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for the actual normalized canonical Wronskian at admissible centers.

---

# 1. Structural vulnerability of the normalized Wronskian

Let

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathcal D=\Delta\partial_m.
\]

Define

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\qquad
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Then

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)
=
\det
\left(
\mathcal D^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}.
\]

The bad finite-jet locus is:

\[
Z_\alpha(m)=0,
\qquad
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m)=0,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
G_j(m,\alpha)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

At such a point, the zeroth Wronskian column is zero:

\[
\left(G_j(m,\alpha)\right)_{j=0}^{R+1}=0.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)=0.
\]

More generally, if

\[
\max_{0\le j\le R+1}|G_j(m,\alpha)|\le \varepsilon
\]

and normalized derivatives satisfy

\[
|\mathcal D^\ell G_j(m,\alpha)|\le Q^C
\quad
1\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

then determinant multilinearity gives:

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)|
\le
C_R\,\varepsilon\, Q^{C(R+1)}.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{any near-hit of the bad locus automatically makes the Wronskian small.}
}
\]

Normalization removes raw derivative blowup. It does **not** protect the determinant from a small first column.

---

# 2. Ladder analysis

## Level 0 — Phase-relaxed model

Let active prime phases be independent:

\[
u_\nu\in \mathbb T.
\]

Model the rows as

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j(\alpha)+\sum_{\nu=1}^M A_{j\nu}(\alpha)u_\nu,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

The bad equations are:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

There are \(R+2\) complex equations, hence about \(2R+4\) real equations. If

\[
M\gg R,
\]

the phase-relaxed torus has enough variables to solve the system generically, unless the coefficient vectors satisfy a special separation property.

At any exact solution:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R=0.
\]

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Phase-relaxed model breaks the Wronskian route.}
}
\]

---

## Level 1 — Fixed coefficients, independent phases

Freeze the coefficients \(A_{j\nu}\), but keep independent phases.

The bad-locus condition becomes a vector-balancing problem:

\[
-b(\alpha)\in \sum_{\nu=1}^M A_\nu(\alpha)\mathbb S^1.
\]

Approximate solvability requires:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
-b(\alpha),
\sum_{\nu=1}^M A_\nu(\alpha)\mathbb S^1
\right)
\le \varepsilon.
\]

If this holds with

\[
\varepsilon\le Q^{-A-C(R+1)},
\]

then

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R|\le Q^{-A}.
\]

I see no purely formal reason fixed coefficients prevent this. One would need a real separation theorem for the actual coefficient vectors.

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Fixed coefficients alone do not rescue the route in the independent-phase model.}
}
\]

---

## Level 2 — One-parameter prime-log orbit

Now impose the true one-parameter phase relation:

\[
u_p=e^{-im\log p}.
\]

For active primes \(p\le P\), the orbit is:

\[
m\mapsto (e^{-im\log p})_{p\le P}.
\]

Since the \(\log p\) are rationally independent over \(\mathbb Q\), the orbit is dense in the finite prime torus.

If the bad tube has radius

\[
\delta=Q^{-C},
\]

and effective real codimension

\[
K_{\rm eff}\approx 2(R+2)-\dim(\alpha),
\]

then the crude recurrence heuristic gives hitting time:

\[
\tau_{\rm rec}
\sim
\delta^{-K_{\rm eff}}
=
Q^{O(R)}.
\]

For

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is:

\[
\exp\!\left(
O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)
\right).
\]

This is large, but not automatically outside the global height budget of the contradiction framework.

Thus one-parameter recurrence plausibly reaches near-bad tubes unless a stronger actual arithmetic obstruction is proved.

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Prime-log one-parameter structure does not by itself kill the bad locus.}
}
\]

---

## Level 3 — Add \(B_2\)

Adding \(B_2\) changes the rows to:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j^{(B)}(m,\alpha)
+
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu.
\]

This only shifts the finite target vector:

\[
\sum_\nu A_{j\nu}u_\nu
=
-b_j^{(B)}(m,\alpha).
\]

It does not change the codimension in any useful way. It does not add positivity. It does not prevent recurrence.

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{ is not a killer constraint.}
}
\]

---

## Level 4 — Add formal \(D_2\)

A formal dual term adds opposite-frequency or Laurent monomial terms:

\[
D_2(m)
\sim
\sum_n \widetilde a_n e^{+im\log n}.
\]

In prime phases this becomes:

\[
u^a
\quad\text{and}\quad
u^{-a}.
\]

The rows become Laurent polynomial equations:

\[
G_j(u,\alpha)
=
b_j
+
\sum_a A_{j,a}u^a
+
\sum_a \widetilde A_{j,a}u^{-a}.
\]

This still gives finitely many equations on a high-dimensional torus.

Formal main/dual structure therefore does not kill the bad locus. Only the exact actual AFE coupling could.

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Formal }D_2\text{ does not rescue the Wronskian route.}
}
\]

---

## Level 5 — Add \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank

Assume the favorable result:

\[
\frac{z_n(\alpha)}{c_n}
\not\approx
P(\log n),
\qquad
\deg P\le R,
\]

on a thick active block.

This means \(Z_\alpha\) is not symbolically redundant with the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet rows.

That is necessary, but not enough.

It increases rank/codimension. It does not prevent the prime-log orbit from entering the common small tube:

\[
Z_\alpha\approx0,
\qquad
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}\approx0,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

At such a near-hit, the Wronskian is automatically small because its first column is small.

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha\text{ rank helps algebraically but does not prove nonrecurrence.}
}
\]

---

## Level 6 — Add \(E_2\) budget

Suppose the truncated model satisfies:

\[
q''_{\can}
=
q''_{\trunc}+E_2
\]

with

\[
|\mathcal D^kE_2|\le \eta,
\qquad
0\le k\le 2R+1.
\]

Then an adversarial bad point for the truncated system only needs to satisfy:

\[
|G_j^{\trunc}|\lesssim \eta.
\]

The error budget does not kill the adversary; it sets the tolerance required for a valid countermodel.

For the Wronskian route to work, the arithmetic determinant lower bound must dominate all determinant-level \(E_2\)-errors:

\[
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{\actual}
-
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{\trunc}|
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A}.
\]

Verdict:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is a transfer constraint, not an anti-recurrence mechanism.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Realistic surrogate with \(Z_\alpha\) rank and small Wronskian

A strong surrogate countermodel is:

\[
q''_{\sur}(u)
=
B_2
+
\sum_{n\le N}a_n u^n
+
\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}\widetilde a_n u^{-n},
\]

\[
Z_{\alpha,\sur}(u)
=
\sum_{n\le N}z_n(\alpha)u^n
+
\sum_{n\le \widetilde N}\widetilde z_n(\alpha)u^{-n}.
\]

Choose the symbols so that \(Z_\alpha\) adds genuine rank:

\[
\frac{z_n(\alpha)}{c_n}
\notin
\mathcal P_{\le R}(\log n)
\]

on a thick block.

Now solve the finite system:

\[
Z_{\alpha,\sur}(u)=0,
\]

\[
\mathcal D^j q''_{\sur}(u)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

Because the number of active phase variables exceeds the number of constraints, the phase-relaxed system is generically solvable.

At such a solution:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(u,\alpha)=0.
\]

Then restrict to a one-parameter orbit:

\[
u_p=e^{-im\log p}.
\]

Kronecker recurrence gives near-hits unless actual arithmetic creates an exclusion.

This surrogate has all of:

\[
Z_\alpha\text{ symbol rank},
\qquad
\text{main/dual Laurent structure},
\qquad
B_2\text{ shift},
\qquad
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R\approx0.
\]

It lacks only exact actual-zeta coefficient compatibility.

So the warning is precise:

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank plus normalized Wronskian scaling is not enough.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Subset-product determinant cancellation

The normalized determinant expands as:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)
=
\sum_S
\widetilde C_S(\alpha)
e^{-im\log N_S}.
\]

Even if all coefficients are well-scaled and not identically zero, this is a fixed-coefficient exponential sum.

The adversarial mechanism is ordinary phase cancellation:

\[
\sum_S
\widetilde C_S(\alpha)
e^{-im\log N_S}
\approx0.
\]

Cauchy–Binet gives structural nonzero-ness, but not pointwise lower bounds.

A lower bound would need one of:

\[
\text{coefficient dominance},
\]

\[
\text{same-sign / positive structure},
\]

\[
\text{sparse block isolation},
\]

\[
\text{admissible-center exclusion},
\]

or

\[
\text{actual subset-product nonrecurrence}.
\]

Absent one of these, the determinant can be small.

---

# 5. First constraint that kills the bad locus

Current ladder result:

| Level | Constraint added | Bad locus survives? | Wronskian small? |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | Phase-relaxed rows | Yes | Yes |
| 1 | Fixed coefficients, independent phases | Yes/generic | Yes |
| 2 | One-parameter prime-log orbit | Plausibly recurrent | Yes near-hits |
| 3 | \(B_2\) | Yes | Yes |
| 4 | Formal \(D_2\) | Yes | Yes |
| 5 | \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank | Yes in surrogate | Yes |
| 6 | \(E_2\) tolerance | Yes unless actual rigidity exceeds tolerance | Yes |
| 7 | Full actual AFE / explicit formula | Unknown | First unbroken level |

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The first potentially lethal constraint is full actual-zeta arithmetic.}
}
\]

Not local geometry. Not symbol rank. Not normalized scaling. Not \(B_2\). Not formal duality.

---

# 6. Stop-rule verdict

No stop-rule counterexample for the actual canonical zeta object has been produced.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not pivot yet.}
}
\]

But the Wronskian route now has a precise required theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual normalized subset-product determinant nonrecurrence at }Z_\alpha\text{-admissible centers.}
}
\]

Without that theorem, the route remains Yellow.

The next adversarial target should be more concrete:

\[
\text{Can the actual coefficient determinant }
\sum_S \widetilde C_S(\alpha)e^{-im\log N_S}
\text{ have polynomially small values at }Z_\alpha(m)=0?
\]

If yes, Wronskian route likely fails.  
If no, Gate 1 has a plausible path.
