## Agent 5 / E — Canonical multilinearity and mixed-minor transfer

Instruction applied: audit the canonical \(\mathbf M\) decomposition and state what is required before any \(\mathbf M\)-lower bound can be treated as canonical. 

---

# 1. Canonical source block

The canonical curvature is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The \(\mathbf M\)-cofactor block is built from

\[
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\qquad
0\le j\le R,
\]

and basis-column operators

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Thus the canonical source matrix is

\[
\mathcal Q_{\can}(m)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}(m)
\right)_{\substack{0\le j\le R\\0\le \ell\le R+1}}.
\]

At a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero,

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m_0,\alpha)
=
\langle \mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha),\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)\rangle,
\]

where \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) is the cofactor vector obtained from \(\mathcal Q_{\can}\).

Because cofactors are exterior minors of degree \(R+1\), \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) is multilinear in the \(R+1\) source rows.

---

# 2. Full multilinear expansion

For each source row \(j\), choose one component

\[
\sigma_j\in\{B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\{B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\}^{R+1}}
\mathbf M[\sigma_0,\ldots,\sigma_R].
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2].
\]

But

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M[X+Y]\ne \mathbf M[X]+\mathbf M[Y].
}
\]

So a lower bound for a pure \(P_2\) cofactor or a pure prime-block cofactor does not automatically lower-bound the canonical object.

---

# 3. Classification of terms

## 3.1 Pure \(P_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[P_2,\ldots,P_2].
\]

This is the cleanest main-prime/subset-product model:

\[
\mathbf M_P(m)
=
\sum_{|S|=R+1}
\mathbf A_S^{P}e^{-im\Omega_S},
\qquad
\Omega_S=\sum_{n\in S}\log n.
\]

This is useful for resonance and zero-set large-sieve analysis.

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{pure }P_2\text{ is only a model lemma, not canonical.}
}
\]

## 3.2 Pure \(D_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[D_2,\ldots,D_2].
\]

This is the reflected-dual analogue. In the hard symmetric range

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1,
\]

there is no general reason to treat \(D_2\) as perturbative. It should be included in the leading model.

## 3.3 Mixed \(P_2/D_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[\sigma_0,\ldots,\sigma_R],
\qquad
\sigma_j\in\{P_2,D_2\},
\]

with both \(P_2\) and \(D_2\) appearing.

These terms can be leading. They encode mixed main/dual subset-product phases and are unavoidable in the actual AFE model.

Therefore a canonical leading model should at least include

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M[P_2+D_2].
}
\]

## 3.4 \(B_2\)-containing terms

\(B_2\) is deterministic:

\[
B_2(t)=\theta'''(t)
=
-\frac18\Re\psi^{(2)}
\left(\frac14+\frac{it}{2}\right).
\]

It is not a source of subset-product oscillation, but \(B_2\)-containing mixed minors can be leading if packet rows become small or normalization amplifies \(B_2\)-rows.

Safe rule:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{-only cofactor mass is diagnostic only.}
}
\]

But:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{-mixed minors must remain in the canonical leading model unless proven perturbative.}
}
\]

## 3.5 \(E_2\)-containing terms

Any minor with at least one \(E_2\)-row is perturbative only under a high-derivative error budget.

Since

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j
\]

can reach derivative order

\[
j+\deg C_\ell
\le
R+(R+1)
=
2R+1,
\]

the necessary condition is

\[
\boxed{
\max_{0\le k\le 2R+1}
|\mathcal D^kE_2(m)|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
}
\]

More invariantly,

\[
\boxed{
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m)
\right|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Leading model recommendation

The safest canonical leading model is

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\lead}=B_2+P_2+D_2.
}
\]

Then

\[
\mathbf M_{\lead}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
\]

The perturbative part is

\[
\mathbf M_{\pert}
=
\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead},
\]

which consists of all multilinear minors with at least one \(E_2\)-row.

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M_{\lead}
+
\mathbf M_{\pert}.
}
\]

This split is justified if and only if \(E_2\) satisfies the \(2R+1\)-derivative error budget with enough margin.

---

# 5. Cofactor perturbation bound

Let

\[
\Delta\mathcal Q
=
\mathcal Q_{\can}-\mathcal Q_{\lead}
=
\mathcal Q[E_2].
\]

Assume row norm control

\[
\|\mathcal Q_{\lead}\|_{\rm row}\le Q^{A_Q}
\]

and

\[
\|\Delta\mathcal Q\|_{\rm row}\le \varepsilon_E.
\]

Since cofactors are degree \(R+1\) minors,

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead}\|
\le
C_R\,\varepsilon_E
\left(
Q^{A_Q}+\varepsilon_E
\right)^R.
\]

The exterior combinatorial factor satisfies

\[
C_R\le \exp(O(R\log R)).
\]

Because

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this loss is polynomial:

\[
\exp(O(R\log R))=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

So the perturbation bound can be written as

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead}\|
\le
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E.
}
\]

---

# 6. Canonical transfer theorem

A safe transfer theorem is:

**Canonical Cofactor Transfer.**  
Suppose

\[
|\mathbf M_{\lead}(m)|\ge Q^{-A_M},
\]

and

\[
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m)
\right|
\le
\varepsilon_E.
\]

Assume cofactor Lipschitz control

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}(m)-\mathbf M_{\lead}(m)\|
\le
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E.
\]

If

\[
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A_M},
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf M_{\can}(m)|
\ge
\frac12 Q^{-A_M}.
}
\]

Equivalently, it is enough to have

\[
\boxed{
\varepsilon_E\le Q^{-A_M-A_L-1}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Prime-block model audit

A prime-supported model is useful for resonance because exact subset-product collisions vanish by unique factorization. But prime-only is not automatically canonical.

Let

\[
q''_{\prime}
=
B_2+P_{2,\prime}+D_{2,\prime}+E_{2,\rm trunc}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathbf M_{\prime}
=
\mathbf M[q''_{\prime}].
\]

A prime-block theorem

\[
|\mathbf M_{\prime}(m)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

transfers to \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) only if the non-prime and omitted packet/tail contribution

\[
q''_{\can}-q''_{\prime}
\]

satisfies the same derivative error condition through order \(2R+1\):

\[
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j
(q''_{\can}-q''_{\prime})(m)
\right|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
\]

Without this, prime-only proves only:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a prime-model }\mathbf M\text{ theorem}.
}
\]

It is not a canonical \(\mathbf M\)-lower bound.

---

# 8. Can \(B_2\) be perturbative?

Possibly, but not by default.

A sufficient condition is

\[
\|\mathcal Q[B_2]\|_{\rm row}
\le
Q^{-A_B}
\|\mathcal Q[P_2+D_2]\|_{\rm row},
\]

with

\[
Q^{A_L}Q^{-A_B}
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A_M}.
\]

If this holds, one may use

\[
q''_{\lead}=P_2+D_2
\]

and treat \(B_2\) with \(E_2\)-type perturbation.

But absent this row dominance, the safe model remains

\[
\boxed{
q''_{\lead}=B_2+P_2+D_2.
}
\]

---

# 9. Final decomposition theorem

The most defensible canonical decomposition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]
+
\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2].
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2]|
\le
\frac12
|\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]|
}
\]

provided

\[
\max_{k\le2R+1}|\mathcal D^kE_2|
\]

is sufficiently small after all operator and cofactor amplification losses.

Thus the theorem agents should prove:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2](m_0)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Then the canonical transfer gives:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

---

# 10. Audit verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Mixed }P_2/D_2/B_2\text{ minors cannot be ignored.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ can be perturbative only with }2R+1\text{ derivative control.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Prime-only or main-only }\mathbf M\text{ results require a separate transfer theorem.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The canonical leading object is }\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
}
\]

So the next \(\mathbf M\)-large-sieve theorem should be stated for

\[
\mathbf M_{\lead}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2],
\]

not for a pure \(P_2\), pure prime, or \(B_2\)-only model.

## Agent 5 / E — Canonical mixed-minor leading model

Instruction applied: refine the canonical leading model for \(\mathbf M\), classify mixed minors, and state the exact theorem \(\mathbf M\)-agents should target. 

---

# 1. Canonical decomposition

The canonical source curvature is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The cofactor vector is multilinear of degree \(R+1\) in the source rows, so

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2].
\]

The safe split is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M_{\lead}
+
\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2],
}
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\lead}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
}
\]

This is the model \(\mathbf M\)-agents should treat as the canonical leading object.

---

# 2. Mixed-minor expansion of \(\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]\)

By multilinearity,

\[
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\{B_2,P_2,D_2\}^{R+1}}
\mathbf M[\sigma_0,\ldots,\sigma_R].
\]

The terms split into the following classes.

## Pure \(P_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[P_2,\ldots,P_2].
\]

This gives the cleanest subset-product expansion:

\[
\sum_{|S|=R+1}\mathbf A_S^P e^{-im\Omega_S}.
\]

It is useful for resonance and kernel analysis, but it is not canonical alone.

## Pure \(D_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[D_2,\ldots,D_2].
\]

This is the reflected-dual analogue. It is potentially leading in the symmetric branch

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1.
\]

## Mixed \(P_2/D_2\)

\[
\mathbf M[\sigma_0,\ldots,\sigma_R],
\qquad
\sigma_j\in\{P_2,D_2\},
\]

with both components appearing.

These mixed minors are potentially leading and must be included. In the hard branch, main and dual branches are comparable, so no canonical proof may discard them without a dominance or nonalignment theorem.

## \(B_2\)-mixed minors

Terms with one or more \(B_2\)-rows:

\[
\mathbf M[\sigma_0,\ldots,\sigma_R],
\qquad
\sigma_j=B_2\text{ for at least one }j.
\]

These are deterministic-background mixed minors. They are canonical. Whether they are perturbative depends on row-scale comparison.

---

# 3. Can \(B_2\) be perturbative?

Only under a row-norm dominance theorem.

A sufficient condition is:

\[
\|\mathcal Q[B_2]\|_{\rm row}
\le
Q^{-A_B}
\|\mathcal Q[P_2+D_2]\|_{\rm row},
\]

with enough margin after exterior amplification:

\[
Q^{A_L}Q^{-A_B}
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A_M}.
\]

If this is proved, then one may use

\[
\mathbf M_{\lead}=\mathbf M[P_2+D_2]
\]

and put \(B_2\) into the perturbative part.

Without this proof, \(B_2\) stays in the leading model:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\lead}=\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
}
\]

Also:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{-only cofactor mass is diagnostic, not canonical proof mass.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Are mixed \(P_2/D_2\) minors leading?

Yes, in the hard symmetric regime.

When

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1,
\]

main and reflected dual coefficients are not separated by a decisive exponential tilt. Therefore all terms in the binomial/exterior expansion of

\[
P_2+D_2
\]

may be comparable:

\[
\mathbf M[P_2^{a},D_2^{R+1-a}],
\qquad
0\le a\le R+1.
\]

So the correct leading theorem must include the full mixed main/dual exterior system.

In tilted regimes,

\[
|\alpha|Q\gtrsim\log Q,
\]

one branch may dominate, but this is a regime-specific reduction, not the uniform canonical theorem.

---

# 5. \(E_2\) perturbation theorem

Since the source block uses

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j,
\qquad
0\le j\le R,\quad 0\le \ell\le R+1,
\]

the derivative order reaches

\[
2R+1.
\]

Required row-level error:

\[
\boxed{
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m)
\right|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
}
\]

Equivalently, if \(C_\ell\) has degree \(\le R+1\),

\[
\boxed{
\max_{0\le k\le 2R+1}
|\mathcal D^kE_2(m)|
\le Q^{-A_E}
}
\]

plus operator-coefficient losses.

Let

\[
\mathcal Q_{\lead}
=
\mathcal Q[B_2+P_2+D_2],
\]

and assume row norm and exterior Lipschitz bounds:

\[
\|\mathcal Q_{\lead}\|_{\rm row}\le Q^{A_Q},
\]

\[
\operatorname{Lip}(\operatorname{cof})\le Q^{A_L}.
\]

Then

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead}\|
\le
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E.
\]

If

\[
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E
\le
\frac12Q^{-A_M},
\]

then

\[
|\mathbf M_{\lead}|\ge Q^{-A_M}
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M_{\can}|\ge \frac12Q^{-A_M}.
\]

---

# 6. Exact leading model theorem

The \(\mathbf M\)-agents should target:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2](m_0)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the canonical leading cofactor theorem.

Then transfer to the full object using:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]
+
\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2],
}
\]

with \(E_2\)-containing minors controlled by the \(2R+1\)-derivative error budget.

---

# 7. Guardrails for \(\mathbf M\)-proofs

A proof is not canonical if it proves only one of:

\[
\mathbf M[P_2,\ldots,P_2],
\]

\[
\mathbf M[D_2,\ldots,D_2],
\]

\[
\mathbf M[B_2,\ldots,B_2],
\]

or a prime-only submodel.

Such results are useful lemmas only. They need transfer/dominance arguments.

A proof is canonical-leading if it handles:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
}
\]

A proof is fully canonical if it also proves perturbative control of all \(E_2\)-containing minors:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2]|
\le
\frac12|\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2]|.
}
\]

---

# 8. Final audit theorem

**Canonical Mixed-Minor Cofactor Theorem.**  
Let

\[
q''_{\lead}=B_2+P_2+D_2.
\]

Suppose:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M[q''_{\lead}](m_0)|\ge Q^{-A_M}.
\]

Suppose also:

\[
\max_{j,\ell}
|C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m_0)|
\le \varepsilon_E,
\]

and

\[
Q^{A_L}\varepsilon_E
\le
\frac12Q^{-A_M}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A_M}/2.
}
\]

This is the guardrail statement all \(\mathbf M\)-agents should use.

## Agent 5 / E — Canonical audit for final Wronskian incidence

Instruction applied: keep the final angle/incidence theorem canonical and prevent drift into a toy Wronskian. 

---

# 1. Fully canonical Wronskian matrix

Work with

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m,
\qquad
\lambda Q\asymp1.
\]

The canonical rows are:

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\]

\[
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

The canonical curvature is:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The basis-normalized Wronskian matrix is:

\[
\mathcal W_{\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}.
\]

The determinant is:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m,\alpha)
=
\det \mathcal W_{\can}(m,\alpha).
\]

At a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\]

expansion along the \(Z_\alpha\)-row gives:

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m_0,\alpha)
=
\langle \mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha),\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)\rangle.
\]

Here:

\[
\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)
\]

is the derivative/jet vector of the \(Z_\alpha\)-row in the chosen normalized column basis, and

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)
\]

is the cofactor/Hodge-dual vector generated by the canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-jet block.

---

# 2. Where \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\) enter

Each source row is:

\[
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}
=
\mathcal D^jB_2
+
\mathcal D^jP_2
+
\mathcal D^jD_2
+
\mathcal D^jE_2.
\]

After applying \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)\),

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}
=
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jB_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jP_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jD_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2.
\]

Thus the determinant and cofactor vector are multilinear in the components:

\[
B_2,\quad P_2,\quad D_2,\quad E_2.
\]

The safe leading model is:

\[
q''_{\lead}=B_2+P_2+D_2.
\]

Then:

\[
\mathbf M_{\can}
=
\mathbf M_{\lead}
+
\mathbf M_{\pert}[E_2],
\]

where

\[
\mathbf M_{\lead}
=
\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2].
\]

Important rule:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Pure }P_2,\text{ pure }D_2,\text{ prime-only, or }B_2\text{-only Wronskians are model lemmas, not canonical final incidence theorems.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Parked inputs

The current final-incidence stage assumes two named black-box inputs.

## Input 1 — Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence

This gives:

\[
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A_z}
\]

at \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\).

It packages the local \(Z_\alpha\)-amplitude / cell-sum theorem.

## Input 2 — Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence

This gives:

\[
|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A_M}
\]

or, in the model-plus-transfer structure,

\[
|\mathbf M_{\lead}(m_0)|\ge Q^{-A_M}
\]

plus a canonical transfer:

\[
|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead}|
\le \frac12 Q^{-A_M}.
\]

These two inputs lower-bound the norms. They do **not** lower-bound the angle.

---

# 4. Remaining canonical theorem

The remaining theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
|\langle\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha),\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\,|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0)|.
}
\]

Equivalently, under the two parked norm inputs,

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is a genuine angle/incidence theorem. It cannot be replaced by separate norm lower bounds.

Bad event:

\[
\langle\mathbf z',\mathbf M_{\can}\rangle\approx0
\]

means:

\[
\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)
\]

is nearly tangent to the source-row span whose normal is \(\mathbf M_{\can}\). In exterior-algebra language:

\[
\mathbf z'\in V_{q''_{\can}}
\]

up to \(Q^{-A}\)-accuracy.

---

# 5. Derivative depth and \(E_2\) requirements

The full determinant uses:

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)G_j,
\qquad
0\le j,\ell\le R+1.
\]

For the source rows:

\[
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

Thus source-row derivative order reaches:

\[
j+\deg C_\ell
\le
R+(R+1)
=
2R+1.
\]

So \(E_2\)-transfer for the final determinant requires:

\[
\boxed{
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m)
\right|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
}
\]

A sufficient simpler condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\max_{0\le k\le2R+1}
|\mathcal D^kE_2(m)|
\le Q^{-A_E}
}
\]

after absorbing \(C_\ell\)-coefficient and basis-conditioning losses.

For the \(Z_\alpha\)-row, any model-to-canonical transfer must also control the corresponding \(Z_\alpha\) row and its first nonzero jet vector. If \(Z_\alpha\) is modelled/truncated, require:

\[
\|\mathbf z'_{\can}-\mathbf z'_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_Z.
\]

For the cofactor vector:

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_M.
\]

---

# 6. Final incidence transfer theorem

Let

\[
\mathbf z_{\model}'\quad\text{and}\quad \mathbf M_{\model}
\]

be the vectors generated by a model Wronskian, and suppose the model angle theorem holds:

\[
|\langle \mathbf z_{\model}',\mathbf M_{\model}\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z_{\model}'|\,|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Assume model norm lower bounds:

\[
|\mathbf z_{\model}'|\ge Q^{-A_z},
\]

\[
|\mathbf M_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_M}.
\]

Assume canonical transfer bounds:

\[
\|\mathbf z_{\can}'-\mathbf z_{\model}'\|\le \varepsilon_Z,
\]

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_M.
\]

Then

\[
\begin{aligned}
|\langle \mathbf z_{\can}',\mathbf M_{\can}\rangle
-
\langle \mathbf z_{\model}',\mathbf M_{\model}\rangle|
&\le
\varepsilon_Z|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_M|\mathbf z_{\model}'|
+
\varepsilon_Z\varepsilon_M.
\end{aligned}
\]

A sufficient transfer margin is:

\[
\varepsilon_Z|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_M|\mathbf z_{\model}'|
+
\varepsilon_Z\varepsilon_M
\le
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z_{\model}'|\,|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Using the norm lower bounds, it is enough to require, for some large margin \(C\),

\[
\varepsilon_Z\le Q^{-A_\angle-A_z-C},
\]

\[
\varepsilon_M\le Q^{-A_\angle-A_M-C}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|\langle \mathbf z_{\can}',\mathbf M_{\can}\rangle|
\ge
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z_{\model}'|\,|\mathbf M_{\model}|
\ge
Q^{-A'}
}
\]

and hence:

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Canonical final-incidence audit theorem

**Canonical Wronskian Incidence Transfer.**  
Let

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\model}
\]

be a model Wronskian built from a specified \(Z_{\alpha,\model}\) and

\[
q''_{\model}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_{2,\rm trunc}
\]

or a declared approximation. Suppose:

1. model \(Z\)-jet lower bound:

\[
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_z};
\]

2. model cofactor lower bound:

\[
|\mathbf M_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_M};
\]

3. model angle theorem:

\[
|\langle \mathbf z'_{\model},\mathbf M_{\model}\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\,|\mathbf M_{\model}|;
\]

4. \(Z\)-row transfer:

\[
\|\mathbf z'_{\can}-\mathbf z'_{\model}\|\le Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}};
\]

5. cofactor transfer:

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|\le Q^{-A_M^{\rm err}};
\]

6. error margins:

\[
Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}}|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
Q^{-A_M^{\rm err}}|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
+
Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}-A_M^{\rm err}}
\le
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\,|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 8. Audit verdict

The final angle theorem must be stated for the canonical vectors:

\[
\mathbf z'_{\can},
\qquad
\mathbf M_{\can}=\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2].
\]

Safe theorem path:

\[
\boxed{
\text{model angle theorem}
+
\text{Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}
+
\text{Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence}
+
\text{canonical transfer}
\Rightarrow
\text{canonical Wronskian lower bound}.
}
\]

Do not accept:

- pure main \(P_2\) angle;
- prime-only angle;
- \(B_2\)-only angle;
- main-only \(\mathbf M\);
- angle theorem without \(E_2\) derivative-transfer margins;
- angle theorem that ignores cofactor multilinearity.

Final Agent 5 guardrail:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The remaining incidence theorem is an angle theorem between canonical } \mathbf z'\text{ and canonical }\mathbf M.
}
\]

It is not solved by norm lower bounds alone.

## Agent 5 / E — Canonical final-Wronskian audit

Instruction applied: keep the final Wronskian incidence theorem canonical, including \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\), the parked \(\mathbf z'\) and \(\mathbf M\) inputs, and the model-to-canonical transfer requirements. 

---

# 1. Fully canonical Wronskian matrix

Use

\[
\mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m,
\qquad
\lambda Q\asymp1.
\]

The canonical rows are

\[
G_0=Z_\alpha,
\]

\[
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

The canonical curvature is

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The full basis-normalized Wronskian matrix is

\[
\mathcal W_{\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\left(
C_\ell(\mathcal D)G_j(m,\alpha)
\right)_{0\le j,\ell\le R+1}.
\]

So explicitly:

\[
\mathcal W_{\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
C_0(\mathcal D)Z_\alpha & C_1(\mathcal D)Z_\alpha & \cdots & C_{R+1}(\mathcal D)Z_\alpha\\
C_0(\mathcal D)q''_{\can} & C_1(\mathcal D)q''_{\can} & \cdots & C_{R+1}(\mathcal D)q''_{\can}\\
C_0(\mathcal D)\mathcal Dq''_{\can} & C_1(\mathcal D)\mathcal Dq''_{\can} & \cdots & C_{R+1}(\mathcal D)\mathcal Dq''_{\can}\\
\vdots & \vdots & & \vdots\\
C_0(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^R q''_{\can} & C_1(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^R q''_{\can} & \cdots & C_{R+1}(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^R q''_{\can}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m,\alpha)
=
\det \mathcal W_{\can}(m,\alpha).
\]

At

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\]

expansion along the \(Z_\alpha\)-row gives

\[
\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)
=
\langle
\mathbf z'_{\can}(m_0,\alpha),
\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)
\rangle.
\]

---

# 2. Where \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\) enter

Every source row decomposes as

\[
\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}
=
\mathcal D^jB_2
+
\mathcal D^jP_2
+
\mathcal D^jD_2
+
\mathcal D^jE_2.
\]

After basis-column operators,

\[
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^j q''_{\can}
=
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jB_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jP_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jD_2
+
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2.
\]

Thus \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\) enter not only the cofactor vector \(\mathbf M\), but the full determinant through every \(q''_{\can}\)-source row.

Because the determinant is multilinear in rows, the source block has mixed minors involving:

\[
P_2/D_2,\quad
P_2/B_2,\quad
D_2/B_2,\quad
P_2/D_2/B_2,
\]

and any term containing \(E_2\) belongs to the perturbative tail only if high-derivative control is available.

The safe leading source is therefore:

\[
q''_{\lead}=B_2+P_2+D_2,
\]

not \(P_2\)-only, \(D_2\)-only, prime-only, or \(B_2\)-only.

---

# 3. Parked canonical inputs

The final angle stage assumes two parked norm inputs.

## Input 1 — Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence

This gives

\[
|\mathbf z'_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A_z}.
\]

It is a named arithmetic input, not proved in the current architecture.

## Input 2 — Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence

This gives

\[
|\mathbf M_{\can}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A_M}.
\]

Equivalently, under the model/transfer structure:

\[
|\mathbf M_{\lead}(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A_M},
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\lead}|
\le \frac12Q^{-A_M}.
\]

These inputs only lower-bound the two vector norms. They do not imply an angle lower bound.

---

# 4. Remaining canonical theorem

The remaining theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
|\langle
\mathbf z'_{\can},
\mathbf M_{\can}
\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\can}|\,
|\mathbf M_{\can}|.
}
\]

Equivalently, using the parked norm inputs:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the **Wronskian Angle Nonalignment / Ordinary Incidence Theorem** if no further structural proof is found.

The bad event is:

\[
\langle\mathbf z'_{\can},\mathbf M_{\can}\rangle\approx0.
\]

Geometrically, if \(V_{\can}\) is the span of the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet rows, then \(\mathbf M_{\can}\) is the Hodge normal to \(V_{\can}\), and the bad event is

\[
\mathbf z'_{\can}\approx V_{\can}.
\]

So the canonical theorem is a quantitative row-span avoidance theorem:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(\mathbf z'_{\can},V_{\can})
\ge
Q^{-A}|\mathbf z'_{\can}|.
\]

---

# 5. \(E_2\)-derivative requirements in the full determinant

For the source rows,

\[
G_{j+1}=\mathcal D^j q''_{\can},
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

The column operators satisfy, schematically,

\[
\deg C_\ell\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Therefore the largest source-row derivative order is

\[
j+\deg C_\ell
\le
R+(R+1)
=
2R+1.
\]

Thus the existing \(2R+1\) derivative budget is sufficient for the source block and cofactor vector:

\[
\boxed{
\max_{0\le k\le 2R+1}
|\mathcal D^kE_2(m)|
\le Q^{-A_E}
}
\]

after absorbing the coefficient norms of the \(C_\ell\).

More invariantly, require

\[
\boxed{
\max_{j,\ell}
\left|
C_\ell(\mathcal D)\mathcal D^jE_2(m)
\right|
\le Q^{-A_E}.
}
\]

The \(Z_\alpha\)-row may require its own model-to-canonical transfer if a model \(Z_{\alpha,\model}\) is used. Then require

\[
\|\mathbf z'_{\can}-\mathbf z'_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_Z.
\]

For the source/cofactor block require

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_M.
\]

No derivative order higher than \(2R+1\) is needed for \(q''_{\can}\) unless the chosen \(C_\ell\) secretly has degree \(>R+1\) or the normalization introduces additional differentiations. Under the present conventions, \(2R+1\) is the correct budget.

---

# 6. Canonical transfer for the final incidence theorem

Let a model Wronskian produce vectors

\[
\mathbf z'_{\model},
\qquad
\mathbf M_{\model}.
\]

Assume the model angle theorem:

\[
|\langle
\mathbf z'_{\model},
\mathbf M_{\model}
\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\,
|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Assume model norm lower bounds:

\[
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_z},
\]

\[
|\mathbf M_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_M}.
\]

Assume canonical transfer bounds:

\[
\|\mathbf z'_{\can}-\mathbf z'_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_Z,
\]

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|\le \varepsilon_M.
\]

Then

\[
\begin{aligned}
&
|\langle
\mathbf z'_{\can},\mathbf M_{\can}
\rangle
-
\langle
\mathbf z'_{\model},\mathbf M_{\model}
\rangle|
\\
&\qquad\le
\varepsilon_Z|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_M|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_Z\varepsilon_M.
\end{aligned}
\]

A sufficient margin is:

\[
\varepsilon_Z|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_M|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
+
\varepsilon_Z\varepsilon_M
\le
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Using the norm lower bounds, it is enough to require

\[
\varepsilon_Z\le Q^{-A_\angle-A_z-C},
\]

\[
\varepsilon_M\le Q^{-A_\angle-A_M-C},
\]

for \(C\) large enough to absorb constants and conditioning losses.

Then

\[
|\langle
\mathbf z'_{\can},\mathbf M_{\can}
\rangle|
\ge
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
|\mathbf M_{\model}|
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\]

and therefore

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)|
\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Canonical final-incidence transfer theorem

**Canonical Wronskian Incidence Transfer.**

Let

\[
q''_{\model}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_{2,\rm trunc}
\]

or another explicitly specified approximation. Suppose:

1. **Model \(Z\)-jet lower bound**

\[
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_z}.
\]

2. **Model cofactor lower bound**

\[
|\mathbf M_{\model}|\ge Q^{-A_M}.
\]

3. **Model angle theorem**

\[
|\langle
\mathbf z'_{\model},\mathbf M_{\model}
\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

4. **\(Z\)-row transfer**

\[
\|\mathbf z'_{\can}-\mathbf z'_{\model}\|
\le Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}}.
\]

5. **Cofactor/source transfer**

\[
\|\mathbf M_{\can}-\mathbf M_{\model}\|
\le Q^{-A_M^{\rm err}}.
\]

6. **Error margin**

\[
Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}}|\mathbf M_{\model}|
+
Q^{-A_M^{\rm err}}|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
+
Q^{-A_Z^{\rm err}-A_M^{\rm err}}
\le
\frac12
Q^{-A_\angle}
|\mathbf z'_{\model}|
|\mathbf M_{\model}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_{R,\can}(m_0,\alpha)|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

---

# 8. What must not be accepted as final

Do not accept any of the following as a canonical final-incidence theorem:

1. pure-main \(P_2\) angle theorem;
2. prime-only angle theorem without transfer;
3. \(B_2\)-only determinant or cofactor mass;
4. main-only \(\mathbf M\) nonalignment;
5. theorem excluding \(D_2\) in the symmetric branch;
6. theorem omitting \(E_2\) without \(2R+1\) derivative control;
7. theorem using norm lower bounds for \(\mathbf z'\) and \(\mathbf M\) but no angle lower bound;
8. theorem treating the cofactor map as linear in \(B_2,P_2,D_2,E_2\).

---

# 9. Final audit verdict

The active theorem must be stated for canonical vectors:

\[
\mathbf z'_{\can},
\qquad
\mathbf M_{\can}=\mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2].
\]

The safe proof path is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{model angle theorem}
+
\text{Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}
+
\text{Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence}
+
\text{canonical transfer}
\Rightarrow
\text{canonical Wronskian lower bound}.
}
\]

The remaining gap is precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical angle/nonalignment between }\mathbf z'\text{ and }\mathbf M.
}
\]

This is not implied by the two parked norm lower bounds.

## Agent 5 / E — no-go and numerical evidence archive

Instruction applied: consolidate the no-go results and numerical/proxy evidence into a proof-state map. No new numerics. 

## 1. Numerical evidence summary

| branch | scope | result | status |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Packet-frequency determinant stress | \(R\le16\), \(m_{\min}=50\), reflected/actual-like dual, \(E_2\) gates | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | Supports ordinary Wronskian route |
| Local \(Z_\alpha\)-amplitude v1 | 1152 rows, \(a\in\{1,R,2R,(\log Q)^2\}\) | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | Supports local amplitude / \(|\mathbf z'|\) input |
| Cell-sum v2 | harder adversarial targets, direct jet minimizers, cell diagnostics | Yellow/Orange/Red \(=0\) | Supports cell-sum route |
| Multiblock \(K=8\) cell test | 794 rows, log-uniform support coverage | Yellow \(=0\), Orange \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | Strongest numerical support for cell theorem |
| Small-\(Z_\alpha\) adversarial rows | \(|Z_\alpha|\sim10^{-15}\)–\(10^{-12}\) | local amplitude, jets, and cell mass stayed large | Positive evidence for \(Z_\alpha=0\Rightarrow|\mathbf z'|\ge Q^{-A}\) |

The strongest numerical conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)\approx0
\not\Rightarrow
\text{local amplitude / jet / cell collapse}.
}
\]

In the \(K=8\) multiblock test, direct minimizers drove \(Z_\alpha\) extremely close to zero, but \(A((\log Q)^2)\), \(J_{\max}\), and \(\mathrm{CellMassRatio}_{2R}\) remained many orders above the danger thresholds. This is precisely the empirical behavior predicted by the Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence input.

---

## 2. No-go / limitation archive

| no-go / limitation | conclusion | consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Phase-relaxed models | Can satisfy artificial cell-flatness / recurrence patterns | Cell theorem must use actual one-parameter phases \(e^{-im\log n}\) |
| Averaged large sieve \(\not\Rightarrow\) pointwise | Isolated bad zeros can survive averaged lower bounds | Cofactor-vector pointwise nonrecurrence remains separate |
| Symbol rank \(\not\Rightarrow\) angle | Symbol-rank/transversality does not force \(\langle\mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle\ne0\) pointwise | Final incidence requires separate angle theorem |
| Hodge/exterior geometry | Identifies \(\mathbf M\) as normal vector, but gives no quantitative lower angle | Angle theorem remains an arithmetic input |
| Main-only / prime-only models | Not canonical without transfer | Must include or transfer to \(B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2\) |
| \(B_2\)-only diagnostics | Can show background behavior but not canonical cofactor lower bound | Never classify as proof-level Gate 1 evidence |
| \(E_2\) truncation | Unsafe without derivative control through \(2R+1\) | Need tail/error transfer theorem |
| \(q_0\)-wall | Gate 1 mass may occur near \(q_0\approx0\); current sampling does not force \(q_0\)-nondegeneracy | Gate 2 needs separate wall/nondegenerate split |

---

## 3. Branch archive table

| branch | result | status | remaining input |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active packet-frequency block | correct block identified; low-prime compression avoided | structurally settled | none unless AFE convention changes |
| \(Z_\alpha\)-symbol rank | non-polynomial ratio structure visible on active blocks | essentially cleared | exact canonical AFE audit only |
| Determinant stress | no Yellow/Red through \(R\le16\) | numerically favorable | proof of arithmetic nonrecurrence |
| Local amplitude / \(|\mathbf z'|\) | no local flatness found | numerically favorable | Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence |
| Multiblock cell theorem | \(K=8\) tests clean | named input, open | actual prime-log cell nonrecurrence |
| \(\mathbf M\) cofactor vector | diagonal/exterior mass likely manageable; kernel hard | named input, open | Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence / zero spectral large sieve |
| Angle/incidence | no structural sign/Hodge/positivity proof found | named input, open | Wronskian Angle Nonalignment |
| Singular branch | ordinary route does not cover deep singular scale | separate open branch | Singular Explicit-Formula Anti-Design Exclusion |
| Gate 2 / row conversion | \(q_0\)-wall remains | open downstream | nondegenerate/wall split and correction control |

---

## 4. Current named ordinary-route inputs

The ordinary branch is now conditional on three named arithmetic inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Input 1: Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}
}
\]

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Input 2: Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence}
}
\]

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf M(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Input 3: Wronskian Angle Nonalignment / Ordinary Incidence}
}
\]

\[
|\langle\mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle|
\ge
Q^{-A}|\mathbf z'||\mathbf M|.
\]

Together they give:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\widetilde{\mathcal W}_R(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

---

## 5. Evidence interpretation

The numerical archive strongly supports the first ordinary input:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow |\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

because even adversarial near-zeros did not produce amplitude, jet, or multiblock cell collapse.

The archive does **not** prove:

\[
|\mathbf M|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

or

\[
|\langle\mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle|
\ge Q^{-A}|\mathbf z'||\mathbf M|.
\]

Those remain actual-zeta arithmetic inputs, not numerical conclusions.

---

## 6. Agent 5 final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{No further broad numerical stress testing is recommended.}
}
\]

Reactivate Agent 5 only for:

1. a targeted \(K=16\) or projected-cell diagnostic requested by theorem agents;
2. a canonical-transfer audit after an explicit model theorem is proposed;
3. a narrow test of a newly proposed failure mechanism.

Current archive verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary route is numerically unbroken, but proof is conditional on three named arithmetic inputs plus downstream Gate 2 closure.}
}
\]

## Agent 5 / E — correction-cancellation and nondegenerate row-conversion theorem

Instruction applied: formalize the nondegenerate row-conversion theorem under \(|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}\), and isolate the correction-control input. 

---

# 1. Setup

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Assume the nondegenerate branch:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

Assume a pointwise Gate 2 sample:

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
\left(
\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2
\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

The known row architecture is:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

with

\[
H=yq''_{\can}
\sim
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}.
\]

So the row-conversion mechanism is:

\[
\text{Gate 2 anisotropy}
+
|q_0|\not\approx0
+
|u|\not\approx0
\Longrightarrow
A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}\text{ non-small}.
\]

The crucial issue is that the rows are divisible by

\[
H\sim q_0u^3.
\]

Thus pointwise Gate 2 alone does not automatically give a row lower bound unless enough \(u\)-mass survives pointwise.

---

# 2. Leading row expansions

Use the normalized leading-row form

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7 H\Gamma_G
+
\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9 H\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\mathcal R_9,
\]

where \(c_7,c_9\neq0\) are fixed normalization constants depending on the chosen row normalization. In the normalization suggested by the shorthand

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G,
\qquad
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

one may take \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\).

For \(H\), write the required expansion as

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H.
\]

Substituting:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
+
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G
+
\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
+
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\mathcal R_9.
\]

Define the leading row vector

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
\left(
\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2
\right)^{1/2},
\]

for some fixed \(c_0>0\), after absorbing \(c_7,c_9\).

---

# 3. What Gate 2 gives and what it does not give

Gate 2 at \(m_\ast\) gives

\[
|u|^2
\left(
\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2
\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
|u|
\left(
\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|u|^3
\left(
\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2
\right)^{1/2}
=
|u|^2
\cdot
|u|
\left(
\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge
|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Thus the leading row lower bound becomes

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^2 Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 Q^{-C_q-A_2/2}|u|^2.
\]

So a pointwise row lower bound also needs a pointwise lower bound for \(u\):

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}.
\]

Then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-2C_u-O(1)}.
\]

Conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Nondegenerate row conversion needs }|q_0|\text{ and }|u|\text{ non-small at the selected point.}
}
\]

Gate 2 alone controls the product

\[
|u|\cdot|\Gamma|,
\]

not \(u\) separately.

---

# 4. Nondegenerate row-conversion theorem

A clean theorem is:

**Nondegenerate Row-Conversion Theorem.**  
Suppose at \(m_\ast\),

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

and

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
\left(
\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2
\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Suppose the leading expansions hold:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\). Suppose the correction terms satisfy

\[
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
+
|\mathcal R_H|
\left(
|\Gamma_G|+|\Gamma_{G,2}|
\right)
\le
\frac12
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge
Q^{-C},
\]

where one may take

\[
C=
C_q+\frac{A_2}{2}+2C_u+O(1).
\]

This is the correct nondegenerate branch row-conversion statement.

---

# 5. Correction terms that can cancel the leading rows

The correction-control theorem must cover at least four sources.

## 5.1 Error in \(H\)

The expansion

\[
H=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H
\]

must satisfy

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
\left(
|\Gamma_G|+|\Gamma_{G,2}|
\right)
\le
\frac14|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3.
\]

## 5.2 Lower-order row corrections

For

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

need

\[
|\mathcal R_7|+|\mathcal R_9|
\le
\frac14|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

## 5.3 Canonical \(E_2\)/tail corrections

Since

\[
u=q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

any row expansion using a model \(u_{\model}\) must control

\[
u_{\can}-u_{\model}
\]

through the row-conversion algebra.

If the rows involve derivatives up to order \(k_{\rm row}\), require

\[
\max_{j\le k_{\rm row}}
|\mathcal D^j(u_{\can}-u_{\model})|
\le Q^{-A_E}
\]

with enough margin relative to the leading term.

## 5.4 Projection / normalization corrections

If \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}\) are projected, normalized, or gauge-adjusted rows, the projection error must satisfy the same half-leading bound.

---

# 6. Correction-control theorem

A usable abstract correction theorem:

Let

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
+
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}.
\]

If

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

With the nondegenerate assumptions,

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-2C_u-O(1)}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-2C_u-O(1)}.
\]

This theorem is elementary but indispensable. Without it, a formal leading term does not yield a row lower bound.

---

# 7. Remaining assumptions

The nondegenerate row-conversion theorem depends on:

1. pointwise \(q_0\)-nondegeneracy:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q};
\]

2. pointwise source non-smallness:

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u};
\]

3. Gate 2 anisotropy at the same point:

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2};
\]

4. exact leading row expansions for \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}\);

5. \(H\)-expansion control:

\[
H=-q_0u^3/288+\mathcal R_H;
\]

6. correction-control:

\[
|\mathrm{Correction}|\le\frac12|\mathrm{Leading}|.
\]

The pointwise \(u\)-lower bound is not optional unless Gate 2 is strengthened to include it.

---

# 8. Main obstruction

The nondegenerate branch is not merely

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

It also requires the selected sampling point to avoid

\[
|u|\ll Q^{-C_u}.
\]

Since Gate 2 is an integrated or product statement, one needs a sampling lemma selecting \(m_\ast\) such that all three hold:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

This is a separate sampling/concentration input.

---

# 9. Final row-conversion theorem

The final nondegenerate branch statement should be:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},\\
&|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},\\
&|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2},\\
&|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}(m_\ast)|
\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}(m_\ast)|
\end{aligned}
}
\]

implies

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

with

\[
C=C_q+\frac{A_2}{2}+2C_u+O(1).
\]

This closes only the **nondegenerate row-conversion branch**.

The remaining downstream blockers are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sampling point with }q_0,u,\text{ and Gate 2 anisotropy all non-small;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
q_0\text{-wall branch;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
correction-control theorem for the actual row expansions.}
}
\]

## Agent 5 / E — Nondegenerate row conversion and correction control

Instruction applied: formalize the row-conversion theorem in the branch

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

The output below is theorem-level, with all assumptions explicit. 

---

# 1. Objects and assumptions

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Gate 2 supplies a point or set where

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Known row architecture:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G,
\qquad
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

with

\[
H=yq''_{\can}
\sim
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}.
\]

For a proof-level nondegenerate row conversion, write the expansions as

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7 H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9 H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H,
\]

where \(c_7,c_9\neq0\) are normalization constants. If the row basis is normalized, absorb its powers into \(c_7,c_9\) and the correction terms.

---

# 2. Leading row vector

Define

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
\]

and

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Then, for \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\),

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

Gate 2 gives

\[
|u|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^2 Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}
\]

and requiring pointwise source non-smallness

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

So the nondegenerate branch requires not only \(q_0\neq0\), but also a pointwise lower bound for \(u\).

---

# 3. Correction terms

Collect all corrections as

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
-
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}.
\]

From the expansions,

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

A sufficient correction-control condition is

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Equivalently, it is enough to require

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\]

for a sufficiently small fixed \(c>0\).

The correction audit must include:

1. error in the \(H=-q_0u^3/288\) expansion;
2. lower-order row corrections in \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}\);
3. canonical \(E_2\)/tail errors in \(u=q''_{\can}\);
4. projection/gauge/normalization corrections;
5. any row-basis conditioning losses.

---

# 4. No cancellation issue between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\)

There is no destructive cancellation if one uses the vector norm

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
\left(
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\]

or the sum

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|.
\]

The two rows are independent coordinates. Cancellation can occur inside each row through corrections, but not between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\) if using a norm.

Thus the right lower-bound conclusion is

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

# 5. Nondegenerate Row-Conversion Theorem

**Theorem.** Suppose at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Assume the leading expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), and assume the correction-control condition

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge
Q^{-C}
}
\]

with

\[
C=
C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

---

# 6. Remaining assumptions

The theorem is conditional on selecting \(m_\ast\) with three simultaneous properties:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)(m_\ast)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Gate 2 alone gives the product condition, not the separate \(q_0\) and \(u\) lower bounds. Therefore a sampling theorem is still required.

The correction-control theorem is also open until the exact row expansions and tail/projection terms are bounded.

---

# 7. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{The nondegenerate row-conversion branch is structurally clear.}
}
\]

It reduces to two remaining inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sampling with }q_0,u,\text{ and Gate 2 anisotropy all non-small;}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|\mathrm{Correction}|\le\frac12|\mathrm{Leading}|.
}
\]

The \(q_0\)-wall branch

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C}
\]

is not handled by this theorem and requires either a wall-exclusion theorem or a source-robust row.

## Agent 5 / E — Nondegenerate row-conversion final theorem

Instruction applied: finalize the nondegenerate branch theorem and isolate exactly what it assumes. 

## 1. Leading row vector

Assume

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

Known expansions:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Then the leading row vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

For \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\),

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

---

## 2. Lower bound from Gate 2 pointwise mass

Assume pointwise Gate 2 mass:

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then

\[
|u|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\ge
Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0|q_0|\,|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-O(1)}|u|^2.
\]

Thus a row lower bound follows if either:

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or if such a bound is derived from a global upper bound on the anisotropy factors.

---

## 3. Is a separate \(|u|\) lower bound required?

From

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}
\]

alone, \(|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}\) does **not** follow unless we also know an upper bound

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

If such a complexity bound holds, then

\[
|u|^2Q^{2C_\Gamma}\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

so

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

Therefore there are two valid formulations.

### Stronger pointwise formulation

Assume explicitly:

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}.
\]

Then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

### Complexity-derived formulation

Assume instead:

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Then

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma},
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac{3}{2}A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

The first version is cleaner; the second version is useful if Gate 2 sampling naturally supplies only the product lower bound plus analytic-complexity upper bounds.

---

## 4. Correction condition

Let

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
+
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}.
\]

The correction vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The needed correction-control condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient expanded condition is

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\]

for sufficiently small fixed \(c>0\).

This condition must include:

\[
E_2
\]

tail errors, projection/gauge corrections, row-normalization errors, and lower-order row terms.

---

## 5. No cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\)

Use the vector norm

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
\left(
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2
\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Then cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\) is impossible because they are separate coordinates.

If

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Consequently,

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

## 6. Final nondegenerate row-conversion theorem

**Theorem — Nondegenerate row conversion.**  
Suppose at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}(m_\ast)\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Assume the row expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), and assume

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

In the explicit-\(|u|\) formulation, one may take

\[
C=C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

In the complexity-derived formulation, one may take

\[
C=C_q+\frac32A_2+2C_\Gamma+O(1).
\]

---

## 7. Hidden assumptions now exposed

The theorem depends on:

1. A sampling point \(m_\ast\) satisfying \(q_0\)-nondegeneracy:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

2. A pointwise Gate 2 lower bound:

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

3. Either explicit source non-smallness:

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or polynomial anisotropy upper bounds.

4. Exact row expansions for \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f},H\).

5. Correction control:

\[
|\mathrm{Correction}|\le\frac12|\mathrm{Leading}|.
\]

This theorem does **not** handle the wall branch

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}.
\]

The wall branch still requires either:

\[
\boxed{\text{a }q_0\text{-wall exclusion theorem}}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{\text{a source-robust row/invariant not divisible by }q_0\text{ or }H.}
\]

|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},

|u(m_\ast)|^2
\left(

\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H.
\]

The leading vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).

If \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0|q_0|\,|u|^3
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Gate 2 pointwise mass gives

\[
|u|
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}
\ge
Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^2 Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

So, with either an explicit source lower bound

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or a polynomial anisotropy upper bound

\[
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}(m_\ast)\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

we get a polynomial leading-row lower bound.

Explicit-\(u\) version:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

Anisotropy-upper-bound version:

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\qquad
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}
\]

implies

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma},
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

---

# 2. Correction-control condition

Define

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
+
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}.
\]

Here

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The required condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient expanded form is

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

The correction audit must include:

\[
\mathcal R_H,\quad
\mathcal R_7,\quad
\mathcal R_9,
\]

plus canonical \(E_2\)-tail errors, projection/gauge corrections, row-normalization losses, and any lower-order row terms.

Using the vector norm prevents cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\):

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
\left(|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Thus

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\Rightarrow
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

# 3. Sampling theorem needed from Gate 2

Gate 2 gives an integrated statement:

\[
\int_I |u|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\,dm
\ge
Q^{-A}|I|.
\]

To use the nondegenerate row theorem, one needs a sample point \(m_\ast\) where:

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)(m_\ast)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}(m_\ast)\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

A sufficient sampling theorem is:

**Nondegenerate Gate 2 Sampling.** Suppose

\[
\int_I |u|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\,dm
\ge Q^{-A}|I|,
\]

and the non-wall region carries a polynomial fraction of this mass:

\[
\int_{I\cap\{|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}\}}
|u|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\,dm
\ge Q^{-A'}|I|.
\]

Assume also the anisotropy upper bound

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}
\]

on \(I\), or an equivalent source lower-bound sampling lemma. Then there exists \(m_\ast\in I\) such that

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)(m_\ast)
\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

and

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-A''/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

This supplies the pointwise hypotheses for row conversion.

---

# 4. Complete nondegenerate downstream theorem

Combining sampling and row conversion:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\int_{I\cap\{|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}\}}
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\,dm
\ge Q^{-A}|I|,\\
&(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}
\quad\text{on }I,\\
&|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\quad\text{at the sampled point}
\end{aligned}
}
\]

implies there exists \(m_\ast\in I\) such that

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

The exponent \(C\) depends on

\[
A,\ C_q,\ C_\Gamma,
\]

and the row-normalization/correction losses.

---

# 5. What remains outside this theorem

This theorem does **not** solve the wall branch:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}.
\]

If Gate 2 mass concentrates there, then the known rows

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G,
\qquad
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

with

\[
H\sim -\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\]

can vanish despite live \(u\)- and \(\Gamma_G\)-mass.

So the downstream architecture requires a split:

## Nondegenerate branch

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}
\]

handled by:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2 mass on non-wall set}
+
\text{correction control}
\Rightarrow
\text{row lower bound}.
}
\]

## Wall branch

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}
\]

still needs one of:

\[
\boxed{\text{Source-Robust Row Theorem}}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{q_0\text{-Wall Exclusion Theorem}.}
\]

---

# 6. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Nondegenerate row conversion is conditionally complete.}
}
\]

Remaining open inputs for this branch:

1. non-wall sampling: Gate 2 mass must occur where \(|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}\);
2. polynomial anisotropy upper bound or explicit \(|u|\)-sampling;
3. correction-control theorem:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

The \(q_0\)-wall remains a separate unresolved branch.

## Agent 5 / E — Correction-row \(q_0\)-wall audit

Instruction applied: audit whether correction sectors \(C_7,C_9,\ldots\) can rescue the \(q_0\)-wall when the nominal \(H\)-leading rows vanish. 

---

# 1. Wall setup

Known rows:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=H\Gamma_G+C_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=H\Gamma_{G,2}+C_9,
\]

with

\[
H\sim -\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\qquad
H=0.
\]

Also,

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3,
\]

so

\[
\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u.
\]

Thus the nominal leading terms vanish:

\[
(H\Gamma_G)|_{q_0=0}=0,
\]

\[
(H\Gamma_{G,2})|_{q_0=0}=0.
\]

The only possible rescue inside these known rows is:

\[
C_7|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0
\quad\text{or}\quad
C_9|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0.
\]

---

# 2. Algebraic criterion for wall-live corrections

Work in the local algebra generated by

\[
q_0,\quad q_0',\quad q_0'',\quad u,\quad u',\quad \Gamma_G,\quad \Gamma_{G,2},\ldots
\]

near \(q_0=0\).

Define the wall ideal

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm wall}=(q_0).
\]

Since

\[
H\sim q_0u^3,
\]

the \(H\)-ideal satisfies

\[
(H)\subset(q_0).
\]

A correction term is wall-dead if it lies in

\[
(q_0).
\]

A correction term is wall-live if its residue modulo \((q_0)\) is nonzero:

\[
C_j \not\equiv0\pmod{(q_0)}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
C_j|_{q_0=0}
\not\equiv0
\]

as a polynomial/differential expression in the remaining variables.

Therefore the exact audit target is:

\[
\boxed{
C_7\equiv0\pmod{q_0}
\quad\text{and}\quad
C_9\equiv0\pmod{q_0}
}
\]

versus

\[
\boxed{
C_7\not\equiv0\pmod{q_0}
\quad\text{or}\quad
C_9\not\equiv0\pmod{q_0}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Candidate wall-live monomials

A correction row can rescue the wall only if it contains at least one term of type

\[
u^p,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

\[
u^p(q_0')^a,
\]

\[
u^p(u')^b,
\]

or similar, with **no factor of \(q_0\)**.

At the wall,

\[
\Gamma_G=u,
\]

so

\[
u^p\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u^{p+1}.
\]

Also,

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
=
2q_0(q_0')^2-\frac16\Gamma_G'',
\]

so at \(q_0=0\),

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}|_{q_0=0}
=
-\frac16\Gamma_G''
\]

provided \(q_0\) itself vanishes but derivatives are not forced to vanish. Since

\[
\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u,
\]

formally this gives

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}|_{q_0=0}
\sim
-\frac16 u''
\]

modulo the exact normalized-derivative convention.

Thus terms like

\[
u^p\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

may also be wall-live if \(u''\) is not constrained to vanish.

---

# 4. Correction-row expansion criterion

Write each correction row as a finite differential polynomial expansion:

\[
C_j
=
\sum_\beta c_{j,\beta}M_\beta,
\]

where each monomial \(M_\beta\) is built from

\[
q_0,\quad q_0',\quad q_0'',\quad u,\quad u',\quad u'',\quad \Gamma_G,\quad \Gamma_{G,2},\ldots
\]

Then decompose

\[
C_j=C_j^{\rm wall}+q_0C_j^{(1)},
\]

where

\[
C_j^{\rm wall}=C_j|_{q_0=0}.
\]

The wall audit has three possible outcomes.

## Case A — wall-live correction

\[
C_j^{\rm wall}\not\equiv0.
\]

Then \(A_j^{\mathfrak f}\) may rescue the wall.

## Case B — wall-dead correction

\[
C_j^{\rm wall}\equiv0.
\]

Then

\[
C_j\in(q_0),
\]

so \(A_j^{\mathfrak f}\) remains wall-dead.

## Case C — algebraic wall-live but dynamically cancelled

\[
C_j^{\rm wall}\not\equiv0,
\]

but the wall dynamics or other constraints force

\[
C_j^{\rm wall}=0
\]

on all wall solutions. Then the row is algebraically wall-live but dynamically dead.

This distinction matters: one must evaluate wall-live terms under the actual wall constraints, not just under \(q_0=0\).

---

# 5. Correction-row wall theorem if a survivor exists

Suppose a finite correction-row vector

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
=
(C_{j_1}^{\rm wall},\ldots,C_{j_s}^{\rm wall})
\]

is found, and suppose it satisfies a wall lower bound:

\[
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\ge
Q^{-A_C}
\]

whenever

\[
|q_0|\le Q^{-C_q},
\]

and

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then the wall branch is rescued by:

\[
\boxed{
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}
+
\text{Gate 2 pointwise mass}
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|\ge Q^{-A_C}.
}
\]

To make this canonical, one also needs correction stability:

\[
|\mathbf C-\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\le
\frac12|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|.
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf C|
\ge
\frac12Q^{-A_C}.
\]

This would give the needed source-robust row theorem.

---

# 6. If corrections are all wall-dead

If for every known correction row,

\[
C_j\in(q_0),
\]

then

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}
=
H\Gamma_{G,k}+C_j
\in(q_0).
\]

Since

\[
H\in(q_0),
\]

this gives

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}|_{q_0=0}=0.
\]

Therefore the projected row tower cannot rescue the wall.

The no-go theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
\forall j,\quad A_j^{\mathfrak f}\in(q_0)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
A_j^{\mathfrak f}|_{q_0=0}=0.
}
\]

In that case, the wall requires a genuinely new row or invariant not contained in the \(q_0\)-ideal.

---

# 7. Current audit verdict

Without explicit formulas for

\[
C_7,\quad C_9,\quad C_{11},\quad C_{13},\ldots,
\]

one cannot honestly certify either wall rescue or wall no-go.

The correct current status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall rescue is undecided at the correction-row level.}
}
\]

The exact computation needed is finite and algebraic:

\[
C_j \bmod (q_0)
\]

for the available correction rows.

If

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)\ne0
\]

for some \(j\), then pursue the Correction-Row Wall Theorem.

If

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)=0
\]

for all known \(j\), then the known projected/correction row architecture is wall-dead, and a new raw/ambient invariant is necessary.

---

# 8. Deliverable theorem statement

The correction-row audit should be recorded as the following dichotomy.

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Correction-Row Wall Dichotomy.}
}
\]

For each corrected row

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}
=
H\Gamma_{G,k}+C_j,
\]

exactly one of the following holds.

## Wall-live case

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)\ne0.
\]

Then, if

\[
|C_j\bmod(q_0)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

under Gate 2 wall mass, the \(q_0\)-wall branch is rescued.

## Wall-dead case

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)=0.
\]

Then

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}|_{q_0=0}=0,
\]

and this row does not rescue the wall.

Final Agent 5 recommendation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute }C_j\bmod(q_0)\text{ explicitly for }j=7,9,11,13,\ldots.
}
\]

Until that finite algebraic audit is done, the \(q_0\)-wall remains open.

## Agent 5 / E — \(q_0\)-wall source-robust row final audit

Instruction applied: audit whether the \(q_0\)-wall can be rescued by projected rows, raw/ambient rows, or correction sectors. 

---

# 1. Projected tower no-go

Known projected tower:

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}
\sim
H\Gamma_{G,k},
\]

with

\[
H=yq''_{\can}
\sim
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

so

\[
H=0.
\]

Therefore every known projected tower row satisfies, at leading order,

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\big|_{q_0=0}=0.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The known projected }H\text{-tower does not rescue the }q_0\text{-wall.}
}
\]

This is not merely a normalization issue. If the row remains \(H\)-divisible after all corrections, then it is wall-dead.

---

# 2. Raw scattering rows: required audit table

A source-robust raw row must satisfy

\[
R_{\rm raw}\big|_{q_0=0,\ u\ne0}\ne0.
\]

Because explicit raw row formulas are not presently in the prompt, the audit cannot certify a survivor. The required finite algebraic table is:

\[
R_j^{\rm raw}
=
\sum_\beta c_{j,\beta}M_{j,\beta},
\]

with each monomial \(M_{j,\beta}\) classified modulo the wall ideal

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm wall}=(q_0).
\]

For each raw row \(R_j^{\rm raw}\), compute:

\[
R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0).
\]

Classification:

| residue | status |
|---|---|
| \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\ne0\) | wall-live candidate |
| \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)=0\) | wall-dead |
| residue nonzero algebraically but forced zero by wall dynamics | dynamically dead |

A wall-live term may have form

\[
u^p,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

\[
u^p(q_0')^a,
\]

\[
u^p(u')^b,
\]

with no factor of \(q_0\) or \(H\).

At the wall,

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3
\]

becomes

\[
\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u,
\]

so

\[
u^p\Gamma_G\big|_{q_0=0}=u^{p+1}.
\]

Such a term would be source-robust.

Current verdict for raw rows:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No raw scattering row is certified wall-live until }R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\text{ is computed.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Ambient / unprojected Gram rows

The same audit applies to ambient or unprojected rows.

For each ambient row \(R_j^{\rm amb}\), compute

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0).
\]

A usable wall-live row must satisfy

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\big|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0.
\]

A row is not usable if it is divisible by any of:

\[
q_0,
\qquad
H,
\qquad
q_0u^3.
\]

The key question is whether \(H\)-divisibility is introduced by the \(\mathfrak f\)-projection or is intrinsic to the underlying row.

Two possible outcomes:

## Ambient rescue

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\notin(q_0)
\quad\text{but}\quad
A_j^{\mathfrak f}\in(q_0).
\]

Then the wall may be rescued by avoiding the projection or by using a modified invariant.

## Intrinsic wall-death

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\in(q_0)
\]

for all known rows. Then the wall is not a projection artifact.

Current verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Ambient rows remain undecided without explicit }R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0)\text{ data.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Correction sectors

Write

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
H\Gamma_G+C_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
H\Gamma_{G,2}+C_9.
\]

Since

\[
H\in(q_0),
\]

the wall behavior is controlled by

\[
C_7\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
C_9\bmod(q_0).
\]

More generally, for higher rows,

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}
=
H\Gamma_{G,k}+C_{2k+1}.
\]

The finite audit is:

\[
C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
k=3,4,5,\ldots
\]

for as many rows as are available.

## Wall-live correction

If

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)\ne0,
\]

then row \(j\) may rescue the wall.

A wall theorem would need:

\[
|C_j\bmod(q_0)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

under wall Gate 2 mass:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

## Wall-dead correction

If

\[
C_j\bmod(q_0)=0,
\]

then

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}\in(q_0)
\]

and

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}|_{q_0=0}=0.
\]

Then the row does not rescue the wall.

Current verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Correction sectors are undecided until }C_j\bmod(q_0)\text{ is explicitly computed.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Certified wall-live row status

At present:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No certified wall-live row exists.}
}
\]

What is known:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Projected leading tower rows are wall-dead.}
}
\]

What is unknown:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raw scattering rows, ambient rows, and correction sectors may still contain wall-live terms.}
}
\]

Therefore the \(q_0\)-wall should not be declared impossible, but it must be treated as a named unresolved input until the finite algebraic audit is performed.

---

# 6. Source-Robust Row theorem or named input

The desired theorem, if a row is found, is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Source-Robust Row Theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a finite row vector

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm rob}
=
(R_{j_1},\ldots,R_{j_s})
\]

such that

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm rob}\big|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0,
\]

and under wall Gate 2 mass,

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

one has

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm rob}|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If no such row exists after the finite audit, then the correct named input is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Source-Robust Row / }q_0\textbf{-Wall Resolution.}
}
\]

Statement:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}
+
\text{Gate 2 wall mass}
\Rightarrow
\text{some non-}H\text{-divisible row/invariant is non-small}.
\]

This input is currently open.

---

# 7. Final Agent 5 verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Projected tower: wall-dead.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Raw/ambient/correction rows: not yet certified.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The immediate finite algebraic task is }R_j\bmod(q_0)\text{ and }C_j\bmod(q_0).
}
\]

Until that audit is completed, the \(q_0\)-wall remains a real unresolved downstream branch.

A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,k},
\qquad
H\sim q_0u^3,
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.

At the wall:

\[
q_0=0,
\]

so

\[
H=0.

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\big|_{q_0=0}=0

at leading order.

The only possible wall rescue is a row, correction, or invariant with a term not divisible by:

\[
q_0,\qquad H,\qquad q_0u^3.

For each raw scattering row \(R_j^{\rm raw}\), compute its residue modulo the wall ideal:

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm wall}=(q_0).

Required table:

\[
R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0).

Classification rule:

| Condition | Classification |
|---|---|
| \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\ne0\) | wall-live candidate |
| \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)=0\) | wall-dead |
| \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\ne0\), but wall dynamics force it zero | dynamically wall-dead |

A wall-live raw row must contain a monomial such as:

\[
u^p,\qquad
u^p\Gamma_G,\qquad
u^p\Gamma_{G,2},\qquad
u^p(q')^a,\qquad
u^p(u')^b,
\]

with no \(q_0\)-factor.

At the wall,

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3
\]

becomes

\[
\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u.
\]

So any term

\[
u^p\Gamma_G

is wall-live and becomes

\[
u^{p+1}.
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{No raw scattering row is certified wall-live until }R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\text{ is computed.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Required finite audit table: ambient / unprojected Gram rows

For each ambient or unprojected row \(R_j^{\rm amb}\), compute:

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0).
\]

This determines whether \(H\)-divisibility is intrinsic or introduced by the \(\mathfrak f\)-projection.

Possible outcomes:

## Ambient rescue

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0)\ne0,
\]

but

\[
A_j^{\mathfrak f}\bmod(q_0)=0.

\[
R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0)=0
\]

for all known ambient rows.

}
\]

# 4. Required finite audit table: correction sectors

Known corrected rows have form:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=H\Gamma_G+C_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=H\Gamma_{G,2}+C_9.
\]

More generally:

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}=H\Gamma_{G,k}+C_{2k+1}.
\]

Since

\[
H\in(q_0),
\]

the wall behavior is controlled entirely by:

\[
C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0).
\]

Required table:

\[
C_7\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
C_9\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
C_{11}\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
C_{13}\bmod(q_0),
\ldots
\]

Classification:

| Condition | Classification |
|---|---|

| \(C_j\bmod(q_0)=0\) | correction row is wall-dead |
| \(C_j\bmod(q_0)\ne0\), but forced zero by wall equations | dynamically wall-dead |

A wall-live correction theorem would need:

\[
|C_j\bmod(q_0)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

under wall Gate 2 mass:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

---

# 5. Candidate row/invariant criterion

A usable source-robust row vector \(\mathbf R_{\rm rob}\) must satisfy:

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm rob}\big|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0.
\]

A theorem should have the form:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}
\quad\text{and}\quad
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}
\]

implies

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm rob}|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

u^p,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
u^p\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

\[
u^p(q')^a,
\]

\[
u^p(u')^b,
\]

with no \(q_0\), \(H\), or \(q_0u^3\) divisor.

| Row family | Current classification | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Projected tower \(A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,k}\) | wall-dead at leading order | \(H\sim q_0u^3\) |
| Raw scattering rows | undecided | need \(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0)\) |
| Ambient/unprojected rows | undecided | need \(R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0)\) |
| Correction rows \(C_7,C_9,\ldots\) | undecided | need \(C_j\bmod(q_0)\) |

So the current honest result is:

}

but also:

+
\text{Gate 2 wall mass}

where \(R_{\rm rob}\) is not divisible by \(q_0\), \(H\), or \(q_0u^3\).

# 8. Final Agent 5 verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The known projected leading tower is wall-dead.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{The wall branch remains unresolved pending a finite algebraic residue audit.}
}

## Agent 5 / E — D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control

Instruction applied: finalize the nondegenerate branch theorem. This branch assumes \(|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}\); it does **not** resolve the \(q_0\)-wall. 

---

# 1. Leading row vector

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Use the expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H.
\]

Then the leading row vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Assume \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\). Then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}.
\]

---

# 2. Lower bound from Gate 2 pointwise mass

Assume the nondegenerate branch

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

and pointwise Gate 2 mass

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then

\[
|u|
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0|q_0|\,|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

So if additionally

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

Alternatively, if one has the polynomial anisotropy upper bound

\[
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}
\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

then the Gate 2 pointwise mass implies

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma},
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

Thus the theorem can be stated in either of two forms:

\[
\boxed{
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
}
\]

The second form is often more convenient if global analytic-complexity bounds are already available.

---

# 3. Correction vector

The correction vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The required correction-control condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient expanded condition is

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
\left(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2\right)^{1/2}
\]

for a small fixed \(c>0\).

Correction sources that must be included:

\[
E_2\text{ and tail errors},
\]

\[
\text{projection/gauge errors},
\]

\[
\text{row normalization losses},
\]

\[
\text{lower-order row terms},
\]

\[
\text{error in }H=-q_0u^3/288.
\]

---

# 4. No cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\)

Use the vector norm

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
\left(
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2
\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Then cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\) is impossible because they are separate coordinates.

If

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
+
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
\]

and

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Therefore

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

# 5. Final theorem: nondegenerate row conversion

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control}
}
\]

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
\left(
\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2
\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
\left(
\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2
\right)^{1/2}
\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Assume the expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), and assume

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge
Q^{-C}.
}
\]

In the explicit \(|u|\)-lower-bound version,

\[
C=
C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

In the anisotropy-upper-bound version,

\[
C=
C_q+\frac32A_2+2C_\Gamma+O(1).
\]

---

# 6. Remaining open pieces

This theorem closes only the **nondegenerate branch** once its hypotheses are supplied.

Still open:

1. **Sampling into the nondegenerate branch**

   Need Gate 2 mass on the set

   \[
   |q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
   \]

   If Gate 2 mass concentrates on

   \[
   |q_0|<Q^{-C_q},
   \]

   this theorem does not apply.

2. **Correction-control theorem**

   Need

   \[
   |\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
   \le
   \frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
   \]

3. **\(q_0\)-wall branch**

   At

   \[
   q_0\approx0,
   \]

   one has

   \[
   H\sim q_0u^3\approx0,
   \]

   while

   \[
   \Gamma_G\approx u
   \]

   can remain live. This requires a separate

   \[
   \boxed{
   \text{Source-Robust Row / }q_0\text{-Wall Resolution}
   }
   \]

   theorem or a wall-live row/invariant.

Final status:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is conditionally formalized.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 does not solve D3, the }q_0\text{-wall.}
}
\]
