## Agent 5 / E — D3 correction-row wall audit + D4 correction control

Instruction applied: audit correction sectors for both the \(q_0\)-wall branch and the nondegenerate row-conversion branch. 

---

# 1. Wall-branch correction audit

Known rows:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

with

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H.
\]

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

the nominal leading term vanishes:

\[
H\Gamma_G\approx0,
\qquad
H\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0.
\]

Thus the only possible rescue inside these rows is from correction residues:

\[
C_7:=\mathcal R_7+c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
C_9:=\mathcal R_9+c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

More generally, for higher rows,

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_{2k+1}H\Gamma_{G,k}
+
C_{2k+1}.
\]

The required finite audit is:

\[
C_7\bmod(q_0),
\quad
C_9\bmod(q_0),
\quad
C_{11}\bmod(q_0),
\quad
C_{13}\bmod(q_0),
\ldots
\]

A correction row is wall-live exactly when

\[
C_{2k+1}\big|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0.
\]

Acceptable wall-live terms include:

\[
u^p,\qquad
u^p\Gamma_G,\qquad
u^p\Gamma_{G,2},\qquad
u^p(q')^a,\qquad
u^p(u')^b,
\]

with no factor of

\[
q_0,\quad H,\quad q_0u^3.
\]

Since

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3,
\]

at the wall,

\[
\Gamma_G|_{q_0=0}=u.
\]

So a correction monomial like \(u^p\Gamma_G\) is wall-live and becomes \(u^{p+1}\).

---

# 2. Wall correction dichotomy

For each corrected row

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_{2k+1}H\Gamma_{G,k}+C_{2k+1},
\]

exactly one of the following holds.

## Wall-live case

\[
C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0)\ne0.
\]

Then the wall branch may be rescued by a theorem of the form:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}
\]

implies

\[
|C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0)|\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

For a vector of wall-live corrections,

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
=
(C_{j_1}^{\rm wall},\ldots,C_{j_s}^{\rm wall}),
\]

the desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}
+
\text{Gate 2 wall mass}
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|\ge Q^{-A'}.
}
\]

This would close the wall branch, modulo canonical correction stability.

## Wall-dead case

\[
C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0)=0.
\]

Then

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\in(q_0),
\]

and

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}|_{q_0=0}=0.
\]

Such a correction row does not rescue the wall.

---

# 3. Current wall-correction status

No wall-live correction is certified from the information currently available.

The status is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D3 remains open pending the finite residue audit }C_j\bmod(q_0).
}
\]

The immediate algebraic task is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute }C_7,C_9,C_{11},C_{13},\ldots \bmod(q_0).
}
\]

If all residues vanish, then known correction sectors are wall-dead and D3 must remain a named black-box input:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Source-Robust Row / }q_0\textbf{-Wall Resolution}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Nondegenerate branch: leading vector

In the nondegenerate branch,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

define the leading row vector:

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

For \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\),

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

If pointwise Gate 2 mass holds,

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

then

\[
|u|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Thus

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0|q_0|\,|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

With

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

we get a polynomial row lower bound.

Explicit \(u\)-lower-bound version:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

Anisotropy-upper-bound version:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

---

# 5. Nondegenerate correction vector

The correction vector is:

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The correction-control theorem needed for D4 is:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient expanded condition is:

\[
|\mathcal R_H|
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

This must include all canonical correction sources:

\[
E_2,
\]

tail errors,

projection/gauge corrections,

row normalization losses,

lower-order row terms,

and the error in

\[
H=-q_0u^3/288.
\]

Using a vector norm avoids cancellation between \(A_7\) and \(A_9\):

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
(|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2)^{1/2}.
\]

If

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

# 6. Final D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control}
}
\]

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}(m_\ast)\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Assume row expansions:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\). Assume:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

In the explicit \(u\)-lower-bound formulation,

\[
C=C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

In the anisotropy-upper-bound formulation,

\[
C=C_q+\frac32A_2+2C_\Gamma+O(1).
\]

---

# 7. Final Agent 5 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is conditionally formalized.}
}
\]

Remaining D4 open pieces:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove exact row expansions and correction control.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{sample Gate 2 mass in the non-wall region }|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
}
\]

For D3:

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall correction rescue is undecided until }C_j\bmod(q_0)\text{ is computed.}
}
\]

So the downstream split remains:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nondegenerate branch: D4;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
q_0\text{-wall branch: D3.}
}

## Agent 5 / E — Final D4 nondegenerate row-conversion package

Instruction applied: finalize D4 and isolate what remains open. 

## 1. D4 scope

D4 applies only in the non-wall branch:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

It does **not** handle the \(q_0\)-wall:

\[
|q_0|<Q^{-C_q}.
\]

D4 converts pointwise Gate 2 mass into a row lower bound through the known rows:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

---

## 2. Leading vector

Define

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Assume \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\). Then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

Given pointwise Gate 2 mass,

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

we have

\[
|u|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

this becomes

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-O(1)}|u|^2.
\]

---

## 3. Two valid lower-bound formulations

### Version A — explicit source lower bound

If additionally

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

### Version B — anisotropy upper bound

If instead

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

then Gate 2 pointwise mass implies

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

Substituting gives

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

So D4 can be stated with either:

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

The anisotropy-upper-bound version is often preferable if global analytic-complexity bounds are available.

---

## 4. Correction vector

Define

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm row}
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}+\mathcal A_{\rm corr},
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The required correction-control condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le \frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient expanded condition is

\[
|\mathcal R_H|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\]

for a small fixed \(c>0\).

Correction sources that must be included:

\[
E_2,
\]

tail/truncation errors,

projection/gauge errors,

row-normalization losses,

lower-order row terms,

and the error in

\[
H=-q_0u^3/288.
\]

---

## 5. No row-to-row cancellation

Use the vector norm

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
\left(
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2
\right)^{1/2}.
\]

Then \(A_7\) and \(A_9\) cannot destructively cancel each other. If

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Consequently,

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

## 6. Final D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control.}
}
\]

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)^{1/2}
\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Assume the row expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), and assume

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le \frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

In the explicit-\(u\) formulation,

\[
C=C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

In the anisotropy-upper-bound formulation,

\[
C=C_q+\frac32A_2+2C_\Gamma+O(1).
\]

---

## 7. D4 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is conditional / closeable, not a wall solution.}
}
\]

Remaining D4 obligations:

1. prove the exact row expansions for \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f},H\);
2. prove correction control:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le \frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|;
\]

3. ensure Gate 2 mass is sampled in the non-wall region:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q};
\]

4. supply either \(|u|\)-non-smallness or a polynomial anisotropy upper bound.

The wall branch remains D3:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Source-Robust Row / }q_0\text{-Wall Resolution.}
}
\]

## Agent 5 / E — Final no-go and evidence archive

Instruction applied: produce the final proof-state archive of numerical evidence and no-go results. 

## 1. Evidence supporting O1

O1 is the **Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence** input:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The numerical evidence is strongest for this input.

| numerical branch | scope | outcome | interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| packet-frequency determinant stress | \(R\le16\), reflected/actual-like dual, \(E_2\) gates | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | no determinant collapse found |
| local \(Z_\alpha\)-amplitude v1 | 1152 rows | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | no local amplitude/jet flatness found |
| cell-sum v2 | adversarial targets, direct jet minimizers | Yellow/Orange/Red \(=0\) | no simultaneous \(Z\)-small / amplitude-small / cell-small event |
| multiblock \(K=8\) cell test | 794 rows, log-uniform support coverage | Yellow \(=0\), Orange \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | strongest support for multiblock cell theorem |
| small-\(Z_\alpha\) adversarial rows | \(|Z_\alpha|\sim10^{-15}\)–\(10^{-12}\) | amplitude, jets, and cell mass stayed large | direct support for \(Z_\alpha=0\not\Rightarrow\) flatness |

Main evidence conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)\approx0
\not\Rightarrow
\text{amplitude / jet / cell collapse}.
}
\]

This is exactly the behavior predicted by O1.

---

## 2. What the numerics do **not** prove

The numerics do not prove any of the named inputs.

Specifically, they do **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O1: Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}}
\]

because clean tests are not a proof of actual prime-log nonrecurrence.

They also do **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O2: Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence}}
\]

because the \(\mathbf M\) theorem depends on subset-product frequencies, zero-set spectral large-sieve behavior, and canonical cofactor transfer.

They do **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O3: Wronskian Angle Nonalignment}}
\]

because norm lower bounds and determinant stress tests do not imply the pointwise angle lower bound.

They do **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{S1: Singular Explicit-Formula Anti-Design Exclusion}}
\]

because the singular branch is a separate long-prime / explicit-formula anti-design problem.

They do **not** prove downstream inputs:

\[
\boxed{\text{D1--D4}}
\]

because Gate 2 propagation, derivative propagation, wall-row resolution, and correction control are analytic/algebraic downstream theorems, not tested by the O1 numerics.

---

## 3. No-go archive

| no-go / limitation | conclusion | consequence |
|---|---|---|
| phase-relaxed models | can violate cell/cofactor/angle claims | actual one-parameter prime-log arithmetic is essential |
| averaged large sieve \(\not\Rightarrow\) pointwise | isolated bad zeros can survive averaged bounds | O2 pointwise cofactor nonrecurrence remains separate |
| symbol rank \(\not\Rightarrow\) angle | rank/transversality does not force \(\langle\mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle\neq0\) | O3 remains a separate input |
| Hodge geometry | identifies \(\mathbf M\) as normal vector but gives no quantitative angle | exterior algebra gives identity, not lower bound |
| positive measure \(\not\Rightarrow\) interval | Gate 2 bad set can be dust-like | D1 needs density/Remez/Cartan input |
| finite \(C^6\) bounds alone | do not yield effective interval propagation | D1 cannot be closed from current smoothness alone |
| \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-smallness alone | does not imply \(q(q')^2\)-smallness due to cancellation with \(\Gamma_G''\) | D2 required |
| known projected rows | \(A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,k}\), \(H\sim q_0u^3\) | projected tower is \(q_0\)-wall-dead |
| \(B_2\)-only / main-only / prime-only models | not canonical without transfer | canonical transfer guardrails required |
| \(E_2\)-truncation | unsafe without derivative control | requires \(2R+1\)-derivative budget in Wronskian/cofactor contexts |

---

## 4. Current status table

| claim | evidence/status | remaining input |
|---|---|---|
| O1: Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence | numerically strong; multiblock \(K=8\) clean | actual prime-log cell nonrecurrence proof |
| O2: Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence | structurally reduced; exterior mass likely manageable | zero-set spectral large sieve / pointwise cofactor nonrecurrence |
| O3: Wronskian Angle Nonalignment | geometry clarified; no structural proof | actual-orbit Wronskian incidence theorem |
| S1: Singular Explicit-Formula Anti-Design Exclusion | separate branch identified | long-prime explicit-formula anti-design theorem |
| T1: Mild Singular Transition Control | identified as coverage bridge | explicit \(\mu=\lambda Q\) transition theorem |
| D1: Gate 2 Density/Remez Propagation | set extraction Green, propagation open | density/Remez/Cartan theorem |
| D2: Gate 2 Derivative Propagation | need \(\Gamma_G''\) or \(q(q')^2\) control | Cauchy/Bernstein/doubling theorem |
| D3: Source-Robust Row / \(q_0\)-Wall Resolution | projected tower wall-dead; raw/correction undecided | finite row residue audit or new invariant |
| D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control | conditionally formalized | exact expansions and correction-control proof |
| Canonical transfer | guardrails known | execute for each model theorem |

---

## 5. Archive summary

The project has not proved RH. It has produced a structured conditional architecture and a strong numerical archive for the \(|\mathbf z'|\) side.

The most defensible evidence statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The ordinary packet-frequency route is numerically unbroken through current stress tests.}
}
\]

The strongest supported theorem candidate is:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

But the proof currently depends on named open inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1--O3, S1, T1, and D1--D4.}
}
\]

Agent 5 final recommendation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No more broad numerics. Preserve this archive and focus proof work on D1/D2 or D3, which are currently the nearest downstream targets.}
}
\]

## Agent 5 / E — Final no-go / evidence / canonical guardrail archive

Instruction applied: produce the compact final archive for handoff: numerical evidence, no-go boundaries, and canonical constraints. 

# 1. Numerical evidence supporting O1

O1 is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}
}
\]

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf z'(m_0,\alpha)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The numerical archive supports this input most strongly.

| branch | scope | result | implication |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| packet-frequency determinant stress | \(R\le16\), reflected/actual-like dual, \(E_2\) gates | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | no determinant collapse detected |
| local \(Z_\alpha\)-amplitude v1 | 1152 rows | Yellow \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | no local amplitude/jet flatness detected |
| v2 adversarial cell-sum test | direct jet minimizers, cell diagnostics | Yellow/Orange/Red \(=0\) | \(Z_\alpha\)-near-zero did not co-occur with cell collapse |
| v3 multiblock \(K=8\) test | 794 rows, log-uniform support | Yellow \(=0\), Orange \(=0\), Red \(=0\) | strongest support for multiblock cell theorem |
| small-\(Z_\alpha\) adversarial rows | \(|Z_\alpha|\sim10^{-15}\)–\(10^{-12}\) | amplitude, jets, cell mass stayed non-small | direct evidence for \(Z_\alpha=0\not\Rightarrow\) flatness |

Main numerical conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)\approx0
\not\Rightarrow
\text{amplitude / jet / cell collapse}.
}
\]

This supports the expected behavior of O1, but it does not prove O1.

---

# 2. What the numerics do not prove

The numerical archive does **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O1: Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence}}
\]

because absence of counterexamples is not a proof of actual one-parameter prime-log nonrecurrence.

It also does **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O2: Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence}}
\]

because \(\mathbf M\) requires subset-product frequency control, zero-set spectral large-sieve input, pointwise cofactor nonrecurrence, and canonical transfer.

It does **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{O3: Wronskian Angle Nonalignment}}
\]

because norm lower bounds for \(\mathbf z'\) and \(\mathbf M\) do not imply a lower bound for their angle.

It does **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{S1: Singular Explicit-Formula Anti-Design Exclusion}}
\]

because the singular branch uses a different long-prime / explicit-formula anti-design mechanism.

It does **not** prove:

\[
\boxed{\text{T1 or D1--D4}}
\]

because transition control, Gate 2 propagation, derivative propagation, \(q_0\)-wall resolution, and row-conversion correction control are separate analytic/algebraic inputs.

---

# 3. No-go archive

| no-go / limitation | conclusion | consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Phase-relaxed models | can force artificial cell/cofactor/angle failures | actual one-parameter phase law \(e^{-im\log n}\) is essential |
| Averaged large sieve \(\not\Rightarrow\) pointwise | isolated bad zeros can survive | O2 requires pointwise cofactor nonrecurrence or bad-zero exclusion |
| Symbol rank \(\not\Rightarrow\) angle | rank does not force \(\langle \mathbf z',\mathbf M\rangle\ne0\) | O3 remains separate |
| Hodge geometry | identifies \(\mathbf M\) as a normal vector but gives no quantitative lower angle | exterior algebra gives identity, not proof |
| Positive measure \(\not\Rightarrow\) interval | bad set \(E\) can be dust-like | D1 requires Density/Remez/Cartan input |
| Finite \(C^6\) bounds alone | do not force low Remez degree or interval smallness | D1 remains named input |
| \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-smallness alone | does not imply \(q(q')^2\)-smallness due to possible cancellation with \(\Gamma_G''\) | D2 remains named input |
| Known projected rows | \(A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,k}\), \(H\sim q_0u^3\) | projected tower is \(q_0\)-wall-dead |
| Main-only / prime-only / \(B_2\)-only models | not canonical without transfer | canonical guardrails required |
| \(E_2\)-truncation | unsafe without derivative control | needs high-order derivative/tail budget |

---

# 4. Canonical guardrails

The canonical curvature is:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

The finalized background term is:

\[
B_2(t)=\theta'''(t)
=
-\frac18\Re\psi^{(2)}
\left(\frac14+\frac{it}{2}\right).
\]

Canonical guardrails:

1. **No \(B_2\)-only proof claims.**  
   \(B_2\)-only rows or cofactors are diagnostics, not Gate-level proof objects.

2. **No pure \(P_2\), pure \(D_2\), or prime-only result without transfer.**  
   These are model lemmas unless canonical transfer is proved.

3. **Mixed minors matter.**  
   Cofactors are multilinear in the \(q''_{\can}\)-jet rows, so

   \[
   \mathbf M[B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2]
   \ne
   \mathbf M[B_2]+\mathbf M[P_2]+\mathbf M[D_2]+\mathbf M[E_2].
   \]

4. **\(E_2\) requires derivative control.**  
   For Wronskian/cofactor transfer, the source block requires roughly

   \[
   0\le k\le 2R+1
   \]

   normalized derivatives.

5. **Final Wronskian incidence must be canonical.**  
   The final angle theorem must concern

   \[
   \mathbf z'_{\can}
   \quad\text{and}\quad
   \mathbf M_{\can}.
   \]

---

# 5. Final archive table

| claim | status / evidence | remaining input |
|---|---|---|
| O1: Fixed-Width Cell Nonrecurrence | numerically strong; \(K=8\) multiblock clean | actual prime-log cell nonrecurrence proof |
| O2: Cofactor-Vector Nonrecurrence | structurally reduced | zero-set spectral large sieve / pointwise cofactor nonrecurrence |
| O3: Wronskian Angle Nonalignment | geometry clarified; no structural proof | actual-orbit Wronskian incidence theorem |
| S1: Singular Explicit-Formula Anti-Design Exclusion | separate branch isolated | long-prime explicit-formula anti-design theorem |
| T1: Mild Singular Transition Control | identified but not fully formalized | \(\mu=\lambda Q\) transition theorem |
| D1: Gate 2 Density/Remez Propagation | set extraction Green; propagation open | density/Remez/Cartan or low-complexity input |
| D2: Gate 2 Derivative Propagation | need \(\Gamma_G''\) or \(q(q')^2\) control | Cauchy/Bernstein/doubling input |
| D3: Source-Robust Row / \(q_0\)-Wall Resolution | projected tower wall-dead; raw/correction undecided | finite residue audit or new invariant |
| D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control | conditional / closeable | exact expansions and correction-control proof |
| Canonical transfer | guardrails known | execute for each model theorem |

---

# 6. Final Agent 5 handoff

\[
\boxed{
\text{No more broad numerical sweeps are recommended.}
}
\]

The archive supports the ordinary packet-frequency route, especially O1, but the project remains conditional on named open inputs.

## Agent 5 / E — D4 targeted attack: correction-control closure

Instruction applied: sharpen D4, the nondegenerate row-conversion theorem, including correction-control requirements. 

# 1. D4 branch

D4 applies on the non-wall branch:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

Assume pointwise Gate 2 mass:

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Known row forms:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H.
\]

---

# 2. Leading vector

Define

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm lead}
=
\left(
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\,
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Assuming \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\),

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}.
\]

From Gate 2 pointwise mass,

\[
|u|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
c_0 |q_0|\,|u|^2 Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-A_2/2-O(1)}|u|^2.
\]

---

# 3. Two valid lower-bound versions

## Version A — explicit \(u\)-lower bound

If

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

then

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

## Version B — anisotropy upper bound

If instead

\[
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

then Gate 2 pointwise mass implies

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

Hence

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

So D4 can be stated with either an explicit \(u\)-lower bound or a polynomial anisotropy upper bound.

---

# 4. Correction vector

The correction vector is

\[
\mathcal A_{\rm corr}
=
\left(
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,\,
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9
\right).
\]

The necessary correction-control condition is

\[
\boxed{
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|
\le
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
}
\]

A sufficient scalar condition is

\[
|\mathcal R_H|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
+
|\mathcal R_7|
+
|\mathcal R_9|
\le
c\,|q_0|\,|u|^3
(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}
\]

for a sufficiently small fixed \(c>0\).

---

# 5. Required correction bounds

D4 needs explicit estimates for the following error sources:

| correction source | required bound |
|---|---|
| \(H\)-expansion error \(\mathcal R_H\) | \(|\mathcal R_H|(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)^{1/2}\le \frac14|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|\) |
| row errors \(\mathcal R_7,\mathcal R_9\) | \(|\mathcal R_7|+|\mathcal R_9|\le \frac14|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|\) |
| \(E_2\) / tail effects in \(u=q''_{\can}\) | derivative/tail bounds strong enough to imply the two inequalities above |
| projection/gauge corrections | absorbed into \(\mathcal R_7,\mathcal R_9\) |
| row normalization losses | either included in \(c_7,c_9\), or bounded by \(Q^{O(1)}\) and absorbed into exponents |
| lower-order row terms | bounded by \(\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|\) collectively |

Vector norm avoids row-to-row cancellation:

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
=
(|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|^2+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|^2)^{1/2}.
\]

If

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|,
\]

then

\[
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
|\mathcal A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

---

# 6. Sharpened D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4: Nondegenerate Row Conversion + Correction Control}
}
\]

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u(m_\ast)|^2
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
(\Gamma_G(m_\ast)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m_\ast)^2)^{1/2}
\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

Assume the row expansions

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H,
\]

with \(c_7,c_9\asymp1\), and assume

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|+
|A_9^{\mathfrak f}(m_\ast)|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

In Version A,

\[
C=C_q+2C_u+\frac{A_2}{2}+O(1).
\]

In Version B,

\[
C=C_q+\frac32A_2+2C_\Gamma+O(1).
\]

---

# 7. Is correction control local bookkeeping or a new input?

D4 is mostly local bookkeeping **once exact row expansions are available**. The remaining estimates are finite and algebraic/analytic:

\[
\mathcal R_H,\quad \mathcal R_7,\quad \mathcal R_9,
\quad E_2\text{-tail terms},
\quad\text{projection/gauge terms}.
\]

But until those expansions and bounds are explicitly written, D4 should remain:

\[
\boxed{
\text{conditional / closeable, not proved.}
}
\]

It is not a deep arithmetic input like O1–O3. It is a local row-expansion and correction-control theorem.

---

# 8. Final D4 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is structurally complete.}
}
\]

Remaining work:

1. write exact formulas for \(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f},H\);
2. prove

\[
|\mathcal A_{\rm corr}|\le\frac12|\mathcal A_{\rm lead}|;
\]

3. ensure the sampling point lies in the non-wall branch

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q};
\]

4. supply either \(|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}\) or a polynomial anisotropy upper bound.

D4 does not solve D3, the \(q_0\)-wall.

## Agent 5/E attack — sharpen D4 into a matrix-leading theorem

I’m attacking D4 directly. The useful improvement is: do **not** treat \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) and \(A_9^{\mathfrak f}\) as two isolated scalar leading terms. Treat the row vector as a **matrix acting on the Gate 2 anisotropy vector**. This is stronger and handles possible same-order cross-corrections. 

---

# 1. Replace scalar leading terms by a row-matrix form

Let

\[
\Gamma
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Instead of insisting on

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

write the usable row vector as

\[
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\\
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Since

\[
H=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+\mathcal R_H,
\]

the ideal target form is

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
q_0u^3\,L(m)\Gamma
+
\mathbf R(m),
}
\]

where \(L(m)\) is a \(2\times2\) leading coefficient matrix.

In the simplest diagonal model,

\[
L_0
=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7 & 0\\
0 & c_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

But if correction terms contribute same-order cross-couplings such as

\[
q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
\quad\text{inside }A_7^{\mathfrak f},
\]

or

\[
q_0u^3\Gamma_G
\quad\text{inside }A_9^{\mathfrak f},
\]

then those should be absorbed into \(L(m)\), not treated as errors.

This is the right canonical local form.

---

# 2. Matrix-leading D4 theorem

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2
=
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Assume the row vector has the form

\[
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R.
\]

Assume the leading matrix is nondegenerate:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
}
\]

Assume correction control:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(L)\,|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(L)\,|\Gamma|.
\]

Using Gate 2 pointwise mass,

\[
|u||\Gamma|\ge Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

So

\[
|\mathbf A_{\rm row}|
\ge
\frac12
Q^{-C_q-C_L}
|u|^2
Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

Thus, with either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

we get a row lower bound.

## Version A: explicit source lower bound

If

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A_{\rm row}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-C_L-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
}
\]

## Version B: anisotropy upper bound

If

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

then

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma},
\]

and therefore

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A_{\rm row}|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-C_L-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
}
\]

This is sharper than the previous scalar formulation because it survives same-order mixing between the \(A_7\) and \(A_9\) rows.

---

# 3. Exact correction-control target

The correction theorem should not be stated as three independent scalar inequalities. It should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
-
q_0u^3L\Gamma
=
\mathbf R,
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This absorbs:

\[
\mathcal R_H,
\quad
\mathcal R_7,
\quad
\mathcal R_9,
\quad
E_2,
\quad
\text{tail/truncation errors},
\quad
\text{projection/gauge errors},
\quad
\text{row normalization errors},
\quad
\text{lower-order row terms}.
\]

The finite algebraic task is to compute \(L\) and prove

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

If \(L\) is singular or nearly singular, D4 fails even in the non-wall branch.

---

# 4. Why this matters

The previous scalar-leading theorem assumed the leading vector was essentially

\[
q_0u^3
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
c_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

But the true expansion could be

\[
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
q_0u^3
\begin{pmatrix}
a & b\\
c & d
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
+
\mathbf R.
\]

Then D4 requires

\[
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
a & b\\
c & d
\end{pmatrix}
\not\approx0.
\]

So the exact local algebraic audit for D4 is:

\[
\boxed{
L=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\frac{\partial(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})}
{\partial(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})}
\quad
\text{mod higher-order errors}.
}
\]

Then prove:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
}
\]

This is the real correction-control object.

---

# 5. What this attack closes

This gives a complete D4 theorem modulo finite algebra:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 reduces to proving }
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
\quad
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\quad
|\mathbf R|\le\frac12|q_0u^3|\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

That is local, finite, and checkable.

D4 is therefore not a deep zeta-arithmetic input. It is a row-expansion / matrix-nondegeneracy / correction-control theorem.

---

# 6. What this attack does **not** close

It does not solve D3.

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

the entire leading matrix term

\[
q_0u^3L\Gamma
\]

vanishes.

Thus D3 still requires either:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\rm rob}|_{q_0=0,u\ne0}\ne0
}
\]

or a wall-exclusion theorem.

The D3 residue audit remains:

\[
R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
C_{2k+1}\bmod(q_0).
\]

---

# 7. Final result of this attack

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 can be sharpened to a finite matrix-nondegeneracy theorem.}
}
\]

The exact theorem to prove next is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_{\rm row}
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
\qquad
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\qquad
|\mathbf R|\le\frac12|q_0u^3|\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}
+
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}
+
(|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}\ \text{or}\ |\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma})
\Rightarrow
|A_7^{\mathfrak f}|+|A_9^{\mathfrak f}|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the strongest current form of D4.

I’ll push D4 one level further into a finite algebra problem, and also extract a sharper D3 wall test from the same framework.

## Continuing the attack — \(q_0\)-adic filtration for D4 and D3

The next useful move is to put the downstream row problem into a **\(q_0\)-adic normal form**. This turns both D4 and D3 into finite residue computations rather than vague correction-control language.

---

# 1. Work in a local differential algebra

Let

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Introduce a local differential algebra

\[
\mathcal R
=
\mathbb C[q_0,q_0',q_0'',\ldots,u,u',u'',\ldots,\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2},\ldots]
\]

near the downstream sampling point.

Define the wall ideal

\[
\mathfrak I=(q_0).
\]

The \(q_0\)-wall is the quotient

\[
\mathcal R/\mathfrak I.
\]

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3
\]

becomes

\[
\Gamma_G\equiv u
\pmod{q_0}.
\]

The known \(H\)-factor satisfies

\[
H
=
-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}
+
\mathcal R_H.
\]

So the leading \(H\)-tower rows lie in the ideal

\[
(q_0u^3)\subset(q_0).
\]

Thus the projected leading tower is wall-dead unless a correction has nonzero residue modulo \(q_0\).

---

# 2. Define the \(q_0\)-adic order of a row

For any row or invariant \(R\in\mathcal R\), define

\[
\nu_{q_0}(R)
=
\max\{k:R\in(q_0)^k\}.
\]

Then:

- wall-live means

\[
\nu_{q_0}(R)=0;
\]

- wall-dead means

\[
\nu_{q_0}(R)\ge1.
\]

For the known projected leading rows,

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f,\lead}
=
H\Gamma_{G,k},
\]

we have

\[
\nu_{q_0}(A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f,\lead})\ge1.
\]

So they are wall-dead.

This gives a clean D3 criterion:

\[
\boxed{
\exists R_{\rm rob}:\nu_{q_0}(R_{\rm rob})=0
}
\]

is exactly the source-robust-row condition.

---

# 3. D4: extract the first nonzero non-wall term

Now localize away from the wall by assuming

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

For D4, the relevant filtration is not just \(q_0\)-adic but

\[
q_0u^3\Gamma\text{-adic},
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\\
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The correct leading extraction is

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
\mathbf R,
}
\]

where \(L\) is a \(2\times2\) local coefficient matrix.

The sharp D4 condition is

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf A|
\ge
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
\]

Using

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A|
\ge
\frac12 Q^{-C_q-C_L}|u|^2Q^{-A_2/2}.
\]

So D4 closes if either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

---

# 4. What is \(L\)?

By definition,

\[
L
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\frac{\partial \mathbf A_{\lead}}{\partial \Gamma}
\]

at leading order.

If the known leading rows are exactly

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f,\lead}=c_7H\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f,\lead}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

and

\[
H_{\lead}=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\]

then

\[
L_{\lead}
=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7 & 0\\
0 & c_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Hence

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L_{\lead})
\asymp 1
\]

provided

\[
c_7,c_9\asymp1.
\]

So the leading diagonal part is nondegenerate. The only D4 risks are:

1. exact row normalizations make \(c_7\) or \(c_9\) vanish;
2. same-order corrections alter \(L\) into a nearly singular matrix;
3. non-leading corrections are too large.

Thus the finite D4 audit is:

\[
\boxed{
L=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7 & 0\\
0 & c_9
\end{pmatrix}
+
L_{\corr},
\qquad
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
}
\]

If

\[
\|L_{\corr}\|\le \frac12\sigma_{\min}(L_{\lead}),
\]

then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge \frac12\sigma_{\min}(L_{\lead})
\asymp1.
\]

This is a finite coefficient check.

---

# 5. D3: wall residue of the same row vector

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

the leading matrix term vanishes:

\[
q_0u^3L\Gamma=0.
\]

Thus

\[
\mathbf A\bmod(q_0)
=
\mathbf R\bmod(q_0).
\]

So the same correction vector controls the wall.

D3 is therefore exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf R\bmod(q_0)\ne0
}
\]

under wall Gate 2 mass.

More concretely:

\[
\mathbf A\bmod(q_0)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\bmod(q_0)\\
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\bmod(q_0)
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
C_7\bmod(q_0)\\
C_9\bmod(q_0)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

If

\[
(C_7,C_9)\bmod(q_0)\ne0,
\]

then D3 may be rescued inside the same rows.

If

\[
(C_7,C_9)\bmod(q_0)=0,
\]

then \(A_7,A_9\) are wall-dead and one must check

\[
C_{11}\bmod(q_0),\quad C_{13}\bmod(q_0),\ldots
\]

or raw/ambient rows.

---

# 6. Stronger dichotomy: D3/D4 from one expansion

The row vector has one local expansion:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
\mathbf C_{\wall}
+
q_0\mathbf C_1
+
\mathbf R_{\higher},
}
\]

where

\[
\mathbf C_{\wall}
=
\mathbf A\bmod(q_0).
\]

Then:

## Non-wall branch

If

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

and

\[
q_0u^3L\Gamma
\]

dominates

\[
\mathbf C_{\wall}+q_0\mathbf C_1+\mathbf R_{\higher},
\]

then D4 applies.

## Wall branch

If

\[
q_0\approx0,
\]

then

\[
q_0u^3L\Gamma
\]

dies, and one needs

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf C_{\wall}|\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

under Gate 2 wall mass.

Thus D3 and D4 are two faces of the same expansion.

The finite algebraic goal is to compute:

\[
\boxed{
L,\qquad \mathbf C_{\wall}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Candidate theorem: row normal form

The best formal statement is:

**Row Normal Form Theorem.**  
For the row vector

\[
\mathbf A=(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}),
\]

there exist a \(2\times2\) matrix \(L\), a wall vector \(\mathbf C_{\wall}\), and a remainder \(\mathbf R_{\higher}\) such that

\[
\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
+
\mathbf C_{\wall}
+
q_0\mathbf C_1
+
\mathbf R_{\higher}.
\]

Moreover:

\[
L=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
0&c_9
\end{pmatrix}
+
L_{\corr}.
\]

If

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

then the non-wall branch is handled by D4.

If

\[
\mathbf C_{\wall}\ne0
\]

and satisfies a wall lower bound under Gate 2 wall mass, then the wall branch is handled by D3.

If

\[
\mathbf C_{\wall}=0
\]

for all available rows, the known row system is wall-dead.

---

# 8. New actionable attack

The immediate next attack is finite and concrete:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute the row normal form for }
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
\text{ and, if needed, }A_{11}^{\mathfrak f},A_{13}^{\mathfrak f}.
}
\]

Specifically compute:

\[
L,
\]

\[
\det L,
\]

\[
\mathbf C_{\wall}=\mathbf A\bmod(q_0),
\]

\[
\mathbf R_{\higher}.
\]

Then classify:

| outcome | meaning |
|---|---|
| \(\det L\not\approx0\), \(\mathbf C_{\wall}\ne0\) | both non-wall and wall branches may close |
| \(\det L\not\approx0\), \(\mathbf C_{\wall}=0\) | D4 closes, D3 remains open |
| \(\det L\approx0\), \(\mathbf C_{\wall}\ne0\) | wall may close, non-wall row pair insufficient |
| \(\det L\approx0\), \(\mathbf C_{\wall}=0\) | this row pair is inadequate |

---

# 9. Conclusion of the attack

The strongest progress is this reduction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D3 and D4 reduce to the row normal form }
\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
\mathbf C_{\wall}
+
q_0\mathbf C_1
+
\mathbf R_{\higher}.
}
\]

D4 needs:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L}
}
\]

and error dominance.

D3 needs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C_{\wall}\ne0
}
\]

with a lower bound under Gate 2 wall mass.

This is now a finite symbolic expansion problem, not a global analytic or zeta-arithmetic problem.

I’ll continue by attacking the gap between D3 and D4: even if the wall residue and non-wall leading matrix are good separately, there is still a near-wall cancellation regime that must be controlled.

_Thought for 26s_

## Continued attack — close the near-wall gap with a \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil theorem

The previous attack reduced D3/D4 to the normal form

\[
\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
+
q_0\mathbf C_1
+
\mathbf R_{\rm higher},
\qquad
\Gamma=
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

That was useful, but it leaves one subtle gap: even if the wall term and non-wall leading term are each good separately, there can be a **near-wall cancellation regime**

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
\approx
-q_0u^3L\Gamma.
\]

So the next attack is to replace the two-branch D3/D4 split by a single row-pencil nonvanishing theorem. This is still finite algebra, not global zeta arithmetic. 

---

# 1. The near-wall obstruction

D4 controls the region where

\[
|q_0|
\]

is not too small and

\[
q_0u^3L\Gamma
\]

dominates all corrections.

D3 controls the exact wall if

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
=
\mathbf A\bmod(q_0)
\]

is nonzero.

But between these two regimes there may be a cancellation scale:

\[
|q_0|
\sim
\frac{|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|}{|u|^3|L\Gamma|}.
\]

At that scale,

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\Gamma
\]

could be small even though both terms are individually non-small.

Therefore the true downstream row theorem should not be split only into:

\[
q_0=0
\quad\text{versus}\quad
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

It should control the full finite polynomial row map in \(q_0\).

---

# 2. Use an augmented row vector

Do not use only

\[
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f}).
\]

Use a finite row vector

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f},A_{11}^{\mathfrak f},A_{13}^{\mathfrak f},\ldots,A_N^{\mathfrak f})
\]

or the corresponding raw/ambient/correction rows if projected rows remain wall-dead.

Then compute the \(q_0\)-adic expansion:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf C_0(Y)
+
q_0\mathbf C_1(Y)
+
q_0^2\mathbf C_2(Y)
+
\cdots
+
q_0^K\mathbf C_K(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K.
}
\]

Here \(Y\) denotes the remaining local variables:

\[
Y=(u,u',u'',q',q'',\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2},\Gamma_G'',\ldots).
\]

The wall residue is

\[
\mathbf C_0(Y)=\mathbf R_N\bmod(q_0).
\]

The non-wall leading D4 term appears inside

\[
\mathbf C_1(Y),
\]

with the component

\[
u^3L\Gamma.
\]

---

# 3. The unified theorem to prove

The right theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\substack{|q_0|\le Q^{C}\\ Y\in\mathcal S_{\rm Gate2}}}
\left|
\sum_{r=0}^K q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal S_{\rm Gate2}\) is the local Gate 2 admissible set, for example:

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

plus polynomial upper bounds on the local variables.

This theorem simultaneously covers:

- wall:

\[
q_0=0;
\]

- non-wall:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q};
\]

- near-wall cancellation:

\[
\mathbf C_0\approx -q_0\mathbf C_1.
\]

Call it:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(q_0\)-Adic Row-Pencil Nonvanishing.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Finite algebra criterion

A finite algebraic sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{r=0}^K \tau^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\ne 0
}
\]

for all

\[
(\tau,Y)
\]

in the normalized compact admissible set

\[
\mathcal K
=
\{(\tau,Y): Y\in\mathcal S_{\rm Gate2}^{\rm norm},\ |\tau|\le \tau_{\max}\}.
\]

If this polynomial/vector map has no zero on \(\mathcal K\), then a quantitative Łojasiewicz inequality gives a power lower bound:

\[
\left|
\sum_{r=0}^K \tau^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\right|
\ge
c\,\operatorname{dist}((\tau,Y),Z)^\beta.
\]

If the zero set \(Z\) is disjoint from \(\mathcal K\) by a polynomial margin, this yields:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\sum_{r=0}^K q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

So the finite symbolic task becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{show the row-pencil has no common zero on the Gate 2 admissible variety.}
}
\]

This is much better than searching row-by-row.

---

# 5. How cancellation can fail

For a two-row vector, near-wall cancellation can occur if

\[
\mathbf C_0(Y)
\]

is nearly colinear with

\[
u^3L\Gamma.
\]

But if we add more rows, cancellation requires

\[
\mathbf C_0(Y),\mathbf C_1(Y),\ldots,\mathbf C_K(Y)
\]

to satisfy a common polynomial relation.

For example, if

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf C_0+q_0\mathbf C_1
\]

and \(\mathbf C_0,\mathbf C_1\in\mathbb C^s\), then there exists a \(\tau\) with

\[
\mathbf C_0+\tau\mathbf C_1=0
\]

only if

\[
\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf C_0,\mathbf C_1)\le1
\]

and the componentwise ratios match.

So a useful finite test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C_0(Y)\wedge \mathbf C_1(Y)\ne0
}
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible set.

If this wedge is polynomially nonzero, then no scalar \(q_0\) can cancel the wall and first non-wall terms simultaneously.

This is the first concrete algebraic invariant to compute.

---

# 6. Proposed attack invariant

Compute:

\[
\Delta_{01}(Y)
=
|\mathbf C_0(Y)|^2|\mathbf C_1(Y)|^2
-
|\langle \mathbf C_0(Y),\mathbf C_1(Y)\rangle|^2.
\]

This is the squared wedge norm:

\[
\Delta_{01}=|\mathbf C_0\wedge \mathbf C_1|^2.
\]

If

\[
\boxed{
\Delta_{01}(Y)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on the wall/Gate 2 admissible set, then the near-wall cancellation is impossible.

If

\[
\Delta_{01}\equiv0,
\]

then \(\mathbf C_0\) and \(\mathbf C_1\) are always colinear, and we must include higher coefficients:

\[
\mathbf C_2,\mathbf C_3,\ldots
\]

or add more rows.

The next invariant is the rank of the coefficient matrix:

\[
\mathcal C(Y)
=
[\mathbf C_0(Y)\ \mathbf C_1(Y)\ \cdots\ \mathbf C_K(Y)].
\]

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}\mathcal C(Y)\ge2
}
\]

at minimum; ideally enough rank to rule out a polynomial \(\tau\)-root.

---

# 7. D3/D4 unified normal-form theorem

A stronger theorem than separate D3 and D4 is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Downstream Row-Pencil Theorem.}
}
\]

Let \(\mathbf R_N\) be a finite row vector with normal form

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\sum_{r=0}^K q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K.
\]

Assume:

1. Gate 2 admissibility:

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

2. Polynomial upper bounds on all local variables.

3. Row-pencil nonvanishing:

\[
\left|
\sum_{r=0}^K q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A_R}
\]

on the admissible set.

4. Error control:

\[
|\mathbf E_K|\le \frac12 Q^{-A_R}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-A_R}/2.
}
\]

This single theorem replaces D3 and D4.

---

# 8. Practical symbolic protocol

The next symbolic agent should compute:

## Step 1

For each candidate row:

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots
\]

compute

\[
A_j=\sum_{r=0}^K q_0^r C_{j,r}(Y)+O(q_0^{K+1}).
\]

## Step 2

Form row vectors:

\[
\mathbf C_r=(C_{7,r},C_{9,r},C_{11,r},C_{13,r},\ldots).
\]

## Step 3

Compute:

\[
\mathbf C_0,
\qquad
\mathbf C_1,
\qquad
\Delta_{01}=|\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1|^2.
\]

## Step 4

Classify:

| outcome | meaning |
|---|---|
| \(\mathbf C_0\ne0\), \(\Delta_{01}\ge Q^{-A}\) | wall and near-wall controlled |
| \(\mathbf C_0=0\), \(\mathbf C_1\) nondegenerate | D4 only; wall open |
| \(\mathbf C_0\ne0\), \(\Delta_{01}=0\) | possible near-wall cancellation; add rows/orders |
| \(\mathbf C_0=0\), \(\mathbf C_1\) degenerate | row family inadequate |

---

# 9. What this attack achieves

It improves the downstream state from:

\[
\text{D3 wall branch}
\quad+\quad
\text{D4 non-wall branch}
\]

to one finite algebraic problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute a \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil and prove it has no small root on the Gate 2 admissible set.}
}
\]

This is probably the most precise current downstream attack.

The new named theorem, if needed, is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{\(q_0\)-Adic Row-Pencil Nonvanishing.}
}
\]

It is stronger and cleaner than separate D3/D4.

## Continued attack — reduce D3/D4 to a vector-polynomial common-root test

The previous row-pencil normal form was:

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\sum_{r=0}^{K}q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K,
\]

where \(Y\) collects the remaining local variables:

\[
Y=(u,u',u'',q',q'',\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2},\ldots).
\]

The new goal is to remove the last ambiguity: **near-wall cancellation**. The correct obstruction is not simply whether \(\mathbf C_0\neq0\) or whether \(L\) is nonsingular. It is whether the vector polynomial

\[
P_Y(\tau)=\sum_{r=0}^{K}\tau^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\]

can have a small root in \(\tau=q_0\) on the Gate 2 admissible set.

So the downstream wall/non-wall problem is now a **finite vector-polynomial common-root exclusion problem**.

---

# 1. Normalize the wall variables

At the wall,

\[
q_0=0,
\]

we have

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3
\]

so

\[
\Gamma_G\equiv u\pmod{q_0}.
\]

Thus on the exact wall,

\[
\Gamma_G=u.
\]

If Gate 2 pointwise mass holds,

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

then on the exact wall this implies at least

\[
|u|^2(u^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

In particular, if \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) is not huge, this forces

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/4-O(1)}.
\]

More generally, under a polynomial anisotropy upper bound

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\qquad
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\]

Gate 2 pointwise mass gives

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

This matters because it prevents wall residues such as \(u^p\), \(u^p\Gamma_G\), or \(u^p\Gamma_{G,2}\) from being automatically tiny.

---

# 2. Replace branch split by normalized row polynomial

Let

\[
\tau=q_0.
\]

For a finite row vector

\[
\mathbf R_N=(A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,A_N),
\]

write the \(q_0\)-adic expansion:

\[
\mathbf R_N(\tau,Y)
=
\mathbf C_0(Y)
+
\tau\mathbf C_1(Y)
+
\tau^2\mathbf C_2(Y)
+\cdots+
\tau^K\mathbf C_K(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K.
\]

Then:

- wall branch is \(\tau=0\);
- non-wall branch is \(|\tau|\ge Q^{-C_q}\);
- near-wall branch is the cancellation regime

\[
\mathbf C_0(Y)+\tau\mathbf C_1(Y)+\cdots\approx0.
\]

Thus D3 and D4 are unified by:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\tau,Y}
\left|
\mathbf C_0(Y)+\tau\mathbf C_1(Y)+\cdots+\tau^K\mathbf C_K(Y)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible set.

---

# 3. Gate 2 admissible set

Define the local admissible set

\[
\mathcal S_{\rm G2}
\]

by:

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}
\quad\text{or}\quad
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

plus polynomial upper bounds on the relevant local variables:

\[
|u^{(j)}|,\ |q^{(j)}|,\ |\Gamma_G^{(j)}|,\ |\Gamma_{G,2}^{(j)}|
\le Q^{C_j}.
\]

Also include the structural identity

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q_0+2q_0^3.
\]

Then the desired row theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
(\tau,Y)\in\mathcal S_{\rm G2}
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf R_N(\tau,Y)|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is now a finite algebraic/analytic local theorem.

---

# 4. First-order row-pencil test

Start with the truncation

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf C_0+\tau\mathbf C_1+\text{higher}.
\]

Near-wall cancellation requires

\[
\mathbf C_0+\tau\mathbf C_1\approx0.
\]

For such a \(\tau\) to exist, the two vectors must be nearly colinear.

Therefore compute the wedge invariant:

\[
\Delta_{01}
=
|\mathbf C_0|^2|\mathbf C_1|^2
-
|\langle\mathbf C_0,\mathbf C_1\rangle|^2.
\]

If

\[
\boxed{
\Delta_{01}\ge Q^{-A}
|\mathbf C_0|^2|\mathbf C_1|^2
}
\]

on \(\mathcal S_{\rm G2}\), then \(\mathbf C_0\) and \(\mathbf C_1\) are separated by a polynomial angle, so no scalar \(\tau\) can cancel them.

Then the row lower bound follows from

\[
\inf_\tau|\mathbf C_0+\tau\mathbf C_1|
=
\frac{|\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1|}{|\mathbf C_1|}
\]

up to constants, assuming \(\mathbf C_1\neq0\).

So a sufficient first-order theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1|
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible set.

---

# 5. If \(\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1=0\)

If

\[
\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1\equiv0,
\]

then first-order row-pencil cancellation is structurally possible.

Then include higher coefficients:

\[
\mathbf C_2,\mathbf C_3,\ldots.
\]

The vector polynomial

\[
P_Y(\tau)=\sum_{r=0}^{K}\tau^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\]

has a root only if all scalar component polynomials have a common root.

Thus the exact algebraic obstruction is:

\[
\gcd(P_{Y,1}(\tau),\ldots,P_{Y,s}(\tau))\neq1.
\]

Equivalently, the multivariate resultant or subresultant system vanishes.

So the finite symbolic test is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Res}_\tau(P_{Y,1},\ldots,P_{Y,s})\ne0
}
\]

or, more robustly, a lower bound for the smallest singular value of the Sylvester/subresultant matrix.

This gives the higher-order row-pencil nonvanishing condition.

---

# 6. Quantitative common-root exclusion

Let

\[
P_Y(\tau)
=
(P_{Y,1}(\tau),\ldots,P_{Y,s}(\tau)).
\]

Define the minimal row-pencil value

\[
m(Y)=\inf_{|\tau|\le Q^{C_\tau}}|P_Y(\tau)|.
\]

Then D3/D4 unify into:

\[
\boxed{
m(Y)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad
\text{for all }Y\in\mathcal S_{\rm G2}.
}
\]

A sufficient finite-dimensional certificate is:

1. coefficient bounds:

\[
\|\mathbf C_r(Y)\|\le Q^{C};
\]

2. subresultant lower bound:

\[
\sigma_{\min}\operatorname{Sylv}(P_{Y,1},\ldots,P_{Y,s})
\ge Q^{-C};
\]

3. admissible variable bounds.

Then standard quantitative polynomial root-separation gives

\[
m(Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is a clean local algebraic path.

---

# 7. Relation to D4

If

\[
\mathbf C_0=0
\]

and

\[
\mathbf C_1(Y)=u^3L(Y)\Gamma,
\]

then

\[
P_Y(\tau)=\tau u^3L\Gamma+\cdots.
\]

This recovers D4.

D4 then needs:

\[
|\tau|=|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and correction dominance.

Thus D4 is just the case where the row-pencil has no wall term:

\[
\mathbf C_0=0.
\]

---

# 8. Relation to D3

If

\[
\mathbf C_0\ne0,
\]

then exact wall is potentially controlled.

But D3 needs more than \(\mathbf C_0\neq0\). It needs:

\[
|\mathbf C_0(Y)|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

under wall Gate 2 mass, and near-wall protection:

\[
\inf_\tau|P_Y(\tau)|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So D3 is not merely “find a wall-live residue.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a wall-live residue and prove it cannot be cancelled by nearby }q_0\text{-terms.}
}
\]

This is why the row-pencil formulation is stronger than the old D3/D4 split.

---

# 9. Practical next computation

For available rows, compute:

\[
A_j(q_0,Y)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{K}q_0^r C_{j,r}(Y),
\qquad
j=7,9,11,13,\ldots.
\]

Then form:

\[
\mathbf C_r=(C_{7,r},C_{9,r},C_{11,r},C_{13,r},\ldots).
\]

Run the following finite tests.

## Test 1 — wall residue

\[
\mathbf C_0\stackrel{?}{=}0.
\]

## Test 2 — first-order wedge

\[
\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1\stackrel{?}{=}0.
\]

## Test 3 — leading D4 matrix

If

\[
\mathbf C_0=0,
\]

extract

\[
\mathbf C_1=u^3L\Gamma+\text{other},
\]

and compute

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L).
\]

## Test 4 — higher common-root obstruction

If first-order wedge degenerates, compute subresultants of the vector polynomial

\[
P_Y(\tau).
\]

---

# 10. New theorem statement

The better downstream row theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Downstream \(q_0\)-Adic Row-Pencil Nonvanishing.}
}
\]

There exists a finite row vector \(\mathbf R_N\) such that, after writing

\[
\mathbf R_N(q_0,Y)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{K}q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K,
\]

one has

\[
\inf_{(q_0,Y)\in\mathcal S_{\rm G2}}
\left|
\sum_{r=0}^{K}q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf E_K|\le \frac12Q^{-A}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2 pointwise mass}
\Rightarrow
\text{row lower bound}.
}
\]

This theorem subsumes:

\[
\text{D3 Source-Robust Row}
\]

and

\[
\text{D4 Nondegenerate Row Conversion}.
\]

---

# 11. Net progress

The attack has improved the downstream layer:

Old split:

\[
\text{D3 wall branch}
\quad+\quad
\text{D4 non-wall branch}.
\]

New unified target:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute and prove nonvanishing of a finite }q_0\text{-adic row pencil.}
}
\]

The wall, non-wall, and near-wall cancellation regimes are all included.

The next concrete action is symbolic:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute }C_{j,r}\text{ for }j=7,9,11,13,\ldots
\text{ and }r=0,1,2,\ldots
}
\]

then test:

\[
\mathbf C_0,\quad
\mathbf C_0\wedge\mathbf C_1,\quad
\sigma_{\min}(L),\quad
\text{subresultants of }P_Y(\tau).
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-27 09:59:02)

I’ll continue by reducing the row-pencil variables using the wall identities. The aim is to shrink the symbolic audit from “all local variables” to a small quotient algebra at \(q_0=0\).

_Thought for 23s_

## Continuing the attack — wall quotient algebra and a smaller row-penciling target

The row-pencil theorem was:

\[
\mathbf R_N(q_0,Y)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{K} q_0^r\mathbf C_r(Y)
+
\mathbf E_K.
\]

The remaining symbolic problem was too large because \(Y\) included many local variables. The next reduction is to compute the **wall quotient algebra**:

\[
\mathcal R/(q_0).
\]

This tells us exactly what survives at the \(q_0\)-wall and gives a smaller target for D3.

---

# 1. Work modulo \(q_0\)

Write

\[
q=q_0,
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Use

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q+2q^3.
\]

Modulo \(q\),

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G\equiv u
\pmod q.
}
\]

Differentiate:

\[
\Gamma_G'
=
u'-6q'+6q^2q',
\]

so

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G'\equiv u'-6q'
\pmod q.
}
\]

Differentiate again:

\[
\Gamma_G''
=
u''-6q''+12q(q')^2+6q^2q''.
\]

If \(u=q''_{\can}\) is the normalized second derivative of \(q\), then modulo \(q\),

\[
q''\equiv u,
\]

so

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G''
\equiv u''-6u
\pmod q.
}
\]

Now use

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
=
2q(q')^2-\frac16\Gamma_G''.
\]

Modulo \(q\), the first term dies:

\[
2q(q')^2\equiv0\pmod q.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{G,2}
\equiv
-\frac16(u''-6u)
=
u-\frac16u''
\pmod q.
}
\]

So at the \(q_0\)-wall the Gate 2 anisotropy vector reduces to

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{\rm wall}
=
\left(
u,\,
u-\frac16u''
\right).
}
\]

This is the key simplification.

---

# 2. Wall Gate 2 mass in quotient form

Gate 2 pointwise mass is

\[
|u|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Modulo \(q\), this becomes the wall condition

\[
\boxed{
|u|^2
\left(
|u|^2+
\left|u-\frac16u''\right|^2
\right)
\ge Q^{-A_2}.
}
\]

Thus a wall-live row does not need to see all variables. It only needs to see

\[
u
\quad\text{or}\quad
u-\frac16u''
\]

or a polynomial consequence of these.

So the D3 wall target becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a row residue nonzero on }
|u|^2\left(|u|^2+\left|u-\frac16u''\right|^2\right)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
}
\]

This is much narrower than “find anything not divisible by \(q_0\).”

---

# 3. Define the wall anisotropy ideal

Let

\[
\eta
=
u-\frac16u''.
\]

Then the wall Gate 2 vector is

\[
W=(u,\eta).
\]

Define the wall anisotropy ideal

\[
\mathfrak I_{\rm G2,wall}
=
(u,\eta).
\]

A wall row residue \(\mathbf C_{\rm wall}\) is useful if it is not in a high power of this ideal, and preferably has a linear or low-degree component in \(u,\eta\).

The strongest possible wall rescue is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
=
M_{\rm wall}
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
\eta
\end{pmatrix}
+
\text{higher order},
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(M_{\rm wall})\ge Q^{-C}.}
\]

Then wall Gate 2 mass gives a row lower bound.

Indeed, if

\[
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\ge
Q^{-C_M}|W|,
\]

then

\[
|u|^2|W|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}
\]

implies

\[
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\ge
Q^{-C_M}\frac{Q^{-A_2/2}}{|u|}.
\]

This still needs an upper bound on \(|u|\), say

\[
|u|\le Q^{C_u^+}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\ge
Q^{-A_2/2-C_M-C_u^+}.
}
\]

So a wall-live row theorem can be stated with upper complexity bounds, not lower bounds.

---

# 4. Stronger wall criterion

The old D3 criterion was:

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}\ne0.
\]

That is too weak.

The sharper criterion is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
\notin
\mathfrak I_{\rm G2,wall}^{N}
}
\]

for small \(N\), preferably \(N=1\) or \(2\).

If

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}
\in (u,\eta)^N,
\]

then Gate 2 mass gives

\[
|\mathbf C_{\rm wall}|
\sim |W|^N,
\]

which may still be usable if \(N\) is fixed and \(|u|\), \(|W|\) are polynomially bounded.

But if \(\mathbf C_{\rm wall}\) vanishes to high or unbounded order on the wall Gate 2 variety, it is useless.

So the finite audit should compute not only

\[
\mathbf C_{\rm wall}\bmod q,
\]

but also its order in the wall Gate 2 ideal:

\[
\boxed{
\nu_{\rm wall}(\mathbf C_{\rm wall})
=
\max\{N:\mathbf C_{\rm wall}\in(u,\eta)^N\}.
}
\]

A row is robust if

\[
\nu_{\rm wall}(\mathbf C_{\rm wall})=O(1)
\]

and its leading wall coefficient is nondegenerate.

---

# 5. Refined row-pencil normal form

The \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil should be refined as

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf C_0(u,\eta,\ldots)
+
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
q_0\mathbf C_1^{\rm rem}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm higher}.
\]

Now decompose the wall term:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf C_0
=
M_{\rm wall}
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
\eta
\end{pmatrix}
+
\mathbf C_{0,\ge2}.
}
\]

Then the unified downstream row theorem becomes:

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
M_{\rm wall}W
+
q_0u^3L\Gamma
+
\text{higher terms}.
\]

This captures:

- wall branch through \(M_{\rm wall}W\);
- non-wall branch through \(q_0u^3L\Gamma\);
- near-wall branch through the pencil

\[
M_{\rm wall}W+q_0u^3L\Gamma.
\]

---

# 6. Near-wall cancellation in the refined form

Near-wall cancellation requires

\[
M_{\rm wall}W
\approx
-q_0u^3L\Gamma.
\]

But on the wall,

\[
\Gamma\equiv W.
\]

Near the wall,

\[
\Gamma
=
W
+
O(q_0).
\]

So the leading near-wall pencil is approximately

\[
\boxed{
\left(M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\right)W.
}
\]

Thus cancellation requires the matrix pencil

\[
M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L
\]

to be nearly singular.

This is much cleaner.

The unified D3/D4 condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}
\left(
M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on the admissible range.

If this holds, then

\[
|\mathbf R_N|
\gtrsim
Q^{-C}|W|
\]

up to higher-order errors.

Together with

\[
|u|^2|W|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and an upper bound

\[
|u|\le Q^{C_u^+},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-C-A_2/2-C_u^+}.
\]

This gives a clean row lower bound across wall, near-wall, and non-wall.

---

# 7. Finite matrix-pencil theorem

The finite theorem to prove is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Wall/Nonwall Matrix-Pencil Nondegeneracy.}
}
\]

There exists a finite row vector \(\mathbf R_N\) such that

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\left(M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\right)
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
u-\frac16u''
\end{pmatrix}
+
\mathbf E,
\]

where

\[
\sigma_{\min}
\left(M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\right)
\ge Q^{-C}
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible set, and

\[
|\mathbf E|
\le
\frac12
\sigma_{\min}
\left(M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\right)
\left|
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
u-\frac16u''
\end{pmatrix}
\right|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2 pointwise mass}
\Rightarrow
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This theorem subsumes:

- D3 wall resolution;
- D4 nondegenerate row conversion;
- near-wall cancellation control.

---

# 8. What must be computed

For rows

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots
\]

compute:

## Wall matrix

\[
M_{\rm wall}
=
\frac{\partial \mathbf C_0}
{\partial(u,\eta)}
\]

at leading wall order, where

\[
\eta=u-\frac16u''.
\]

## Nonwall matrix

\[
L
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\frac{\partial \mathbf R_N}
{\partial(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})}
\]

at first \(q_0\)-order.

## Matrix pencil determinant

Compute

\[
\det(M_{\rm wall}+\tau L)
\]

where

\[
\tau=q_0u^3.
\]

If for all admissible \(\tau\),

\[
|\det(M_{\rm wall}+\tau L)|\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then the two-row case already works.

If it has roots, add more rows and study the smallest singular value of the taller matrix pencil.

---

# 9. Outcome classification

| computation | implication |
|---|---|
| \(M_{\rm wall}\) nonsingular | wall and near-wall likely closed |
| \(M_{\rm wall}=0\), \(L\) nonsingular | D4 only; wall open |
| \(M_{\rm wall}\) singular but pencil \(M_{\rm wall}+\tau L\) full rank for admissible \(\tau\) | wall/near-wall controlled except exact kernel directions |
| pencil has admissible singular root | add rows or wall branch remains open |
| all row residues vanish to high order in \((u,\eta)\) | known rows wall-dead |

---

# 10. New best attack target

The strongest reduction is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute the matrix pencil }
M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L.
}
\]

This is better than the earlier vector-polynomial resultant because it uses the wall identities to reduce the relevant variables to

\[
W=
\left(u,\ u-\frac16u''\right).
\]

If this pencil is uniformly nonsingular, D3 and D4 collapse into one local theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2 pointwise mass}
\Rightarrow
\text{row lower bound}.
}
\]

If it is singular, the exact singular directions tell us which additional rows or invariants are needed.

## Continued attack — turn D3/D4 into an ideal-saturation certificate

The current best normal form is:

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\left(M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L\right)
W
+
\mathbf E,
\]

where

\[
W=
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
\eta
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\eta=u-\frac16u'',
\]

and Gate 2 wall/non-wall pointwise mass gives

\[
|u|^2|W|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

This already unified D3 and D4. The next attack is to make the row lower bound an **ideal-saturation / Nullstellensatz-style certificate**.

The desired theorem becomes finite algebra:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{whenever}\quad
|u|^2|W|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
}
\]

---

# 1. The right algebraic object is an ideal quotient

Let the local polynomial/differential algebra be

\[
\mathcal R
=
\mathbb C[q_0,u,u',u'',\ldots,\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2},\ldots],
\]

with the wall reductions

\[
\Gamma_G\equiv u\pmod{q_0},
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}\equiv \eta=u-\frac16u''\pmod{q_0}.
\]

Let the row vector be

\[
\mathbf R_N=(R_1,\ldots,R_s).
\]

Define the row ideal

\[
\mathcal I_R
=
(R_1,\ldots,R_s)\subset \mathcal R.
\]

Define the Gate 2 wall-mass ideal

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm G2}
=
(u,\eta).
\]

The bad algebraic event is:

\[
\mathbf R_N=0
\quad\text{while}\quad
(u,\eta)\ne(0,0).
\]

So the key algebraic condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V(\mathcal I_R)\cap
\left\{(u,\eta)\ne(0,0)\right\}
=
\varnothing.
}
\]

Equivalently, in ideal language:

\[
\boxed{
\sqrt{\mathcal I_R:\mathcal I_{\rm G2}^{\infty}}
=
\mathcal R.
}
\]

This is the cleanest symbolic target.

If true, the rows cannot vanish on the Gate 2 wall-mass locus.

---

# 2. Quantitative version

The qualitative saturation says no exact common zero. For the proof we need a polynomial lower bound.

A quantitative form is:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_N|
\ge
Q^{-A}
|(u,\eta)|^{N}
}
\]

for some fixed \(N\), under polynomial upper bounds on all local variables.

Then Gate 2 pointwise mass,

\[
|u|^2|(u,\eta)|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

plus an upper bound

\[
|u|\le Q^{C_u^+},
\]

gives

\[
|(u,\eta)|
\ge
Q^{-A_2/2-C_u^+},
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathbf R_N|
\ge
Q^{-A-N(A_2/2+C_u^+)}.
\]

So a quantitative saturation certificate closes D3/D4 uniformly.

---

# 3. Ideal certificate to compute

A concrete finite certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
u^N
=
\sum_{j=1}^s P_jR_j
}
\]

and/or

\[
\boxed{
\eta^N
=
\sum_{j=1}^s Q_jR_j
}
\]

or more symmetrically,

\[
\boxed{
(u,\eta)^N
\subset
\mathcal I_R.
}
\]

This would imply:

\[
|(u,\eta)|^N
\lesssim
Q^{C}|\mathbf R_N|
\]

if the certificate coefficients \(P_j,Q_j\) have polynomially bounded size on the local domain.

Thus the strongest finite symbolic result would be:

\[
\boxed{
(u,\eta)^N\subset (R_1,\ldots,R_s)
\quad\text{mod canonical row relations}.
}
\]

This is a row-ideal generation statement.

---

# 4. Relation to the matrix pencil

The matrix-pencil theorem is the degree-one version of this.

If

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
M(q_0,u)W+\mathbf E,
\]

where

\[
M(q_0,u)=M_{\rm wall}+q_0u^3L,
\]

and

\[
\sigma_{\min}M(q_0,u)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-C}|W|-|\mathbf E|.
\]

Algebraically, this says the row ideal contains the linear ideal

\[
(u,\eta)
\]

up to an invertible matrix.

In ideal language:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I_R\supset (u,\eta)
}
\]

locally, after inverting \(\det M\).

If the matrix pencil is singular, the weaker possibility is:

\[
\mathcal I_R\supset (u,\eta)^N
\]

for some \(N>1\), after adding more rows or higher-order corrections.

So the hierarchy is:

\[
(u,\eta)\subset \mathcal I_R
\quad\text{best};
\]

\[
(u,\eta)^N\subset \mathcal I_R
\quad\text{acceptable};
\]

\[
\sqrt{\mathcal I_R:(u,\eta)^\infty}=\mathcal R
\quad\text{qualitative only}.
\]

---

# 5. New finite symbolic protocol

For the row family

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,
\]

do the following.

## Step 1 — Reduce modulo \(q_0\)

Compute

\[
R_j^{(0)}=A_j\bmod(q_0).
\]

Use

\[
\Gamma_G\equiv u,
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}\equiv \eta=u-\frac16u''.
\]

## Step 2 — Form the wall row ideal

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm wall}
=
(R_7^{(0)},R_9^{(0)},R_{11}^{(0)},R_{13}^{(0)},\ldots).
\]

## Step 3 — Test ideal generation

Check whether

\[
(u,\eta)\subset \mathcal I_{\rm wall}.
\]

If yes, wall branch closes strongly.

If no, check whether

\[
(u,\eta)^2\subset \mathcal I_{\rm wall},
\]

then \((u,\eta)^3\), etc.

## Step 4 — If wall ideal fails, include \(q_0\)-terms

Compute

\[
A_j
=
R_j^{(0)}+q_0R_j^{(1)}+q_0^2R_j^{(2)}+\cdots.
\]

Build

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm pencil}
=
(A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots)
\]

in the variables

\[
q_0,u,\eta,\ldots.
\]

Test the saturation:

\[
\sqrt{\mathcal I_{\rm pencil}:(u,\eta)^\infty}
=
\mathcal R.
\]

This captures exact wall, near-wall, and non-wall simultaneously.

---

# 6. What this buys us

This reframes D3/D4 as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do known rows generate the Gate 2 anisotropy ideal }(u,\eta)
\text{ at the wall, possibly after saturation?}
}
\]

That is extremely concrete.

If yes, then the downstream row problem is no longer a conceptual blocker.

If no, then the current row system is provably insufficient at the wall, and a new invariant is genuinely needed.

---

# 7. Candidate theorem: anisotropy-ideal generation

The refined theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Anisotropy-Ideal Generation Theorem.}
}
\]

There exists a finite row vector

\[
\mathbf R_N=(A_7,A_9,\ldots,A_N)
\]

such that, in the local wall quotient,

\[
(u,\eta)^N
\subset
(A_7,\ldots,A_N)\bmod(q_0),
\]

with certificate coefficients bounded by \(Q^C\).

Then wall Gate 2 mass implies

\[
|\mathbf R_N|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If the theorem holds with \(N=1\), it gives the cleanest source-robust row theorem.

If it holds only after including \(q_0\)-terms, then the conclusion is the unified row-pencil theorem.

---

# 8. Attack the likely obstruction

The most likely failure mode is:

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\in(q_0)
\quad\forall k.
\]

Then

\[
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\bmod(q_0)=0,
\]

so

\[
\mathcal I_{\rm wall}=0.
\]

In that case no projected row can generate \((u,\eta)\). The wall is not a proof artifact; it is a real null direction of the projected row architecture.

Then one must switch to raw/ambient rows.

So the first symbolic check is brutally simple:

\[
\boxed{
A_{2k+1}^{\mathfrak f}\bmod(q_0)
\stackrel{?}{=}0
\quad
\text{for }k=3,4,5,\ldots
}
\]

If yes for all projected rows, stop searching projected rows and move to raw/ambient invariants.

---

# 9. Raw/ambient row version

For raw rows

\[
R_j^{\rm raw},
\]

compute

\[
R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0).
\]

Then test:

\[
(u,\eta)^N
\subset
(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0))_j.
\]

For ambient rows

\[
R_j^{\rm amb},
\]

do the same.

The best possible result is:

\[
\boxed{
(u,\eta)\subset
(R_j^{\rm raw}\bmod(q_0))_j
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
(u,\eta)\subset
(R_j^{\rm amb}\bmod(q_0))_j.
}
\]

This would give an actual source-robust row family.

---

# 10. Result of this attack

We have reduced the \(q_0\)-wall to a finite algebraic ideal membership problem:

\[
\boxed{
(u,\eta)^N
\subset
\mathcal I_{\rm rows}\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
\eta=u-\frac16u''.
}
\]

If true with bounded coefficients, D3 closes.

If false for all known projected/raw/ambient/correction rows, then D3 becomes a genuine named downstream input requiring a new row/invariant.

This is now the cleanest and most actionable form of the attack.
