## Assistant (2026-04-27 11:11:32)

I’ll continue by attacking the residual after the directional-rank test: once the two anisotropy directions are independent, choose the best row combination and project away irrelevant correction channels.

_Thought for 8s_

## Continued attack — optimal minor selection and residual projection

We now have a smaller D4 rank test:

\[
v_1=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3,\Gamma=e_1}(\mathbf A_N),
\qquad
v_2=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3,\Gamma=e_2}(\mathbf A_N),
\]

and D4 rank follows if

\[
v_1\wedge v_2\neq0.
\]

The next obstruction is the residual:

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N.
\]

Even if \(L_N\) has rank \(2\), the correction \(\mathbf R_N\) might be large in bad row directions. The attack now is to **choose the best two-row minor or best two-dimensional row projection** so the leading singular value is maximized and the residual is minimized. This improves D4 from “some rank exists” to a usable lower bound. 

---

# 1. Use row projections instead of fixed rows

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N\in \mathbb C^s
\]

be the finite downstream row vector, for example

\[
\mathbf A_N=(A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,A_N)^T.
\]

Suppose

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N,
\]

where

\[
L_N:\mathbb C^2\to\mathbb C^s.
\]

For any \(2\times s\) matrix \(P\), define a projected row pair:

\[
\mathbf B=P\mathbf A_N.
\]

Then

\[
\mathbf B
=
q_0u^3(PL_N)\Gamma+P\mathbf R_N.
\]

D4 needs:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(PL_N)
\]

large and

\[
|P\mathbf R_N|
\]

small.

So the best D4 row pair is not necessarily \((A_7,A_9)\); it is the best two-dimensional projection \(P\) of the available row space.

---

# 2. Canonical choice: left singular vectors

Let the singular value decomposition of \(L_N\) be

\[
L_N=U\Sigma V^\ast,
\]

where

\[
\Sigma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sigma_1&0\\
0&\sigma_2\\
0&0\\
\vdots&\vdots
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\sigma_1\ge\sigma_2>0.
\]

Choose \(P\) to be the first two left singular rows:

\[
P=U_2^\ast,
\]

where \(U_2\) spans the column space of \(L_N\). Then

\[
PL_N
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sigma_1&0\\
0&\sigma_2
\end{pmatrix}
V^\ast.
\]

Thus

\[
\sigma_{\min}(PL_N)=\sigma_2(L_N).
\]

Also,

\[
|P\mathbf R_N|\le |\mathbf R_N|.
\]

So the optimal projection theorem is:

If

\[
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf R_N|\le\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|,
\]

then

\[
|P\mathbf A_N|\ge
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

Since

\[
|P\mathbf A_N|\le |\mathbf A_N|,
\]

this gives the row lower bound for the full row vector:

\[
|\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Therefore D4 does **not** need a concrete two-row minor if we allow row-space norms. It only needs the full row-symbol map \(L_N\) to have two polynomially non-small singular values.

---

# 3. D4 rank theorem in invariant form

The sharper D4 rank input is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
}
\]

Here \(L_N\) is the coefficient map

\[
\Gamma\mapsto
\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3,\Gamma\text{-linear}}(\mathbf A_N).
\]

Equivalently, with directional responses

\[
v_1=L_Ne_1,\qquad v_2=L_Ne_2,
\]

we need

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

and

\[
|v_1|+|v_2|\le Q^C.
\]

Then

\[
\sigma_2(L_N)
=
\frac{|v_1\wedge v_2|}{\sigma_1(L_N)}
\ge Q^{-C'}.
\]

So the invariant rank certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
|v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C},
\qquad
|v_1|+|v_2|\le Q^C.
}
\]

This avoids choosing a minor.

---

# 4. Residual projection hierarchy

The residual can also be decomposed relative to the leading column space:

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf R_{\parallel}
+
\mathbf R_{\perp},
\]

where

\[
\mathbf R_{\parallel}\in \operatorname{im}L_N,
\qquad
\mathbf R_{\perp}\perp \operatorname{im}L_N.
\]

Only \(\mathbf R_{\parallel}\) can cancel the leading vector.

Indeed,

\[
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma
\in \operatorname{im}L_N.
\]

Thus

\[
\mathbf R_{\perp}
\]

is harmless for cancellation: it increases the norm or contributes orthogonally.

The dangerous correction is

\[
\mathbf R_{\parallel}=P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}\mathbf R_N.
\]

So the correction-control target can be sharpened to:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_{\parallel}|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

We do **not** need to bound the orthogonal residual by half-leading. This can substantially weaken the correction estimates.

---

# 5. Best possible residual condition

Let

\[
P_L
\]

be the orthogonal projection onto

\[
\operatorname{im}L_N.
\]

Then

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma
+
P_L\mathbf R_N
+
(I-P_L)\mathbf R_N.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathbf A_N|^2
=
|q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+P_L\mathbf R_N|^2
+
|(I-P_L)\mathbf R_N|^2.
\]

It suffices to prove:

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12 |q_0u^3L_N\Gamma|.
\]

Since

\[
|L_N\Gamma|\ge \sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|,
\]

a sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This is strictly better than bounding \(|\mathbf R_N|\).

---

# 6. Interpret corrections via row-space directions

Every correction term \(\mathbf R_\alpha\) has a row-space direction.

If

\[
\mathbf R_\alpha\perp \operatorname{im}L_N,
\]

it cannot cancel the leading D4 term.

Thus the correction audit should classify each correction not only by size, but by whether it projects into the leading row-symbol span.

Finite protocol:

1. Compute \(L_N\) and its column space.
2. For each correction vector \(\mathbf R_\alpha\), compute

   \[
   P_L\mathbf R_\alpha.
   \]

3. Only require half-leading bounds for

   \[
   \sum_\alpha P_L\mathbf R_\alpha.
   \]

This may close D4 even if the full correction vector is large.

---

# 7. D4 theorem with projected residual

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Projected-Residual Theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N.
\]

Assume:

\[
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

Let

\[
P_L
\]

be the orthogonal projection onto \(\operatorname{im}L_N\). If

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

Thus under D4 sampling hypotheses,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

---

# 8. New correction-control target

Old target:

\[
|\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

New target:

\[
\boxed{
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This is the most efficient form.

It says: corrections in non-leading row directions are not dangerous.

---

# 9. Consequence for row choice

If a fixed pair \((A_7,A_9)\) has large corrections, add more rows.

Increasing \(N\) can make

\[
\operatorname{im}L_N
\]

larger inside the row space, but it also changes the projection. However, since the leading image remains 2-dimensional, adding rows gives more room for corrections to lie outside the leading image.

In practice, adding rows can improve correction control because:

- the leading two-dimensional column space may become better conditioned;
- correction vectors may become more orthogonal to it;
- one can choose optimal projections.

Thus the finite row vector should be selected to maximize

\[
\frac{\sigma_2(L_N)}
{|P_L\mathbf R_N|/(|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|)}.
\]

This is an algebraic optimization over finite rows.

---

# 10. Attack result

D4 now reduces to:

1. Compute directional response vectors

   \[
   v_1,\ v_2
   \]

   for available rows.

2. Prove

   \[
   |v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
   \]

3. Compute the leading-image projection

   \[
   P_L.
   \]

4. Bound only

   \[
   P_L\mathbf R_N,
   \]

   not all of \(\mathbf R_N\).

This should make correction-control substantially easier.

The sharpened D4 statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N,
\quad
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C},
\quad
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then D4 follows.

This is the strongest current D4 formulation.

I’ll push the projected-residual formulation one step further: reduce correction control to two scalar inner-product estimates against the leading response vectors.

_Thought for 9s_

## Continued attack — reduce D4 correction control to two scalar projection estimates

We have the strongest current D4 form:

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N,
\qquad
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

Let

\[
v_1=L_Ne_1,\qquad v_2=L_Ne_2,
\]

so

\[
q_0u^3L_N\Gamma
=
q_0u^3(\Gamma_Gv_1+\Gamma_{G,2}v_2).
\]

The previous improvement was that we do **not** need to bound all of \(\mathbf R_N\). We only need to bound its projection onto

\[
V=\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2\}.
\]

Now attack that projection.

---

# 1. Projection onto the leading image is controlled by two inner products

Let

\[
G_L=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle v_1,v_1\rangle & \langle v_1,v_2\rangle\\
\langle v_2,v_1\rangle & \langle v_2,v_2\rangle
\end{pmatrix}
=
L_N^\ast L_N.
\]

Assume

\[
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

Then

\[
G_L^{-1}
\]

exists and

\[
\|G_L^{-1}\|\le Q^{2C_L}.
\]

For any residual vector \(\mathbf R_N\), its projection to \(V\) is

\[
P_L\mathbf R_N
=
L_N(G_L^{-1}L_N^\ast \mathbf R_N).
\]

Therefore

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\|L_N\|\,\|G_L^{-1}\|\,|L_N^\ast\mathbf R_N|.
\]

If

\[
\|L_N\|\le Q^{C_U},
\]

then

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
Q^{C_U+2C_L}|L_N^\ast\mathbf R_N|.
\]

So D4 correction control reduces to controlling the two scalar quantities

\[
\boxed{
\langle v_1,\mathbf R_N\rangle,
\qquad
\langle v_2,\mathbf R_N\rangle.
}
\]

This is a major reduction.

---

# 2. Scalar correction-control theorem

D4 needs

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

It suffices to prove

\[
\boxed{
|L_N^\ast\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12
Q^{-C_U-3C_L}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
|\langle v_a,\mathbf R_N\rangle|
\le
Q^{-C_*}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|,
\qquad a=1,2,
}
\]

with \(C_*\) large enough to absorb conditioning.

Thus the full vector correction theorem is replaced by two scalar projection identities.

---

# 3. Why these scalar projections may be much smaller

Most correction terms may be large in row space, but if they are orthogonal to the leading anisotropy response vectors \(v_1,v_2\), they cannot cancel the D4 leading row.

So for every correction component

\[
\mathbf R_\alpha,
\]

we only need

\[
\langle v_a,\mathbf R_\alpha\rangle
\]

small, not

\[
|\mathbf R_\alpha|
\]

small.

This is important for correction families such as:

- gauge correction rows;
- projected-out lower rows;
- normalization residuals;
- raw/ambient rows orthogonal to the \(A_7,A_9\) anisotropy plane.

Large correction terms in the orthogonal complement are harmless.

---

# 4. New finite certificate: orthogonality of residual channels

Decompose

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\sum_\alpha \mathbf R_\alpha.
\]

For each residual channel \(\alpha\), compute the two scalar projections:

\[
r_{\alpha,1}
=
\langle v_1,\mathbf R_\alpha\rangle,
\]

\[
r_{\alpha,2}
=
\langle v_2,\mathbf R_\alpha\rangle.
\]

Classify:

## Harmless orthogonal channel

\[
r_{\alpha,1}=r_{\alpha,2}=0
\]

at principal residual order.

Then \(\mathbf R_\alpha\) cannot cancel the leading term.

## Perturbative projected channel

\[
|r_{\alpha,a}|
\le
Q^{-C}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Then it is harmless by size.

## Dangerous projected channel

\[
r_{\alpha,a}
\]

has the same scale as the leading term.

Then it must be absorbed into \(L_N\), not treated as residual.

Thus the rule is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any correction with non-small projection onto }V
\text{ becomes part of }L_N.
}
\]

After doing that, the remaining residual must be projected-small.

---

# 5. Effective leading matrix including projected corrections

Let

\[
\mathbf R_N^{\rm lin}
\]

be the part of the correction that is linear in \(\Gamma\) and has scale \(q_0u^3\). Instead of calling it residual, define

\[
L_{\rm eff}
=
L_N+
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\mathbf R_N^{\rm lin}
\quad
\text{as a map in }\Gamma.
\]

Then

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm eff}\Gamma+\mathbf R_N^{\rm true}.
\]

Now the D4 rank condition becomes

\[
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

The correction-control condition becomes

\[
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_{\rm eff}}\mathbf R_N^{\rm true}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|.
\]

This is the final robust form. It says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all same-scale projected corrections are not errors; they are part of the leading matrix.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Normal-equation D4 theorem

A clean theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Normal-Equation Correction Theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm eff}\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm true}.
\]

Assume:

\[
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

\[
\|L_{\rm eff}\|\le Q^{C_U}.
\]

Assume the two scalar normal-equation residuals satisfy

\[
\boxed{
|L_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm true}|
\le
\frac12
Q^{-C_U-2C_L}
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_{\rm eff}}\mathbf R_{\rm true}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|,
\]

and hence

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|.
\]

So D4 follows.

This is strictly weaker than requiring

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm true}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|.
\]

---

# 7. What to compute next

Given exact row formulas, compute only:

## 1. Leading responses

\[
v_1=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_G}(\mathbf A_N),
\]

\[
v_2=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}}(\mathbf A_N).
\]

## 2. Effective leading rank

\[
G_L=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle v_1,v_1\rangle & \langle v_1,v_2\rangle\\
\langle v_2,v_1\rangle & \langle v_2,v_2\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Check:

\[
\det G_L\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is equivalent to

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## 3. True residual

Subtract all \(q_0u^3\Gamma\)-linear terms:

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm true}
=
\mathbf A_N-q_0u^3L_{\rm eff}\Gamma.
\]

## 4. Normal-equation residuals

Compute:

\[
\langle v_1,\mathbf R_{\rm true}\rangle,
\qquad
\langle v_2,\mathbf R_{\rm true}\rangle.
\]

Bound these by the D4 normal-equation threshold.

---

# 8. Why this helps with projection/gauge terms

Projection/gauge residuals often lie in the row space generated by lower constraints. They may be large but orthogonal to the active anisotropy response plane. The normal-equation test detects exactly whether they can cancel the leading D4 term.

If their normal-equation projections vanish by construction, D4 correction control is automatic for those channels.

Thus the projection/gauge correction audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
L_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm proj}=0
}
\]

or polynomially small.

This is likely much easier than bounding \(|\mathbf R_{\rm proj}|\).

---

# 9. Attack result

D4 correction control is now reduced to:

\[
\boxed{
\det(L_{\rm eff}^\ast L_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|L_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm true}|
\le Q^{-C}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This is the smallest current finite certificate for D4.

It avoids bounding residuals in harmless row directions and converts correction-control into two scalar projection estimates.

This should be the next exact algebraic computation once row formulas are available.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 11:14:09)

I’ll turn the D4 row lower bound into a contradiction-style normal-equation lemma: assume the row vector is small, then force \(\Gamma\) small, contradicting Gate 2 mass.

_Thought for 13s_

## Continued attack — D4 as a normal-equation contradiction

The previous step reduced D4 correction control to two scalar estimates:

\[
\det(L_{\rm eff}^\ast L_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
|L_{\rm eff}^\ast \mathbf R_{\rm true}|
\le Q^{-C}|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Now push one level further: instead of directly lower-bounding the row vector, prove that **if the row vector were small, then \(\Gamma\) would be small**, contradicting Gate 2 pointwise mass.

This is cleaner and avoids unnecessary estimates in row directions orthogonal to the leading image. 

---

# 1. D4 normal form

Let

\[
\mathbf A
=
\mathbf A_N
\]

be a finite row vector, possibly

\[
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})
\]

or a larger row family.

Use the effective leading form

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
}
\]

where

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Define the Gram matrix

\[
G_L=L^\ast L.
\]

Assume

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_L)=\sigma_2(L)^2\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

---

# 2. Apply the normal equations

Apply \(L^\ast\) to the row equation:

\[
L^\ast\mathbf A
=
q_0u^3L^\ast L\Gamma
+
L^\ast\mathbf R.
\]

So

\[
q_0u^3G_L\Gamma
=
L^\ast\mathbf A
-
L^\ast\mathbf R.
\]

Taking norms,

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\lambda_{\min}(G_L)|\Gamma|
\le
|L^\ast\mathbf A|
+
|L^\ast\mathbf R|.
\]

If

\[
\|L\|\le Q^{C_U},
\]

then

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf A|\le Q^{C_U}|\mathbf A|.
\]

Thus

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\lambda_{\min}(G_L)|\Gamma|
\le
Q^{C_U}|\mathbf A|
+
|L^\ast\mathbf R|.
\]

---

# 3. Projected residual condition

Require only the normal-equation residual estimate

\[
\boxed{
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\lambda_{\min}(G_L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
\frac34
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\lambda_{\min}(G_L)|\Gamma|
\le
Q^{C_U}|\mathbf A|.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A|
\ge
\frac34
Q^{-C_U}
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\lambda_{\min}(G_L)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Since

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A|
\ge
Q^{-C_U-C_L}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

This is D4.

---

# 4. Contradiction form

Equivalently, suppose the row vector is too small:

\[
|\mathbf A|
\le
Q^{-C_{\rm row}}.
\]

Then the normal equation gives

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|
\le Q^{C_U+C_L}
\left(
Q^{-C_{\rm row}}
+
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\right).
\]

Under projected residual control, this implies

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|
\le
Q^{-C_{\rm row}+O(1)}.
\]

But at a non-wall Gate 2 sample,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

If additionally either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

then

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|
\]

is polynomially non-small. Contradiction.

This is often the easiest way to present D4.

---

# 5. Final D4 normal-equation theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Normal-Equation Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Assume at \(m_\ast\):

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Assume the row normal form

\[
\mathbf A=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R.
\]

Assume:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

\[
\|L\|\le Q^{C_U},
\]

and

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)
|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A|
\ge
Q^{-C}
}
\]

provided either:

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u},
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

More explicitly:

## If \(|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}\)

Because

\[
|u||\Gamma|\ge Q^{-A_2/2},
\]

we have

\[
|u|^3|\Gamma|
=
|u|^2(|u||\Gamma|)
\ge
Q^{-2C_u-A_2/2}.
\]

Thus

\[
|\mathbf A|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-C_U-C_L-2C_u-A_2/2-O(1)}.
\]

## If \(|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}\)

Then

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

Hence

\[
|u|^3|\Gamma|
=
|u|^2(|u||\Gamma|)
\ge
Q^{-A_2-2C_\Gamma}Q^{-A_2/2}
=
Q^{-3A_2/2-2C_\Gamma}.
\]

So

\[
|\mathbf A|
\ge
Q^{-C_q-C_U-C_L-\frac32A_2-2C_\Gamma-O(1)}.
\]

---

# 6. What remains finite

D4 now needs only these finite checks.

## Check 1 — effective leading map

Extract all \(q_0u^3\Gamma\)-linear terms into \(L\):

\[
\mathbf A=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R.
\]

## Check 2 — leading Gram lower bound

Compute

\[
G_L=L^\ast L.
\]

Prove

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

and

\[
\|L\|\le Q^{C_U}.
\]

For a \(2\times2\) pair, this is equivalent to

\[
|\det L|\ge Q^{-C},
\qquad
\|L\|\le Q^C.
\]

For a taller row vector, use the two directional responses

\[
v_1=Le_1,\qquad v_2=Le_2,
\]

and prove

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C},
\qquad
|v_1|+|v_2|\le Q^C.
\]

## Check 3 — normal residual

Compute

\[
L^\ast\mathbf R.
\]

Do **not** need all of \(\mathbf R\).

Prove

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)
|\Gamma|.
\]

This is now the only correction-control condition.

---

# 7. Why this is strongest

Earlier correction control required:

\[
|\mathbf R|
\le \frac12 |q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(L)|\Gamma|.
\]

Now we only need:

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0||u|^3\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)|\Gamma|.
\]

This is weaker because any residual orthogonal to the leading image is harmless.

So the remaining correction audit is two scalar equations:

\[
\langle v_1,\mathbf R\rangle,
\qquad
\langle v_2,\mathbf R\rangle.
\]

If those vanish by projection/gauge orthogonality, D4 correction control is essentially automatic.

---

# 8. Attack result

D4 is now reduced to the smallest finite certificate so far:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)\ge Q^{-C},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)
|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This is strictly better than bounding the whole residual and gives a clean contradiction-style proof of row non-smallness.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 11:15:11)

I’ll continue by attacking the remaining scalar residual condition \(L^\ast\mathbf R\): the goal is to make it vanish, or become higher order, by choosing the leading map \(L\) as the least-squares projection of the row vector onto the anisotropy plane.

_Thought for 10s_

## Continued attack — make \(L^\ast\mathbf R=0\) by defining \(L\) via row-space projection

The current D4 normal-equation certificate is:

\[
\mathbf A=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
\]

\[
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14|q_0|\,|u|^3\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)|\Gamma|.
\]

The remaining hard part is the scalar residual condition

\[
L^\ast\mathbf R.
\]

The next attack is to choose \(L\) so that this residual is orthogonal to the leading anisotropy image **by construction**. This turns the correction-control condition from an estimate into a normal-form identity. This is the cleanest possible D4 formulation. 

---

# 1. Treat the row vector as a function of \(\Gamma\)

Let

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Near a D4 sample, decompose the row vector as

\[
\mathbf A(\Gamma)
=
\mathbf A^{(0)}
+
\mathbf A^{(1)}(\Gamma)
+
\mathbf A^{(\ge2)}(\Gamma),
\]

where:

- \(\mathbf A^{(0)}\) is independent of \(\Gamma\);
- \(\mathbf A^{(1)}\) is linear in \(\Gamma\);
- \(\mathbf A^{(\ge2)}\) is at least quadratic in \(\Gamma\).

D4’s leading term should be the part of \(\mathbf A^{(1)}\) that has scale

\[
q_0u^3\Gamma.
\]

So define

\[
\mathbf A^{(1)}_{\rm lead}(\Gamma)
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm eff}\Gamma.
\]

Then the true residual is

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm true}
=
\mathbf A-\mathbf A^{(1)}_{\rm lead}.
\]

The key is to choose \(L_{\rm eff}\) as the **exact coefficient map** of the \(\Gamma\)-linear part, not as an approximate guessed matrix.

---

# 2. Define \(L_{\rm eff}\) by formal differentiation

Let

\[
\Gamma_1=\Gamma_G,
\qquad
\Gamma_2=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Define

\[
\boxed{
L_{\rm eff}
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\left[
\frac{\partial \mathbf A}{\partial \Gamma_1}
\quad
\frac{\partial \mathbf A}{\partial \Gamma_2}
\right]_{\Gamma=0,\ \text{same-scale part}}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
q_0u^3L_{\rm eff}\Gamma
\]

is the full \(q_0u^3\)-scale, \(\Gamma\)-linear component of \(\mathbf A\).

With this definition, the residual has no \(q_0u^3\)-scale linear term in \(\Gamma\):

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3,\Gamma\text{-linear}}(\mathbf R_{\rm true})=0.
}
\]

Therefore any contribution to

\[
L_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm true}
\]

must come from:

1. \(\Gamma\)-independent terms;
2. terms quadratic or higher in \(\Gamma\);
3. canonical/tail/projection errors not at \(q_0u^3\Gamma\)-scale;
4. non-polynomial normalization errors.

This is already a significant reduction.

---

# 3. If projection is orthogonal, \(L^\ast R\) should vanish to first order

If the \(\mathfrak f\)-projection is an orthogonal projection in the row metric used to form the row vector, then the residual after projecting onto the anisotropy response plane satisfies

\[
P_{\operatorname{im}L}\mathbf R_{\rm true}=0
\]

at the same order.

In normal-equation form:

\[
L^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm true}=0.
\]

This is exactly the least-squares orthogonality condition.

Thus the strongest possible D4 correction theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
L_{\rm eff}
\text{ is defined by the row-space least-squares projection of }
\mathbf A
\text{ onto }q_0u^3\Gamma.
}
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
L_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm true}=0
}
\]

for all same-scale \(\Gamma\)-linear correction channels.

Remaining \(L^\ast R\) terms are automatically higher order or canonical-error terms.

---

# 4. Least-squares normal form

Let

\[
B(\Gamma)=q_0u^3\Gamma\in \mathbb C^2
\]

be the local anisotropy input.

Let the row vector be

\[
\mathbf A\in\mathbb C^s.
\]

We seek a matrix

\[
L:\mathbb C^2\to\mathbb C^s
\]

such that

\[
\mathbf A=L B+\mathbf R
\]

and

\[
L^\ast\mathbf R=0.
\]

If \(\mathbf A\) is linear in \(B\), this is exact. If \(\mathbf A\) has nonlinear/canonical errors, this is exact at the linearized level.

The normal equations are:

\[
L^\ast(\mathbf A-LB)=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
L^\ast\mathbf A=L^\ast L B.
\]

D4 then follows directly if

\[
L^\ast L
\]

is invertible with polynomial condition number.

This is the cleanest contradiction argument:

if \(\mathbf A\) were small, then

\[
L^\ast\mathbf A
\]

would be small, hence

\[
L^\ast L B
\]

would be small, hence \(B=q_0u^3\Gamma\) would be small, contradicting non-wall Gate 2 mass.

---

# 5. D4 least-squares theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Least-Squares Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Assume there is a finite row vector \(\mathbf A_N\) and a matrix \(L\) such that

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R
\]

and

\[
L^\ast\mathbf R=0
\]

up to higher-order/canonical errors satisfying

\[
|L^\ast\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14|q_0|\,|u|^3\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)|\Gamma|.
\]

Assume

\[
\lambda_{\min}(L^\ast L)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\qquad
\|L\|\le Q^{C_U}.
\]

Then:

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge Q^{-C}
\]

under

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

This is the same D4 conclusion, but the correction-control burden is now just:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove the residual is normal-equation orthogonal up to higher-order errors.}
}
\]

---

# 6. What remains to estimate

With \(L\) defined as the exact \(\Gamma\)-linear coefficient map, the residual splits as

\[
\mathbf R
=
\mathbf R_{\Gamma^0}
+
\mathbf R_{\Gamma^{\ge2}}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm can}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm norm}.
\]

Now estimate \(L^\ast\mathbf R\) term-by-term.

## 6.1 \(\Gamma\)-independent residual

A \(\Gamma\)-independent term \(\mathbf R_{\Gamma^0}\) is dangerous only if it projects onto \(\operatorname{im}L\).

But if rows \(A_7,A_9,\ldots\) are anisotropy rows, there should be no same-scale \(\Gamma^0\) residual at weight \(q_0u^3\Gamma\). Any such term must either:

- have lower row order and be removed by projection;
- contain extra small factors;
- be part of canonical error.

Audit target:

\[
\boxed{
L^\ast\mathbf R_{\Gamma^0}
\text{ is higher order or zero.}
}
\]

## 6.2 Quadratic anisotropy residual

Terms quadratic in \(\Gamma\) are bounded by

\[
|\mathbf R_{\Gamma^{\ge2}}|
\le
Q^C |q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|^2.
\]

To compare with leading scale

\[
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|,
\]

need

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{-c}
\]

or include such terms in \(L_{\rm eff}\) if \(\Gamma\) is not small.

But in the D4 application after wall escape, \(\Gamma_G\) is at least \(Q^{-C}\), not necessarily small. It is polynomially bounded. Quadratic \(\Gamma\)-terms may be larger than linear ones if \(\Gamma\) is large.

Therefore the robust approach is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all nonperturbative polynomial dependence on }\Gamma
\text{ must be included in an effective nonlinear map.}
}
\]

This suggests a further refinement.

---

# 7. Nonlinear D4 map

Instead of assuming row response is linear in \(\Gamma\), define a nonlinear map:

\[
\Phi(q_0,u,\Gamma)
\]

so that

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3\Phi(\Gamma)
+
\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

D4 requires a lower bound:

\[
|\Phi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible range.

The linear matrix \(L\) is the first approximation. If higher powers of \(\Gamma\) are present and not perturbative, they should be included in \(\Phi\).

Then D4 becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Effective Row Map Nonvanishing.}
}
\]

There exists a finite row map \(\Phi\) such that

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Phi(\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

\[
|\Phi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|,
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le \frac12|q_0||u|^3|\Phi(\Gamma)|.
\]

This is stronger and avoids false linearization if \(\Gamma\) is not small.

---

# 8. When linear D4 is sufficient

Linear D4 is sufficient if one of the following holds:

## Case A — row formulas are genuinely linear in \(\Gamma\)

That is,

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma+\text{terms independent of or perturbative in }\Gamma.
\]

Then the normal-equation theorem is enough.

## Case B — anisotropy is small

If

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{-c},
\]

then quadratic terms are perturbative.

But in Gate 2 row conversion, we often only know polynomial upper bounds, not smallness. So Case B is not reliable.

## Case C — nonlinear terms have favorable sign/orthogonality

If quadratic terms are orthogonal to the leading image or increase the row norm, they are harmless. This can be checked by normal-equation projections.

Thus the safe audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Are the downstream rows linear in }(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\text{ at all nonperturbative orders?}
}
\]

If yes, linear D4 closes. If no, use nonlinear \(\Phi\).

---

# 9. New final D4 hierarchy

D4 should be attacked in this order.

## Level 1 — Linear response

Compute

\[
L=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3,\Gamma\text{-linear}}\mathbf A_N.
\]

If

\[
\sigma_2(L)\ge Q^{-C}
\]

and all nonlinear/projected residuals are perturbative or orthogonal, D4 closes.

## Level 2 — Effective nonlinear response

If nonlinear \(\Gamma\)-terms are nonperturbative, define

\[
\Phi(\Gamma)
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}
\mathbf A_N
\]

after removing canonical errors.

Prove

\[
|\Phi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible \(\Gamma\)-range.

## Level 3 — Add rows

If \(\Phi\) has zeros for current rows, add more rows until the vector map has no zeros except \(\Gamma=0\).

This becomes an ideal-generation condition:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
(A_7,A_9,A_{11},\ldots)/(q_0u^3)
\]

after localizing away from \(q_0u\).

---

# 10. Attack result

The normal-equation attack reveals a hidden assumption:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 linear matrix form is valid only if nonperturbative row dependence on }\Gamma
\text{ is linear, or otherwise harmless.}
}
\]

The safest final D4 theorem is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3\Phi(\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
}
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
|\Phi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le\frac12|q_0||u|^3|\Phi(\Gamma)|.
}
\]

The matrix theorem is the special case

\[
\Phi(\Gamma)=L\Gamma.
\]

So the next exact audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{check whether }A_7,A_9,\ldots
\text{ are nonperturbatively linear in }\Gamma.
}
\]

If yes, D4 is the normal-equation matrix theorem.

If no, D4 becomes a finite nonlinear map nonvanishing theorem.

I’ll continue by removing the last hidden assumption in D4: linearity in \(\Gamma\). The clean replacement is an anisotropy-ideal generation condition for the full nonlinear row map.

_Thought for 14s_

## Continued attack — replace linear D4 by anisotropy-ideal generation

The previous D4 form was:

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3L\Gamma+\mathbf R,
\qquad
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

The hidden assumption was that all nonperturbative dependence on \(\Gamma\) is linear. That may be true, but we should not assume it. The stronger attack is to let the row map be nonlinear:

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3\Phi(\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Then D4 needs:

\[
|\Phi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N
\]

for some fixed \(N\), plus canonical-error control. This turns D4 into a finite ideal-generation problem in the anisotropy variables. This is the natural nonlinear upgrade of the matrix theorem already developed. 

---

# 1. Define the anisotropy ideal

Let

\[
\Gamma_1=\Gamma_G,\qquad
\Gamma_2=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Define the anisotropy ideal

\[
\mathfrak a=(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2).
\]

Work in the local ring where \(q_0\) and \(u\) are localized away from zero in the non-wall D4 branch:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}
\]

is available.

Write the row vector after removing the common factor \(q_0u^3\):

\[
\Psi
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}\mathbf A_N
\quad
\text{mod canonical errors}.
\]

Then D4 becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma\ne0
\Rightarrow
|\Psi(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N.
}
\]

Algebraically, this is equivalent to saying the row components generate a power of the anisotropy ideal:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak a^N\subset (\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
}
\]

This is the nonlinear replacement for the linear rank condition.

---

# 2. Linear D4 is the case \(N=1\)

If

\[
\Psi=L\Gamma+\text{higher},
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{rank}L=2,
\]

then

\[
(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2)\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

locally, so \(N=1\).

This recovers the matrix theorem.

If the linear rank degenerates but quadratic or higher row terms still detect every nonzero \(\Gamma\), then one may have:

\[
\mathfrak a^2\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

or more generally:

\[
\mathfrak a^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

That still gives a polynomial row lower bound, with a worse exponent but no conceptual failure.

So the correct D4 hierarchy is:

\[
N=1:\quad \text{matrix rank theorem;}
\]

\[
N>1:\quad \text{nonlinear anisotropy-ideal generation theorem.}
\]

---

# 3. Quantitative D4 from ideal generation

Suppose

\[
\mathfrak a^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

with polynomially bounded certificate coefficients. Concretely, for every monomial

\[
\Gamma_1^a\Gamma_2^{N-a},
\qquad 0\le a\le N,
\]

there exist local coefficients \(P_{a,j}\) such that

\[
\Gamma_1^a\Gamma_2^{N-a}
=
\sum_{j=1}^s P_{a,j}\Psi_j,
\]

and

\[
|P_{a,j}|\le Q^C
\]

on the D4 admissible region.

Then

\[
|\Gamma|^N\le Q^C|\Psi|.
\]

Therefore

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
=
|q_0u^3\Psi+\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\ge
|q_0||u|^3|\Psi|-|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|.
\]

If

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3|\Psi|,
\]

then

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
\frac12Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|^N.
\]

Now use Gate 2 pointwise mass:

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

If \(|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}\), then

\[
|\Gamma|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_u}.
\]

So

\[
|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|^N
\ge
Q^{-C_q-3C_u-N(A_2/2+C_u)}.
\]

This is polynomially non-small.

If instead \(|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}\), then

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_\Gamma}.
\]

Then

\[
|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|^N
\]

still has a polynomial lower bound, provided \(|\Gamma|\) is not zero; Gate 2 gives

\[
|\Gamma|\ge Q^{-A_2/2-C_u^+}
\]

if an upper bound \(|u|\le Q^{C_u^+}\) is also available. Such upper bounds are part of the canonical local complexity package.

Thus fixed \(N\) is acceptable.

---

# 4. New D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Anisotropy-Ideal Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N=(A_7,A_9,\ldots,A_N)
\]

be a finite row vector. Suppose on the non-wall branch,

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3\Psi(\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

where

\[
\Psi=(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

is a finite polynomial/differential-polynomial row map in \(\Gamma\) and local variables.

Assume:

1. **Anisotropy ideal generation**

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

with certificate coefficients bounded by \(Q^C\).

2. **Canonical residual control**

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3|\Psi(\Gamma)|.
\]

3. **Non-wall and Gate 2 pointwise mass**

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

4. **Polynomial upper/lower complexity bounds** sufficient to convert the ideal-generation estimate into a polynomial lower bound.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This theorem strictly contains the matrix D4 theorem as the \(N=1\) case.

---

# 5. Why this is useful

The linear D4 matrix theorem can fail if:

\[
L
\]

is singular or if same-order nonlinear terms in \(\Gamma\) are nonperturbative.

But D4 does not actually require linear response. It only requires the row vector to vanish only when

\[
\Gamma=0.
\]

That is exactly:

\[
\sqrt{(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)}\supset(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

The power containment

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

is the effective version.

So if row formulas produce nonlinear anisotropy terms, we can still close D4 by ideal generation.

---

# 6. Finite symbolic protocol

Given exact row formulas:

## Step 1 — localize

Work in the non-wall ring where \(q_0u^3\) is factored out:

\[
\Psi_j=
\frac{A_j^{\rm lead}}{q_0u^3}.
\]

Remove canonical error terms into \(\mathbf R_{\rm can}\).

## Step 2 — build row ideal

\[
\mathcal I_\Psi=(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

## Step 3 — test linear rank first

Compute the Jacobian with respect to \(\Gamma\) at \(\Gamma=0\):

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi(0).
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}J_\Gamma\Psi(0)=2,
\]

then \(N=1\), and the matrix theorem applies.

## Step 4 — if linear rank fails, test powers

Check ideal membership:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^2\subset\mathcal I_\Psi,
\]

then \(N=3\), etc.

This is done by Gröbner reduction of the monomial generators:

\[
\Gamma_G^a\Gamma_{G,2}^{N-a}.
\]

## Step 5 — certificate coefficient bounds

Record representations

\[
\Gamma_G^a\Gamma_{G,2}^{N-a}
=
\sum_jP_{a,j}\Psi_j.
\]

Check that \(P_{a,j}\) are polynomially bounded on the admissible region.

If yes, D4 closes with exponent loss depending on \(N\).

---

# 7. Relation to row-pencil wall theorem

This nonlinear D4 theorem is the non-wall counterpart of the \(q_0\)-adic wall ideal theorem.

Wall theorem used:

\[
(u,\eta)^N
\subset
(R_1,\ldots,R_s)\bmod(q_0),
\qquad
\eta=u-\frac16u''.
\]

Non-wall theorem uses:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
\left(
\frac{A_7}{q_0u^3},
\frac{A_9}{q_0u^3},
\ldots
\right).
\]

So the same algebraic machinery handles both wall and non-wall row conversion.

If Wall-Escape Sampling is valid, the wall theorem may be unnecessary. If Wall-Escape fails, the same ideal-generation protocol applies at \(q_0=0\).

---

# 8. Current strongest D4 formulation

The final D4 row-conversion target should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{There exists a finite row vector }\mathbf A_N
\text{ such that after factoring }q_0u^3,
\text{ its row ideal contains a fixed power of }
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
}
\]

This avoids assuming linearity, avoids choosing specific rows prematurely, and is robust under nonlinear corrections.

Matrix D4 remains the preferred first check:

\[
\operatorname{rank}J_\Gamma\Psi(0)=2.
\]

If that succeeds, D4 closes with the cleanest exponent.

If not, continue the ideal-generation test.

---

# 9. Attack result

D4 is no longer dependent on a fragile linear model. It reduces to an exact finite algebra statement:

\[
\boxed{
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
\left(
A_7/(q_0u^3),\,
A_9/(q_0u^3),\,
A_{11}/(q_0u^3),\ldots
\right)
}
\]

with bounded certificate coefficients and canonical residual control.

This is the robust final form of row conversion in the non-wall branch.

## Continued attack — use Nakayama/Jacobian rank to close D4 ideal generation

The current robust D4 target is:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
\left(
\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s
\right),
\qquad
\Psi_j:=\frac{A_j}{q_0u^3}
\]

after removing canonical residuals, on the non-wall branch

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q}.
\]

This is stronger than the linear matrix theorem and does not assume that the rows depend linearly on

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

Now attack the ideal-generation condition itself.

---

# 1. Work in the anisotropy local ring

Let

\[
x=\Gamma_G,\qquad y=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Let \(Z\) denote all other local variables:

\[
Z=(q_0,u,u',u'',q_0',q_0'',\ldots).
\]

After localizing away from the non-wall factors

\[
q_0u^3\neq0,
\]

write the row map as

\[
\Psi(Z;x,y)
=
(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

Work in the local ring at the anisotropy origin:

\[
\mathcal O_Z[[x,y]]
\]

with maximal anisotropy ideal

\[
\mathfrak a=(x,y).
\]

D4 needs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak a^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
}
\]

with \(N=O(1)\) and polynomially bounded certificates.

---

# 2. First attack: Jacobian rank criterion

Compute the anisotropy Jacobian at the origin:

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0,0)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\partial_x\Psi_1 & \partial_y\Psi_1\\
\vdots & \vdots\\
\partial_x\Psi_s & \partial_y\Psi_s
\end{pmatrix}_{(x,y)=0}.
\]

If

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0,0)=2,
}
\]

then the row ideal contains the anisotropy ideal itself:

\[
\boxed{
(x,y)\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
}
\]

locally, after possibly replacing rows by linear combinations.

This is just Nakayama’s lemma: the images of \(\Psi_j\) span

\[
\mathfrak a/\mathfrak a^2.
\]

If they span the cotangent space, they generate \(\mathfrak a\) in the completed local ring.

So \(N=1\).

This recovers the linear D4 matrix theorem:

\[
\sigma_2(L_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

The exact certificate is the nonzero \(2\times2\) minor of the Jacobian. In the expected architecture,

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi
=
-\frac1{288}I_2
\]

using the \(A_7,A_9\) rows. 

---

# 3. If Jacobian rank is one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}J_\Gamma\Psi=1,
\]

then only one anisotropy direction is detected linearly.

By row changes and a coordinate change in \((x,y)\), reduce to:

\[
\Psi_1=x+\text{higher},
\]

and all other rows have no linear \(y\)-term.

Modulo \(\Psi_1\), the problem reduces to one variable \(y\). Let

\[
\overline{\Psi}_j(y)=\Psi_j(0,y)\mod \Psi_1.
\]

If some row has leading term

\[
\overline{\Psi}_j(y)=c y^d+\text{higher},
\qquad c\neq0,
\]

then

\[
y^d\in(\Psi),
\]

and since \(x\in(\Psi)\),

\[
(x,y)^d\subset(\Psi).
\]

So D4 closes with

\[
N=d.
\]

Thus rank-one is not fatal. It merely worsens the exponent.

The finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{after quotienting the detected linear direction, does some row contain a finite-order pure term in the missing direction?}
}
\]

If yes, D4 closes by ideal generation.

---

# 4. If Jacobian rank is zero

If all rows vanish to order at least two in \((x,y)\), inspect the lowest nonzero homogeneous parts:

\[
P_j^{(d_j)}(x,y)
=
\operatorname{gr}_{d_j}^{\mathfrak a}(\Psi_j).
\]

Let

\[
d=\min_j d_j.
\]

Form the homogeneous ideal

\[
I_d=(P_j^{(d_j)}:\ d_j=d)
\subset \mathbb C[x,y].
\]

If the common zero set of all leading homogeneous forms in projective anisotropy space is empty:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V_{\mathbb P^1}(P_j^{(d_j)})=\varnothing,
}
\]

then the ideal generated by the leading forms is \((x,y)\)-primary. Hence

\[
(x,y)^N\subset(P_j^{(d_j)})\subset(\Psi_j)
\]

for some fixed \(N\).

In two variables, this is easy to test: the leading homogeneous polynomials must have no common linear factor.

Equivalently:

\[
\gcd(P_1,\ldots,P_s)=1
\]

in \(\mathbb C[x,y]\).

If their gcd is \(1\), then D4 closes with some \(N\le O(d)\).

If they share a common factor, then there is a projective anisotropy direction undetected at leading order; add more rows or go to higher order.

---

# 5. General finite-codimension criterion

The most compact D4 algebra theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim_{\mathbb C}
\frac{\mathbb C[[x,y]]}{(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)}
<\infty.
}
\]

This is equivalent to saying that the row map has an isolated zero at

\[
(x,y)=(0,0).
\]

If the quotient has finite dimension \(M\), then

\[
\boxed{
(x,y)^M\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
}
\]

So D4 closes with \(N=M\), provided the multiplicity \(M\) is bounded independently of \(Q\) and the certificate coefficients have polynomial bounds.

This gives a clear finite algebra target:

\[
\boxed{
\text{show the anisotropy local algebra has bounded dimension.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Quantitative version

Qualitative ideal containment must become a lower bound.

Suppose

\[
(x,y)^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

with explicit certificate

\[
x^ay^{N-a}
=
\sum_j P_{a,j}(Z;x,y)\Psi_j
\]

and

\[
|P_{a,j}|\le Q^C
\]

on the admissible local domain.

Then

\[
|(x,y)|^N\le Q^C|\Psi|.
\]

Thus

\[
|\Psi|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N.
\]

Returning to rows,

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

and with

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le \frac12 |q_0||u|^3|\Psi|,
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
\frac12Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|^N.
\]

Using Gate 2 pointwise mass,

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and polynomial upper/lower bounds, this is still a polynomial row lower bound for fixed \(N\).

---

# 7. Practical algorithm

Given row formulas for

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,
\]

perform:

## Step 1 — factor non-wall scale

\[
\Psi_j=\frac{A_j^{\rm lead}}{q_0u^3}.
\]

## Step 2 — expand in anisotropy variables

\[
\Psi_j=\sum_{a,b\ge0} c_{j,a,b}(Z)x^ay^b.
\]

## Step 3 — compute Jacobian rank

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi(0).
\]

- If rank \(2\): D4 closes with \(N=1\).
- If rank \(1\): quotient by detected linear direction and inspect missing direction.
- If rank \(0\): go to leading homogeneous forms.

## Step 4 — compute local algebra dimension

Use Gröbner basis in \(\mathbb C(Z)[[x,y]]\) or polynomial truncation.

Find the smallest \(N\) such that

\[
(x,y)^N\subset(\Psi).
\]

## Step 5 — record certificates

For every monomial of degree \(N\), record

\[
x^ay^{N-a}=\sum_jP_{a,j}\Psi_j.
\]

The coefficient sizes determine the exponent loss.

---

# 8. Expected outcome for \(A_7,A_9\)

Expected row map:

\[
\Psi_7=-\frac1{288}x+\cdots,
\]

\[
\Psi_9=-\frac1{288}y+\cdots.
\]

Then

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi(0)
=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\
0&1
\end{pmatrix},
\]

so rank \(2\), \(N=1\), and D4 closes immediately.

If projection modifies this but remains regular, the Jacobian changes by a row operation. Rank should stay \(2\).

If rank drops, the failure is localized and visible:

- one row lost an anisotropy direction;
- projection killed a leading channel;
- exact row architecture differs from \(H\Gamma_G,H\Gamma_{G,2}\).

Then add rows and rerun.

---

# 9. D4 nonlinear theorem, final form

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Anisotropy Local-Algebra Theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\Psi_j=\frac{A_j}{q_0u^3}
\]

after removing canonical errors. Suppose the local anisotropy algebra

\[
\mathcal A_\Gamma
=
\mathbb C[[\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}]]
/
(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

has dimension

\[
\dim\mathcal A_\Gamma\le N_0
\]

uniformly, with polynomially bounded ideal-membership certificates.

Then

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^{N_0}
\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s),
\]

and D4 row conversion follows from Gate 2 pointwise mass.

The matrix theorem is the special case

\[
N_0=1.
\]

---

# 10. Attack result

We have replaced the fragile linearity assumption by a robust finite-codimension condition:

\[
\boxed{
\dim
\mathbb C[[\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}]]
/
(A_7/(q_0u^3),A_9/(q_0u^3),\ldots)
<\infty.
}
\]

If the expected \(A_7,A_9\) principal symbols hold, this dimension is \(1\) and D4 closes at once.

If not, the algorithm identifies exactly which anisotropy direction is missing and which additional row must be added.

This is the cleanest final algebraic attack on D4.

## Continued attack — split D4 into local Nakayama + anisotropy-sphere nonvanishing

The last attack reduced D4 to a local anisotropy ideal condition:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s),
\qquad
\Psi_j=\frac{A_j}{q_0u^3}
\]

after removing canonical residuals. The Jacobian-rank case gives \(N=1\). That is excellent, but it is only a **local** statement near

\[
\Gamma=(0,0).
\]

D4, however, must hold on the whole Gate 2 admissible range:

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

with only polynomial upper bounds. So \(\Gamma\) may be small, moderate, or large. The next attack is to split the anisotropy range and give separate finite certificates.

---

# 1. D4 row map

Let

\[
x=\Gamma_G,
\qquad
y=\Gamma_{G,2},
\qquad
\Gamma=(x,y).
\]

After factoring the non-wall scale,

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi(Z;\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

where

\[
\Psi=(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s),
\]

and \(Z\) denotes the other local variables.

D4 needs:

\[
|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N
\]

or at least

\[
|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

on the Gate 2 admissible region, with canonical residual smaller than half.

The expected matrix case is:

\[
\Psi_7=-\frac1{288}x+\cdots,
\qquad
\Psi_9=-\frac1{288}y+\cdots,
\]

so the local Jacobian is full rank. This is the \(N=1\) case. 

---

# 2. Problem: local rank does not preclude nonlinear zeros away from \(\Gamma=0\)

Even if

\[
J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0)
\]

has rank \(2\), the nonlinear map

\[
\Psi(Z;\Gamma)
\]

could, in principle, vanish again at some nonzero \(\Gamma\).

Example model:

\[
\Psi(x,y)=(x(1-x),y)
\]

has full Jacobian at \(0\), but vanishes at \((1,0)\).

Therefore D4 must be split:

1. **small anisotropy**: handled by local Nakayama / inverse function;
2. **non-small anisotropy**: handled by a finite “anisotropy-sphere” nonvanishing certificate.

---

# 3. Small anisotropy regime

Choose a threshold

\[
|\Gamma|\le r_\Gamma,
\qquad
r_\Gamma=Q^{-C_r}
\]

or a fixed small constant, depending on coefficient sizes.

Assume the Jacobian rank condition:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0))\ge Q^{-C_J}.
\]

Assume second derivative bound in anisotropy variables:

\[
\|\partial_\Gamma^2\Psi\|\le Q^{C_2}
\]

on

\[
|\Gamma|\le r_\Gamma.
\]

Then Taylor gives

\[
\Psi(Z;\Gamma)
=
J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0)\Gamma
+
O(Q^{C_2}|\Gamma|^2).
\]

If

\[
Q^{C_2}|\Gamma|\le \frac12 Q^{-C_J},
\]

then

\[
|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|
\ge
\frac12 Q^{-C_J}|\Gamma|.
\]

So for

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{-C_2-C_J-O(1)},
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This proves D4 in the small-anisotropy region.

---

# 4. Non-small anisotropy regime

Now assume

\[
|\Gamma|\ge r_\Gamma.
\]

Normalize:

\[
\omega=\frac{\Gamma}{|\Gamma|}\in S^1.
\]

Write

\[
\Gamma=r\omega,
\qquad
r=|\Gamma|.
\]

Define the normalized row map

\[
\Phi(Z;r,\omega)
=
\frac{1}{r}\Psi(Z;r\omega).
\]

D4 needs

\[
|\Phi(Z;r,\omega)|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

for

\[
r\in[r_\Gamma,Q^{C_\Gamma}],
\qquad
\omega\in S^1.
\]

This is a compact semialgebraic/analytic nonvanishing problem in finitely many variables.

The finite certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
\Psi(Z;\Gamma)\neq0
\quad
\text{for all }
\Gamma\neq0
\text{ in the admissible anisotropy range.}
}
\]

Quantitatively, need:

\[
\boxed{
\inf_{\mathcal K}
\frac{|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|}{|\Gamma|}
\ge Q^{-C},
}
\]

where \(\mathcal K\) is the polynomially bounded admissible set.

This is the global nonlinear counterpart of the local Jacobian criterion.

---

# 5. Algebraic certificate for non-small anisotropy

If \(\Psi_j\) are polynomial or rational functions in \((Z,x,y)\), clear denominators and define the row ideal

\[
I_\Psi=(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

The bad set is

\[
I_\Psi=0,
\qquad
(x,y)\ne(0,0),
\]

plus the admissibility constraints.

Algebraically, exclude this by saturation:

\[
\boxed{
\sqrt{I_\Psi:(x,y)^\infty}
=
\mathcal R
}
\]

on the admissible branch, or more concretely:

\[
\boxed{
(x,y)^N\subset I_\Psi
}
\]

after localizing by the non-wall and admissibility denominators.

If this holds globally, then no nonzero anisotropy root exists anywhere, not just near \(0\).

Quantitatively, the certificate

\[
x^ay^{N-a}
=
\sum_j P_{a,j}\Psi_j
\]

with

\[
|P_{a,j}|\le Q^C
\]

gives

\[
|\Psi|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N.
\]

This is sufficient because \(|\Gamma|\ge r_\Gamma=Q^{-C_r}\).

So the same ideal-generation theorem actually covers both local and non-small regimes if the certificate is global on the admissible coefficient domain.

---

# 6. Practical two-layer D4 theorem

The robust theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Global Anisotropy Nonvanishing.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi(Z;\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Assume:

## Local certificate

\[
\sigma_{\min}(J_\Gamma\Psi(Z;0))\ge Q^{-C_J}.
\]

This handles small \(\Gamma\).

## Global certificate

On the admissible region with

\[
|\Gamma|\ge Q^{-C_r},
\]

one has

\[
|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C_G}.
\]

This handles non-small \(\Gamma\).

## Residual control

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3|\Psi(Z;\Gamma)|.
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf A_N|\ge \frac12|q_0||u|^3|\Psi|.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

and Gate 2 pointwise mass, this gives a polynomial row lower bound.

---

# 7. How to verify the global certificate without brute force

Use homogeneous leading structure.

If the row map is exactly or effectively linear:

\[
\Psi=L\Gamma+\text{perturbative},
\]

then the global certificate follows from

\[
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

If \(\Psi\) has nonlinear terms, homogenize:

\[
\Psi(Z;r\omega)=r\Psi_1(Z;\omega)+r^2\Psi_2(Z;\omega)+\cdots.
\]

A global zero for \(r\ne0\) requires

\[
\Psi_1+r\Psi_2+\cdots=0.
\]

This is a vector polynomial in \(r\). Use the row-pencil/subresultant test:

\[
P_{Z,\omega}(r)=\Psi_1+r\Psi_2+\cdots.
\]

A common root in

\[
r\in[Q^{-C_r},Q^{C_\Gamma}]
\]

is excluded by the same vector-polynomial nonvanishing machinery:

\[
\operatorname{Sylv}(P_1,\ldots,P_s)
\]

or by ideal saturation.

Thus the global anisotropy problem is the same as the earlier \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil, but in the anisotropy radius \(r\).

---

# 8. Expected simplification for the actual rows

For the expected rows,

\[
\Psi_7=-\frac1{288}x+\text{higher},
\]

\[
\Psi_9=-\frac1{288}y+\text{higher}.
\]

If the higher terms are absent or perturbative, then

\[
|\Psi|\asymp |\Gamma|
\]

globally.

If higher terms exist but all are multiples of \(x,y\), the map can be written

\[
\Psi=M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma.
\]

Then the condition becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(M(Z,\Gamma))\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on the admissible region.

This is the nonlinear effective matrix form. It is often easier than ideal generation.

So the audit should determine whether

\[
\Psi\in (\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

linearly factorizes as

\[
\Psi=M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma.
\]

Since every anisotropy row should vanish when

\[
\Gamma=0,
\]

this factorization is likely.

Then D4 reduces to nonvanishing of the effective matrix

\[
M(Z,\Gamma).
\]

---

# 9. New exact audit target

Given row formulas, compute:

\[
\Psi=\frac{\mathbf A_N}{q_0u^3}
\]

after removing canonical errors.

Then check:

## Check 1

Does

\[
\Psi(0,0)=0?
\]

It should. If not, rows are even stronger.

## Check 2

Can \(\Psi\) be written as

\[
\Psi=M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma?
\]

This is equivalent to

\[
\Psi\in(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

## Check 3

Does

\[
M(Z,\Gamma)
\]

have rank \(2\) with polynomial lower bound on singular value across the admissible range?

If yes, D4 closes globally.

If no, fall back to ideal generation:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

---

# 10. Result of this attack

D4’s final shape is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi(Z;\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
}
\]

The row conversion theorem is true if either:

## Matrix-factor case

\[
\Psi=M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma,
\qquad
\sigma_{\min}(M)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

or:

## Ideal-generation case

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s)
\]

with bounded certificates.

This closes the gap between local linear rank and global nonlinear anisotropy behavior.

So the next concrete audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute whether the effective row map }\Psi
\text{ factors through }(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\text{ with a full-rank matrix factor.}
}
\]

That is the cleanest final D4 algebra target.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 11:30:05)

I’ll push the global D4 algebra target into module language. This avoids depending on one determinant and lets any pair of rows certify rank on each admissible patch.

_Thought for 14s_

## Continued attack — replace one determinant by a Fitting-ideal rank certificate

The current D4 target is:

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi(Z;\Gamma)+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

with

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]

The best case is factorization:

\[
\Psi(Z;\Gamma)=M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma,
\]

with

\[
\sigma_{\min}M(Z,\Gamma)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

But requiring one fixed \(2\times2\) determinant to be nonzero everywhere is unnecessarily rigid. The right object is the **rank-two Fitting ideal** generated by all \(2\times2\) minors of the row map \(M\). This lets different row pairs certify D4 on different parts of the admissible set. This is more robust than the single-pair \(A_7,A_9\) formulation. 

---

# 1. Factor through the anisotropy ideal

Let

\[
x=\Gamma_G,\qquad y=\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Assume the effective row map satisfies

\[
\Psi\in (x,y)\mathcal R^s.
\]

Equivalently, every row vanishes when anisotropy vanishes:

\[
\Psi(Z;0,0)=0.
\]

Then there exists an \(s\times2\) matrix \(M(Z,x,y)\) such that

\[
\boxed{
\Psi=M
\begin{pmatrix}
x\\y
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

This factorization is not unique, but its rank condition is intrinsic modulo row/column changes.

If the rows are true anisotropy rows, this factorization should hold. If some row has a nonzero anisotropy-independent term, that row is even stronger, but it must be checked against canonical errors and whether it is genuinely part of the downstream row system.

---

# 2. Fitting ideal for rank two

Let \(M\) be the \(s\times2\) effective matrix.

Define the rank-two Fitting ideal:

\[
\mathfrak F_2(M)
=
\left(
\Delta_{ij}: 1\le i<j\le s
\right),
\]

where

\[
\Delta_{ij}
=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
M_{i1}&M_{i2}\\
M_{j1}&M_{j2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The condition

\[
\operatorname{rank}M=2
\]

at a point is equivalent to

\[
\exists i<j:\quad \Delta_{ij}\ne0.
\]

So the global D4 rank condition is not

\[
\det L\ne0
\]

for one chosen pair, but:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on the D4 admissible set.

This quantity is the squared exterior area of the two column vectors of \(M\):

\[
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2
=
|M e_1\wedge M e_2|^2.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\det(M^\ast M)
=
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2.
\]

Thus the robust rank certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
\det(M^\ast M)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

---

# 3. D4 lower bound from Fitting rank

If

\[
\det(M^\ast M)\ge Q^{-C_\Delta}
\]

and

\[
\|M\|\le Q^{C_M},
\]

then

\[
\sigma_{\min}(M)^2
=
\lambda_{\min}(M^\ast M)
\ge
\frac{\det(M^\ast M)}{\lambda_{\max}(M^\ast M)}
\ge
Q^{-C_\Delta-2C_M}.
\]

So

\[
\sigma_{\min}(M)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then

\[
|\Psi|
=
|M\Gamma|
\ge
Q^{-C}|\Gamma|.
\]

Since

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

and if

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3|\Psi|,
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3 Q^{-C}|\Gamma|.
\]

Using

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or polynomial upper bounds giving lower bounds for \(u,\Gamma\), D4 follows.

---

# 4. Fitting-rank theorem

The improved theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Fitting-Rank Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3M(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Assume:

1. **Rank-two Fitting lower bound**

\[
\det(M^\ast M)
=
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2
\ge Q^{-C_\Delta}.
\]

2. **Operator upper bound**

\[
\|M\|\le Q^{C_M}.
\]

3. **Canonical residual control**

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3 |M\Gamma|.
\]

4. **D4 sampling**

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\qquad
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This replaces all single-minor determinant checks.

---

# 5. Algebraic way to prove the Fitting lower bound

The Fitting lower bound fails exactly on the rank-drop variety:

\[
\mathcal V(\mathfrak F_2(M)).
\]

So we need to show this rank-drop variety does not meet the D4 admissible set, or only meets it where Gate 2 mass fails.

The algebraic certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
1\in \sqrt{
\mathfrak F_2(M)
+
\mathcal I_{\rm adm}
}
}
\]

where \(\mathcal I_{\rm adm}\) encodes any algebraic equalities defining the admissible branch.

Quantitatively, a more useful certificate is ideal membership of a power of the Gate 2 anisotropy ideal:

\[
\boxed{
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
\mathfrak F_2(M)
+
\mathcal I_{\rm adm}.
}
\]

But since \(\mathfrak F_2(M)\) is scalar-valued, a more direct certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

on the admissible semialgebraic region.

If \(M\) is close to the expected constant matrix

\[
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\
0&1\\
0&0\\
\vdots&\vdots
\end{pmatrix},
\]

this lower bound is immediate.

---

# 6. Expected Fitting certificate

For rows \(A_7,A_9\),

\[
M_{\{7,9\}}
\approx
-\frac1{288}I_2.
\]

Then one minor is

\[
\Delta_{7,9}
\approx
\frac1{288^2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\det(M^\ast M)
\ge
|\Delta_{7,9}|^2
\approx
\frac1{288^4}.
\]

So if all same-scale corrections are smaller than, say,

\[
\frac1{2\cdot 288^2}
\]

at the determinant level, then the Fitting lower bound holds.

Even if \(A_7,A_9\) are partially corrupted, adding rows may restore:

\[
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is the robust row-family advantage.

---

# 7. Residual control in Fitting form

Previously the best residual condition was projected:

\[
|P_{\operatorname{im}M}\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M)|\Gamma|.
\]

In Fitting form, it is enough to require:

\[
|M^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12 |q_0|\,|u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(M^\ast M)|\Gamma|.
\]

Since

\[
\lambda_{\min}(M^\ast M)
\ge
Q^{-C},
\]

this is a pair of scalar estimates:

\[
\boxed{
|\langle M e_1,\mathbf R_{\rm can}\rangle|,
\quad
|\langle M e_2,\mathbf R_{\rm can}\rangle|
\le
Q^{-C}|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Thus D4 has two finite certificates:

1. Fitting rank:

\[
\det(M^\ast M)\ge Q^{-C};
\]

2. normal residual smallness:

\[
|M^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

---

# 8. What to compute from row formulas

Given rows

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,A_N,
\]

do:

## Step 1 — remove canonical residuals

Write

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3\Psi+\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

## Step 2 — factor anisotropy

Try to write

\[
\Psi=M\Gamma.
\]

If not exactly possible, use ideal generation instead. But expected anisotropy rows should factor.

## Step 3 — compute all \(2\times2\) minors

\[
\Delta_{ij}=M_{i1}M_{j2}-M_{i2}M_{j1}.
\]

## Step 4 — prove

\[
\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Step 5 — compute normal residuals

\[
M^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Bound them by the normal-equation threshold.

---

# 9. If factorization fails

If

\[
\Psi\notin(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\mathcal R^s,
\]

then \(\Psi(0)\ne0\). That is actually helpful:

\[
|\Psi|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

may hold even when \(\Gamma=0\).

But such anisotropy-independent terms must be canonical and not projection artifacts. If valid, they make row conversion easier.

If \(\Psi\) vanishes at \(\Gamma=0\) but does not factor cleanly because of rational terms, clear denominators and use the ideal-generation theorem:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N\subset(\Psi_1,\ldots,\Psi_s).
\]

Fitting rank is the \(N=1\) matrix-factor case.

---

# 10. Attack result

D4’s best finite certificate is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3M\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\det(M^\ast M)=\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
|M^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This is invariant under row choices, robust under adding rows, and avoids reliance on one fixed determinant.

It is the final stable algebraic form of D4.

## Continued attack — make the D4 residual vanish by quotienting out constraint rows

We now have the stable D4 algebraic form:

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3M\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm can},
\]

with rank certificate

\[
\det(M^\ast M)=\sum_{i<j}|\Delta_{ij}|^2\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and normal-residual requirement

\[
|M^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

The next attack is to show that much of \(\mathbf R_{\rm can}\) should be **invisible** to \(M^\ast\), because it lives in the row subspace killed by the projection/gauge constraints. In other words, D4 correction control should be proved in a quotient row space, not the raw row space. This is the natural continuation of the Fitting-rank formulation. 

---

# 1. Split the row space into active and constrained directions

Let the finite row vector be

\[
\mathbf A_N\in \mathcal H_{\rm row},
\]

where \(\mathcal H_{\rm row}\) is the finite-dimensional row space spanned by

\[
A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots,A_N.
\]

The \(\mathfrak f\)-projection imposes constraints. Let

\[
\mathcal C\subset \mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

be the constrained/gauge/lower-row subspace removed by projection.

The actual useful row space is the quotient

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm eff}
=
\mathcal H_{\rm row}/\mathcal C.
\]

Let

\[
\pi:\mathcal H_{\rm row}\to \mathcal H_{\rm eff}
\]

be the quotient map.

The D4 row theorem should be stated for

\[
\pi\mathbf A_N,
\]

not necessarily for the raw \(\mathbf A_N\).

---

# 2. Effective leading matrix in the quotient

The row normal form becomes

\[
\pi\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3M_{\rm eff}\Gamma
+
\pi\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Here

\[
M_{\rm eff}
=
\pi M.
\]

The correct rank condition is

\[
\boxed{
\det(M_{\rm eff}^\ast M_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is stronger and cleaner than trying to prove that a particular projected row pair survives.

It says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the anisotropy response has rank two after quotienting out all gauge/lower-row constraints.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Residuals inside the constraint space are harmless

If

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm can}\in \mathcal C,
\]

then

\[
\pi\mathbf R_{\rm can}=0.
\]

So such residuals do not need to be bounded.

This is important for:

- gauge corrections;
- lower-row subtraction terms;
- projection artifacts;
- normalization terms that lie entirely in the constrained row space.

Thus the old correction-control target

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm can}|\le \frac12 |q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M)|\Gamma|
\]

was too strong.

The correct target is

\[
\boxed{
|\pi\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M_{\rm eff})|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Or in normal-equation form,

\[
\boxed{
|M_{\rm eff}^\ast\pi\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac14
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(M_{\rm eff}^\ast M_{\rm eff})
|\Gamma|.
}
\]

---

# 4. Quotient-Fitting D4 theorem

The best D4 theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Quotient-Fitting Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Let

\[
\pi\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3M_{\rm eff}\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm eff}.
\]

Assume:

\[
\det(M_{\rm eff}^\ast M_{\rm eff})\ge Q^{-C_\Delta},
\]

\[
\|M_{\rm eff}\|\le Q^{C_M},
\]

and

\[
|M_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm eff}|
\le
\frac14
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(M_{\rm eff}^\ast M_{\rm eff})
|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
|\pi\mathbf A_N|
\ge
Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Under D4 sampling,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

one gets

\[
\boxed{
|\pi\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Since quotient norm lower bound implies that some actual representative row combination is non-small, this gives the desired downstream row output.

---

# 5. How to compute \(M_{\rm eff}\)

Start with raw effective matrix

\[
M.
\]

Let \(C\) be a matrix whose columns span the constrained row subspace \(\mathcal C\).

Then the quotient projection can be represented by orthogonal projection onto \(\mathcal C^\perp\):

\[
P_{\rm eff}=I-C(C^\ast C)^{-1}C^\ast,
\]

provided

\[
C^\ast C
\]

is polynomially well-conditioned.

Then

\[
M_{\rm eff}=P_{\rm eff}M.
\]

The rank certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the exact quotient-Fitting determinant.

If

\[
C^\ast C
\]

has small eigenvalues, then the projection itself is ill-conditioned and must be audited. That recovers the earlier projection-regularity requirement.

---

# 6. Quotient residual control

The effective residual is

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm eff}=P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

The normal residual is

\[
M_{\rm eff}^\ast\mathbf R_{\rm eff}
=
M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

So the correction audit reduces to two scalar estimates:

\[
\boxed{
M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R_{\rm can}
=
O\!\left(
|q_0||u|^3Q^{-C}|\Gamma|
\right).
}
\]

This is the cleanest correction-control statement.

Any residual in \(\mathcal C\) is annihilated by \(P_{\rm eff}\). Any residual orthogonal to \(\operatorname{im}M_{\rm eff}\) is annihilated by \(M_{\rm eff}^\ast\).

Therefore the genuinely dangerous residual is only:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\operatorname{im}M_{\rm eff}}P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
}
\]

Everything else is harmless.

---

# 7. Finite audit protocol

Given exact row/projection formulas:

## Step 1 — build the raw anisotropy matrix

Compute

\[
M
\]

from

\[
\mathbf A_N/(q_0u^3)
\]

by extracting the effective \((\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\)-response, or more generally the effective anisotropy map.

## Step 2 — build the constraint matrix

List lower-row/gauge/projection constraints and form

\[
C.
\]

## Step 3 — verify projection regularity

Check

\[
\lambda_{\min}(C^\ast C)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Step 4 — compute quotient rank

Compute

\[
G_{\rm eff}=M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M.
\]

Prove

\[
\det G_{\rm eff}\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This replaces all ad hoc determinant checks.

## Step 5 — compute quotient normal residual

Compute

\[
M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R_{\rm can}.
\]

Prove it is below the normal-equation threshold.

---

# 8. Interpretation of possible outcomes

| Outcome | Meaning |
|---|---|
| \(\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)\ge Q^{-C}\) | anisotropy response survives projection |
| \(\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)=0\) but \(\det(M^\ast M)\neq0\) | projection kills an essential anisotropy direction |
| \(C^\ast C\) singular | projection/gauge scheme ill-conditioned |
| \(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}R\) small | corrections harmless |
| \(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}R\) large | correction has dangerous active component; absorb into \(M\) or add rows |

---

# 9. Advantage over prior D4 statements

Earlier D4 required proving something like:

\[
|\mathbf R|\le\frac12|\text{leading}|.
\]

Now D4 requires only:

\[
\boxed{
|M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R|\le\text{small}.
}
\]

This is much weaker.

It also respects the actual structure of \(\mathfrak f\)-projection: corrections introduced purely to satisfy constraints should not be counted as dangerous if they lie in the constrained subspace.

---

# 10. Final sharpened D4 target

The strongest current D4 finite certificate is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3M\Gamma+\mathbf R,
}
\]

with constraint projection

\[
P_{\rm eff}=I-C(C^\ast C)^{-1}C^\ast,
\]

such that:

\[
\boxed{
\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)\ge Q^{-C},
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R|
\le
\frac14
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)
|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then D4 follows.

This is now a quotient-space, projection-aware, correction-minimal formulation.

I’ll continue by attacking the quotient-space D4 certificate from the other side: instead of projecting away constraint rows, choose test functionals that annihilate constraints and detect the two anisotropy channels directly.

_Thought for 9s_

## Continued attack — dual test-functionals for D4

The quotient-Fitting form was:

\[
\pi \mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3M_{\rm eff}\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\rm eff},
\]

with the quotient certificate

\[
\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and

\[
|M^\ast P_{\rm eff}\mathbf R|\ll |q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

A more executable dual form is to construct two row-space test functionals

\[
\ell_1,\ell_2
\]

that:

1. annihilate the constrained/gauge/lower-row subspace;
2. detect \(\Gamma_G\) and \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) independently;
3. kill or suppress residual channels.

This avoids explicitly building the quotient projection matrix.

---

# 1. Dual quotient formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

be the finite row space, and let

\[
\mathcal C\subset\mathcal H_{\rm row}
\]

be the constrained subspace removed by \(\mathfrak f\)-projection.

Instead of using

\[
P_{\rm eff},
\]

choose functionals

\[
\ell_1,\ell_2\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}^\ast
\]

such that

\[
\ell_a|_{\mathcal C}=0.
\]

Then \(\ell_a\) descend to the quotient

\[
\mathcal H_{\rm row}/\mathcal C.
\]

Apply them to the row vector:

\[
B_a:=\ell_a(\mathbf A_N).
\]

If

\[
\mathbf A_N
=
q_0u^3M\Gamma+\mathbf R,
\]

then

\[
B_a
=
q_0u^3\,\ell_a(M\Gamma)+\ell_a(\mathbf R).
\]

Define the \(2\times2\) dual response matrix

\[
K_{ab}:=\ell_a(Me_b),
\qquad
e_1=(1,0)^T,\quad e_2=(0,1)^T.
\]

Then

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
B_1\\
B_2
\end{pmatrix}
=
q_0u^3K\Gamma
+
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_1(\mathbf R)\\
\ell_2(\mathbf R)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

D4 reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\det K\not\approx0
}
\]

and residual control in these two scalar channels.

---

# 2. Dual D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Dual-Functional Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Suppose there exist two functionals

\[
\ell_1,\ell_2
\]

with

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal C)=0
\]

such that

\[
K=
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_1(Me_1)&\ell_1(Me_2)\\
\ell_2(Me_1)&\ell_2(Me_2)
\end{pmatrix}
\]

satisfies

\[
|\det K|\ge Q^{-C_K},
\]

\[
\|K\|\le Q^{C_U}.
\]

Assume residual control:

\[
\left|
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_1(\mathbf R)\\
\ell_2(\mathbf R)
\end{pmatrix}
\right|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(K)|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
\left|
\begin{pmatrix}
B_1\\
B_2
\end{pmatrix}
\right|
\ge
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(K)|\Gamma|.
\]

Since

\[
|(B_1,B_2)|
\le
\|\ell\|\,|\mathbf A_N|,
\]

and if

\[
\|\ell_1\|+\|\ell_2\|\le Q^{C_\ell},
\]

we get

\[
|\mathbf A_N|
\ge
Q^{-C}
|q_0|\,|u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Then D4 follows from the usual pointwise assumptions:

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

plus either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma}.
\]

---

# 3. Why dual functionals are easier

The quotient matrix

\[
P_{\rm eff}=I-C(C^\ast C)^{-1}C^\ast
\]

requires a full metric and constraint projection. Dual functionals only need to satisfy linear equations:

\[
\ell_a(C_j)=0
\]

for each constraint row \(C_j\).

Then choose \(\ell_1,\ell_2\) so that their action on the anisotropy responses is simple.

Expected ideal choice:

\[
\ell_1(A_7)=1,\quad \ell_1(A_9)=0,
\]

\[
\ell_2(A_7)=0,\quad \ell_2(A_9)=1,
\]

modulo the constraint space.

If \(A_7,A_9\) survive in the quotient, then

\[
K\approx -\frac1{288}I_2.
\]

This directly proves D4.

---

# 4. Finite construction of \(\ell_1,\ell_2\)

Let row basis be

\[
R_1,\ldots,R_s.
\]

Let constraint rows span columns of a matrix

\[
C\in\mathbb C^{s\times r}.
\]

Let anisotropy response vectors be

\[
v_1=Me_1,\qquad v_2=Me_2.
\]

We need functionals \(\ell_a\in\mathbb C^s\) satisfying:

\[
\ell_a^\ast C=0.
\]

Thus

\[
\ell_a\in \ker C^\ast.
\]

Inside \(\ker C^\ast\), choose \(\ell_1,\ell_2\) dual to the projected anisotropy vectors

\[
P_{\ker C^\ast}v_1,\quad P_{\ker C^\ast}v_2.
\]

This is possible iff

\[
P_{\ker C^\ast}v_1,\quad P_{\ker C^\ast}v_2
\]

are independent.

That condition is exactly:

\[
\det(M^\ast P_{\rm eff}M)\neq0.
\]

So dual functionals are equivalent to quotient-Fitting, but easier to implement.

---

# 5. Residual annihilation by construction

Now exploit the freedom in \(\ell_a\).

Suppose residual channels span a subspace

\[
\mathcal R_0\subset\mathcal H_{\rm row}.
\]

If possible, choose

\[
\ell_a
\]

such that

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal C)=0,
\]

and also

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal R_0)=0.
\]

Then dangerous residual channels vanish exactly.

This is stronger than merely bounding residuals.

The condition is that the two anisotropy response vectors remain independent after quotienting by both constraints and residual channels:

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\frac{\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2\}}
{\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0}
\right)
=2.
\]

Equivalently, if \(P_{\perp(\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0)}\) is projection away from constraints and residuals,

\[
P v_1,\quad P v_2
\]

must remain independent.

This yields a stronger theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Find two test functionals killing constraints and leading residual channels while detecting both anisotropy directions.}
}
\]

Then D4 correction control becomes almost automatic.

---

# 6. D4 residual-killing theorem

Let

\[
\mathcal C
\]

be the constraint subspace and

\[
\mathcal R_0
\]

be the span of non-small residual channels.

Assume there exist two functionals

\[
\ell_1,\ell_2
\]

such that:

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal C)=0,
\]

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal R_0)=0,
\]

and

\[
K_{ab}=\ell_a(Me_b)
\]

satisfies

\[
|\det K|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then only residual channels outside \(\mathcal R_0\) remain, and by definition those are perturbative.

Thus D4 follows with much weaker correction estimates.

This is the most surgical correction-control method.

---

# 7. Practical audit protocol

Given row formulas:

## Step 1 — compute anisotropy response vectors

\[
v_1=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_G}(\mathbf A_N),
\]

\[
v_2=\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}}(\mathbf A_N).
\]

## Step 2 — list constraint directions

Construct basis vectors for:

- lower-row projection constraints;
- gauge constraints;
- normalization constraints;
- any row combinations declared irrelevant downstream.

Call their span \(\mathcal C\).

## Step 3 — list non-small residual channels

Any residual channel not already known to be perturbative goes into \(\mathcal R_0\).

## Step 4 — quotient test

Check whether \(v_1,v_2\) are independent modulo

\[
\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0.
\]

In matrix form:

\[
\operatorname{rank}
P_{\perp(\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0)}
[v_1\ v_2]
=2.
\]

## Step 5 — construct dual functionals

Solve linear equations:

\[
\ell_a(\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0)=0,
\]

\[
\ell_a(v_b)=\delta_{ab}
\]

up to scaling.

## Step 6 — estimate remaining residuals

Only residuals not in \(\mathcal R_0\) need size bounds.

---

# 8. If the quotient test fails

If \(v_1,v_2\) are not independent modulo \(\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0\), then one of the residual directions is consuming an anisotropy channel.

Options:

1. Do not kill that residual; instead absorb it into the effective leading map \(M\).
2. Add more rows \(A_{11},A_{13},\ldots\).
3. Use nonlinear anisotropy ideal generation rather than linear D4.
4. If all fail, D4 needs a new row/invariant.

Thus the test gives an exact diagnostic.

---

# 9. Attack result

D4 can be reformulated as the existence of two dual test functionals:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_1,\ell_2\in\mathcal H_{\rm row}^\ast
}
\]

such that:

\[
\ell_a(\text{constraints})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_a(\text{non-small residual channels})=0,
\]

and

\[
\det(\ell_a(v_b))\neq0.
\]

This is the cleanest correction-control formulation yet.

It turns D4 into a finite linear algebra problem over the row space:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2\}
\cap
(\mathcal C+\mathcal R_0)
=
\{0\}
}
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

If true, D4 correction control is essentially solved.
