## Continued attack — make the downstream row output a quotient-energy invariant

The last fork was:

\[
\text{raw/ambient row energy}
\quad\text{vs.}\quad
\mathfrak f\text{-projected row energy}.
\]

The clean way to remove this ambiguity is to define the downstream row output as a **quotient row-energy norm**. Then raw rows, projected rows, and any well-conditioned row coordinates are just different frames for the same object. This makes D4 independent of a fragile choice of row presentation. 

---

# 1. Define the row space and nuisance subspace

Let

\[
\mathcal H_N
=
\operatorname{span}\{R_7,R_9,R_{11},R_{13},\ldots,R_N\}
\]

be the finite raw/ambient downstream row space.

Let

\[
\mathcal C_N\subset\mathcal H_N
\]

be the nuisance subspace generated by:

\[
\text{gauge rows},
\quad
\text{lower-row constraints},
\quad
\text{normalization artifacts},
\quad
\text{rows explicitly quotiented out by }\mathfrak f.
\]

Define the effective row space:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Q_N=\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N.
}
\]

For a row vector \(R\in\mathcal H_N\), write its class as

\[
[R]\in\mathcal Q_N.
\]

Choose a row inner product on \(\mathcal H_N\). Then define the quotient norm:

\[
\boxed{
\|[R]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
=
\inf_{C\in\mathcal C_N}
\|R+C\|_{\mathcal H_N}.
}
\]

Equivalently, if \(P_{\mathcal C^\perp}\) is orthogonal projection away from \(\mathcal C_N\),

\[
\|[R]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
=
\|P_{\mathcal C^\perp}R\|.
\]

This is the canonical downstream row-energy norm.

---

# 2. D4 should prove quotient row energy

Instead of targeting a specific coordinate vector, D4 should prove

\[
\boxed{
\int_I
\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}^2\,dm
\ge
Q^{-C}|I|.
}
\]

This is stronger than saying “some row coordinate is nonzero” but weaker and more invariant than demanding a specific projected row pair.

If a projected row system is a frame for \(\mathcal Q_N\), this quotient energy is equivalent to projected row energy.

If raw/ambient rows are admissible, this quotient energy is already enough.

So D4 should output:

\[
\boxed{
\text{quotient row energy, not coordinate-specific row energy.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Frame equivalence theorem

Suppose the \(\mathfrak f\)-projected rows define a linear map

\[
P_{\mathfrak f}:\mathcal H_N\to \mathbb C^s
\]

that vanishes on \(\mathcal C_N\), hence descends to

\[
\bar P_{\mathfrak f}:\mathcal Q_N\to\mathbb C^s.
\]

If \(\bar P_{\mathfrak f}\) has polynomial frame bounds:

\[
\boxed{
Q^{-C}\|[R]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\le
\|\bar P_{\mathfrak f}[R]\|
\le
Q^C\|[R]\|_{\mathcal Q_N},
}
\]

then quotient row energy and projected row energy are equivalent:

\[
\int_I\|\bar P_{\mathfrak f}[\mathbf R_N]\|^2
\ge
Q^{-C}
\int_I\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}^2.
\]

Thus if downstream insists on projected coordinates, the only required audit is a **frame lower bound** on \(\bar P_{\mathfrak f}\).

This is exactly the previous anisotropy-plane coercivity, but stated at the correct row-output level.

---

# 4. Anisotropy response in the quotient

The raw D4 expansion is:

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm raw}\Gamma
+
\mathbf E_{\rm raw}.
\]

Pass to the quotient:

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
q_0u^3[L_{\rm raw}\Gamma]
+
[\mathbf E_{\rm raw}].
\]

Let

\[
\bar L_N:
\mathbb C^2\to\mathcal Q_N
\]

be the quotient anisotropy response:

\[
\boxed{
\bar L_N\Gamma=[L_{\rm raw}\Gamma].
}
\]

Then the D4 rank condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_2(\bar L_N)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Equivalently, if

\[
v_1=L_{\rm raw}e_1,
\qquad
v_2=L_{\rm raw}e_2,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
[\!v_1\!]\wedge[\!v_2\!]\ne0
\quad\text{in }\wedge^2\mathcal Q_N,
}
\]

with polynomial lower bound.

For expected raw rows:

\[
v_1=-\frac1{288}e_7,
\qquad
v_2=-\frac1{288}e_9.
\]

So D4 quotient rank says:

\[
\boxed{
[e_7],[e_9]\text{ remain independent in }\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N.
}
\]

That is the true structural condition.

---

# 5. Quotient D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Quotient Row-Energy Conversion.}
}
\]

Assume:

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
q_0u^3\bar L_N\Gamma
+
[\mathbf E_N].
\]

Assume rank:

\[
\sigma_2(\bar L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

Assume quotient residual control:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\operatorname{im}\bar L_N}[\mathbf E_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_2(\bar L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\ge
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3
\sigma_2(\bar L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

Under the D4 sampling hypotheses,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

this gives

\[
\boxed{
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

By row mobility,

\[
\boxed{
\int_I\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C'}|I|.
}
\]

This is the final row-energy output.

---

# 6. Coordinate versions are corollaries

## Raw/ambient version

If

\[
\mathcal C_N=0
\]

or if the raw norm is accepted directly, then

\[
\mathcal Q_N=\mathcal H_N
\]

and D4 is the raw exterior-rank theorem.

## Projected version

If projected rows are required, prove that

\[
\bar P_{\mathfrak f}:\mathcal Q_N\to \mathbb C^s
\]

has a polynomial lower frame bound.

It suffices to check this on the anisotropy plane:

\[
\mathcal V_{\rm aniso}
=
\operatorname{span}\{[v_1],[v_2]\}.
\]

Namely:

\[
\boxed{
|\bar P_{\mathfrak f}[v_1]\wedge \bar P_{\mathfrak f}[v_2]|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

If this holds, projected row energy follows from quotient row energy.

---

# 7. The final D4 finite audit

D4 now needs exactly these finite checks.

## Audit 1 — raw principal symbols

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## Audit 2 — quotient independence

Check:

\[
[e_7]\wedge[e_9]\ne0
\quad
\text{in }\wedge^2(\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N).
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\operatorname{span}\{e_7,e_9\}\cap\mathcal C_N=\{0\}
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

## Audit 3 — quotient residual

Control only the quotient projection of the residual:

\[
P_{\operatorname{im}\bar L_N}[\mathbf E_N].
\]

Residuals lying in \(\mathcal C_N\) vanish. Residuals orthogonal to the anisotropy image are harmless.

## Audit 4 — coordinate frame, if needed

If projected rows are required, show:

\[
\bar P_{\mathfrak f}
\]

has polynomial frame lower bound on the quotient anisotropy plane.

---

# 8. Why this settles the raw/projected ambiguity

The question was:

\[
\text{raw, quotient, or projected row energy?}
\]

The answer is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the canonical target should be quotient row energy.}
}
\]

Raw row energy is a representative norm.

Projected row energy is a coordinate/frame norm.

Both are acceptable if they are polynomially equivalent to the quotient norm on the anisotropy response plane.

Thus D4 no longer depends on choosing raw or projected rows prematurely.

---

# 9. Result of this attack

D4 is now an invariant row-space theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raw principal anisotropy responses }e_7,e_9
\text{ survive in the quotient row space.}
}
\]

Together with quotient residual control, this gives row energy.

The final D4 statement should be:

\[
\boxed{
R_7\sim H\Gamma_G,\quad R_9\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\quad
H\sim-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\quad
[e_7],[e_9]\text{ independent in }\mathcal Q_N
}
\]

\[
\Longrightarrow
\boxed{
\text{D4 row-energy lower bound}.
}
\]

This is the cleanest resolution of the raw/projected-row fork.

## One step further — D4 can be stated as a quotient **Gram determinant** on two canonical row classes

The previous endpoint was:

\[
R_7\sim H\Gamma_G,\qquad R_9\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\qquad H\sim-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\]

and D4 should target quotient row energy in

\[
\mathcal Q_N=\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N.
\]

The extra step is this: D4 does not need the full quotient row space. It only needs the \(2\times2\) quotient Gram determinant of the two canonical row classes

\[
[e_7],\ [e_9]\in\mathcal Q_N.
\]

So the whole row-span/projection/coercivity problem collapses to one finite Gram determinant. 

---

# 1. Canonical D4 quotient Gram

Let

\[
G_Q=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle [e_7],[e_7]\rangle_{\mathcal Q_N}
&
\langle [e_7],[e_9]\rangle_{\mathcal Q_N}
\\
\langle [e_9],[e_7]\rangle_{\mathcal Q_N}
&
\langle [e_9],[e_9]\rangle_{\mathcal Q_N}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then the D4 quotient-rank condition is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
[e_7]\wedge[e_9]\ne0
\quad\text{in }\wedge^2\mathcal Q_N
}
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

This is the cleanest finite audit. It replaces:

- raw/projection ambiguity;
- denominator audits;
- global projection regularity;
- arbitrary row-pair selection.

Everything reduces to whether \(e_7,e_9\) survive independently after quotienting out nuisance rows.

---

# 2. D4 leading response in quotient form

Using the raw symbols,

\[
R_7=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G+\cdots,
\]

\[
R_9=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}+\cdots.
\]

Passing to the quotient gives

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3
\left(
\Gamma_G[e_7]+\Gamma_{G,2}[e_9]
\right)
+
[\mathbf E_N].
\]

Therefore

\[
\left\|
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3
\left(
\Gamma_G[e_7]+\Gamma_{G,2}[e_9]
\right)
\right\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\ge
\frac1{288}
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(G_Q)^{1/2}
|\Gamma|.
\]

If

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C},
\qquad
\|G_Q\|\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\lambda_{\min}(G_Q)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

So the leading quotient row is polynomially non-small.

---

# 3. Minimal D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Quotient-Gram Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. Raw principal symbols:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

2. Quotient Gram nondegeneracy:

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

3. Quotient residual control:

\[
\left\|
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}
[\mathbf E_N]
\right\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\le
\frac{1}{576}
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(G_Q)^{1/2}
|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\ge
Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Under

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\qquad
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

this gives

\[
\boxed{
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}\ge Q^{-C'}.
}
\]

Then row mobility gives integrated quotient row energy.

---

# 4. What must actually be computed

Only three finite objects:

## A. Raw symbols

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7),
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9).
\]

Expected:

\[
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\qquad
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

## B. Quotient Gram determinant

If \(C\) is the matrix whose columns span \(\mathcal C_N\), then

\[
G_Q
=
E^\ast P_{\mathcal C^\perp}E,
\]

where

\[
E=(e_7,e_9),
\]

and

\[
P_{\mathcal C^\perp}
=
I-C(C^\ast C)^{-1}C^\ast.
\]

Equivalently, by Schur complement:

\[
\det G_Q
=
\frac{
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
C^\ast C & C^\ast E\\
E^\ast C & E^\ast E
\end{pmatrix}
}{
\det(C^\ast C)
}.
\]

So compute:

\[
\boxed{
\det(C^\ast C)
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
C^\ast C & C^\ast E\\
E^\ast C & E^\ast E
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

This is the precise quotient-rank audit.

## C. Quotient residual projection

Compute only

\[
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}
[\mathbf E_N].
\]

Residuals in \(\mathcal C_N\) vanish.

Residuals quotient-orthogonal to \([e_7],[e_9]\) cannot cancel the leading D4 term.

---

# 5. Anything extra?

The only remaining “extra” issue is whether \([e_7],[e_9]\) are the correct canonical row classes. If the row construction later shows that the real principal symbols are not exactly \(e_7,e_9\), replace them by the actual two principal response classes:

\[
[w_1]=\left[\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_G}\mathbf R_N\right],
\]

\[
[w_2]=\left[\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}}\mathbf R_N\right].
\]

Then use the same theorem with

\[
G_Q(w)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle[w_1],[w_1]\rangle & \langle[w_1],[w_2]\rangle\\
\langle[w_2],[w_1]\rangle & \langle[w_2],[w_2]\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

So the absolute final D4 audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\det G_Q(w)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is invariant under changing row coordinates, adding rows, or switching raw/projected frames.

---

# 6. Final reduction

D4 has now been reduced to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raw principal symbols}
+
\text{quotient Gram determinant}
+
\text{quotient residual projection}.
}
\]

No broader projection regularity is needed.

No specific projected row pair is needed.

No full residual norm bound is needed.

The exact next computation is:

\[
\boxed{
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
C^\ast C & C^\ast E\\
E^\ast C & E^\ast E
\end{pmatrix}
/
\det(C^\ast C)
}
\]

for the two principal row classes \(E=(e_7,e_9)\), or their actual response-class replacements \(w_1,w_2\).

## Continued attack — quotient residual projection can be killed by choosing the quotient representative

We reduced D4 to three objects:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7),\qquad \operatorname{gr}_5(R_9),
\]

\[
G_Q=\left(\langle [e_i],[e_j]\rangle_{\mathcal Q_N}\right)_{i,j\in\{7,9\}},
\]

and the quotient-projected residual

\[
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}[\mathbf E_N].
\]

The next attack is to reduce the last object. If the quotient representative is chosen optimally, the dangerous residual is not an arbitrary correction: it is exactly the component of the residual in the two-dimensional quotient anisotropy plane. This can be eliminated or minimized by a normal-form choice. 

---

# 1. Current D4 quotient form

In the quotient row space

\[
\mathcal Q_N=\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N,
\]

we have

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3
\left(
\Gamma_G[e_7]+\Gamma_{G,2}[e_9]
\right)
+
[\mathbf E_N].
\]

Let

\[
W=\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}\subset\mathcal Q_N.
\]

D4 needs

\[
\left\|P_W[\mathbf E_N]\right\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\le
\frac1{576}
|q_0||u|^3
\lambda_{\min}(G_Q)^{1/2}
|\Gamma|.
\]

The attack is to replace the raw residual \([\mathbf E_N]\) by a **normal-form residual** that is quotient-orthogonal to \(W\).

---

# 2. Absorb all \(W\)-components into the effective leading map

Any residual component lying in \(W\) has the form

\[
P_W[\mathbf E_N]
=
\alpha_7[e_7]+\alpha_9[e_9].
\]

If \(\alpha_7,\alpha_9\) are linear in \(\Gamma\) and scale like \(q_0u^3\Gamma\), they are not true errors. They modify the effective leading matrix.

More generally, define

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
q_0u^3 M_{\rm eff}(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma
+
[\mathbf E_N^{\perp}],
\]

where the effective matrix \(M_{\rm eff}\) is chosen so that

\[
P_W[\mathbf E_N^{\perp}]=0
\]

for all same-scale \(\Gamma\)-linear contributions.

Then the D4 residual condition becomes automatic for the entire same-scale \(W\)-component.

Thus the correction-control rule is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any residual in }W\text{ that is not genuinely small must be absorbed into }M_{\rm eff}.
}
\]

Only the residual quotient-orthogonal to \(W\), plus genuinely small \(W\)-residuals, remains.

---

# 3. Effective quotient Gram after absorption

After absorption, the leading map is no longer necessarily

\[
-\frac1{288}I_2.
\]

It is

\[
M_{\rm eff}(Z,\Gamma)
=
-\frac1{288}I_2+\Delta M(Z,\Gamma)
\]

acting from the anisotropy vector into \(W\).

The quotient Gram condition becomes

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}
\left(
M_{\rm eff}^{\ast}G_QM_{\rm eff}
\right)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the coordinate-free version of “the effective D4 leading matrix is nonsingular.”

If

\[
\|\Delta M\|_{G_Q}\le \frac12\cdot\frac1{288}\lambda_{\min}(G_Q)^{1/2},
\]

then the bound follows immediately.

If \(\Delta M\) is not small, D4 can still close if

\[
\det M_{\rm eff}\not\approx0.
\]

So the exact determinant to compute is

\[
\boxed{
\det(M_{\rm eff}^{\ast}G_QM_{\rm eff})
=
|\det M_{\rm eff}|^2\det G_Q.
}
\]

Since \(\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}\), D4 reduces to

\[
\boxed{
|\det M_{\rm eff}|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Normal-form D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Quotient Normal-Form Theorem.}
}
\]

Suppose in \(\mathcal Q_N\),

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
q_0u^3 E M_{\rm eff}\Gamma
+
[\mathbf E_\perp]
+
[\mathbf E_{\rm small}],
\]

where

\[
E=([e_7]\ [e_9])
\]

maps \(\mathbb C^2\to W\),

\[
[\mathbf E_\perp]\perp W,
\]

and

\[
[\mathbf E_{\rm small}]\in W
\]

is genuinely small.

Assume:

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C_Q},
\]

\[
|\det M_{\rm eff}|\ge Q^{-C_M},
\]

and

\[
\|[\mathbf E_{\rm small}]\|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3
\sigma_{\min}(E M_{\rm eff})
|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\ge
Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Under D4 sampling hypotheses, this gives row energy.

This is stronger than the previous theorem because it does not ask that the original residual’s \(W\)-component be small; it only asks that the unabsorbed \(W\)-component be small.

---

# 5. How to construct \(M_{\rm eff}\)

Given the quotient row class \([\mathbf R_N]\), define the \(W\)-projection:

\[
P_W[\mathbf R_N].
\]

Since \(E:\mathbb C^2\to W\) is an isomorphism when \(\det G_Q\neq0\), write

\[
P_W[\mathbf R_N]
=
E\,\Theta(Z,\Gamma).
\]

Here

\[
\Theta(Z,\Gamma)\in\mathbb C^2.
\]

If

\[
\Theta(Z,\Gamma)
\]

is divisible by

\[
q_0u^3
\]

and by the anisotropy ideal \((\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\), then define

\[
M_{\rm eff}\Gamma
=
\frac{1}{q_0u^3}\Theta(Z,\Gamma).
\]

If \(\Theta\) is linear in \(\Gamma\), this is an ordinary matrix.

If \(\Theta\) is nonlinear in \(\Gamma\), then D4 becomes a nonlinear anisotropy map:

\[
\Theta=q_0u^3\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma),
\]

and the condition becomes

\[
|\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma)|\ge Q^{-C}|\Gamma|^N.
\]

So the normal form automatically tells us whether D4 is linear or nonlinear.

---

# 6. Final obstruction after normal form

After quotient-normalization, D4 can fail only in two ways.

## Obstruction 1 — quotient Gram degeneracy

\[
\det G_Q\approx0.
\]

Meaning:

\[
[e_7],[e_9]
\]

do not survive independently in the quotient row space.

This is a row-space architecture failure.

## Obstruction 2 — effective anisotropy map degeneracy

\[
\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma)\approx0
\]

for some nonzero admissible \(\Gamma\).

In the linear case:

\[
\det M_{\rm eff}\approx0.
\]

In the nonlinear case:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\not\subset
(\Phi_1,\Phi_2).
\]

This is an anisotropy-map failure.

Everything else is orthogonal or perturbative.

---

# 7. Finite audit, final form

The exact D4 audit now has four steps.

## Step 1 — quotient Gram

Compute

\[
G_Q=E^\ast P_{\mathcal C^\perp}E
\]

or Schur complement:

\[
G_Q
=
E^\ast E
-
E^\ast C(C^\ast C)^{-1}C^\ast E.
\]

Verify

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Step 2 — quotient projection of row vector

Compute

\[
P_W[\mathbf R_N].
\]

Represent it as

\[
P_W[\mathbf R_N]=E\Theta(Z,\Gamma).
\]

## Step 3 — factor the D4 scale

Check whether

\[
\Theta(Z,\Gamma)=q_0u^3M_{\rm eff}(Z,\Gamma)\Gamma
\]

or, more generally,

\[
\Theta=q_0u^3\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma).
\]

## Step 4 — anisotropy nonvanishing

Linear case:

\[
|\det M_{\rm eff}|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Nonlinear case:

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^N
\subset
(\Phi_1,\Phi_2)
\]

with bounded certificates.

Then D4 closes, modulo canonical small residuals.

---

# 8. What this reveals

The most important new insight is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 residual control is not an independent estimate if we define the effective leading map using the quotient projection.}
}
\]

The only true residuals are:

1. components outside the quotient anisotropy plane, which cannot cancel;
2. canonical/tail/projection errors that are not captured by the row normal form;
3. genuinely small \(W\)-components left after factoring \(q_0u^3\Phi_{\rm eff}\).

Thus D4 is fundamentally a **quotient anisotropy-map nonvanishing theorem**, not a raw correction estimate.

---

# 9. Final reduction

D4 is now reduced to:

\[
\boxed{
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma)\not\approx0
\quad
\text{for }\Gamma\neq0.
}
\]

In the expected linear case:

\[
\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma)
=
-\frac1{288}\Gamma,
\]

so the second condition is automatic.

Therefore the expected final D4 proof is:

1. raw principal symbols identify \([e_7],[e_9]\);
2. quotient Gram shows \([e_7],[e_9]\) survive;
3. quotient projection gives

   \[
   \Phi_{\rm eff}=-\frac1{288}I_2
   \]

   plus perturbative canonical errors.

That is the cleanest possible form.

## One step further — D4 can be reduced to a **two-scalar quotient coordinate identity**

The previous endpoint was:

\[
[\mathbf R_N]
=
q_0u^3 E\Phi_{\rm eff}(\Gamma)
+
[\mathbf E_\perp]
+
[\mathbf E_{\rm small}],
\]

where

\[
E=([e_7]\ [e_9])
\]

spans the quotient anisotropy plane

\[
W=\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}\subset\mathcal Q_N.
\]

The extra step is: instead of working with the full quotient row vector, choose the **dual quotient coordinates** associated to \([e_7],[e_9]\). Then D4 becomes two scalar identities. This is the most compressed form of the row conversion problem. 

---

# 1. Dual coordinates on the quotient anisotropy plane

Let

\[
G_Q=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle [e_7],[e_7]\rangle & \langle [e_7],[e_9]\rangle\\
\langle [e_9],[e_7]\rangle & \langle [e_9],[e_9]\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Assume

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then \([e_7],[e_9]\) form a well-conditioned basis for \(W\).

Define dual functionals

\[
\ell_7,\ell_9: \mathcal Q_N\to\mathbb C
\]

by

\[
\ell_i([e_j])=\delta_{ij},
\qquad i,j\in\{7,9\},
\]

and

\[
\ell_i(W^\perp)=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(x)\\
\ell_9(x)
\end{pmatrix}
=
G_Q^{-1}
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle [e_7],x\rangle\\
\langle [e_9],x\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Since \(G_Q^{-1}\) is polynomially bounded, these functionals are polynomially controlled.

---

# 2. Apply dual coordinates to the row vector

Apply

\[
(\ell_7,\ell_9)
\]

to the quotient row class:

\[
[\mathbf R_N]\in\mathcal Q_N.
\]

Define

\[
B_7:=\ell_7([\mathbf R_N]),
\qquad
B_9:=\ell_9([\mathbf R_N]).
\]

Then the D4 leading term should become

\[
\boxed{
B_7
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
+
\text{controlled error},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B_9
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\text{controlled error}.
}
\]

This is the scalar-coordinate version of D4.

It is stronger as a proof tool because it removes every row-space direction orthogonal to the useful quotient anisotropy plane.

---

# 3. D4 scalar-coordinate theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Quotient Coordinate Theorem.}
}
\]

Assume there are quotient dual coordinates \(B_7,B_9\) such that

\[
B_7
=
q_0u^3\left(\alpha_{77}\Gamma_G+\alpha_{79}\Gamma_{G,2}\right)
+
\epsilon_7,
\]

\[
B_9
=
q_0u^3\left(\alpha_{97}\Gamma_G+\alpha_{99}\Gamma_{G,2}\right)
+
\epsilon_9.
\]

Let

\[
M_Q=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_{77}&\alpha_{79}\\
\alpha_{97}&\alpha_{99}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

If

\[
|\det M_Q|\ge Q^{-C_M},
\]

and

\[
\left(
|\epsilon_7|^2+|\epsilon_9|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M_Q)|\Gamma|,
\]

then

\[
\left(|B_7|^2+|B_9|^2\right)^{1/2}
\ge
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M_Q)|\Gamma|.
\]

Since the dual coordinate map is polynomially bounded below/above on \(W\), this implies

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}\ge Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Then D4 follows from the usual sample hypotheses.

---

# 4. Expected scalar identities

In the expected raw-symbol case,

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If the quotient coordinates are normalized by \([e_7],[e_9]\), then

\[
\boxed{
M_Q=
-\frac1{288}I_2.
}
\]

So

\[
|\det M_Q|=\frac1{288^2}.
\]

This means the entire D4 rank issue is reduced to verifying the scalar identities:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7([\mathbf R_N])
+
\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
=
\epsilon_7,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9([\mathbf R_N])
+
\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
=
\epsilon_9,
}
\]

with

\[
|\epsilon|\le \frac{1}{576}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

---

# 5. Why this is better than quotient Gram alone

The quotient Gram condition only says

\[
[e_7],[e_9]
\]

survive.

The scalar-coordinate formulation also tells us exactly what to compute:

1. project the row class into quotient coordinates;
2. read off the two scalar leading coefficients;
3. bound two scalar errors.

So D4 no longer needs a vector residual estimate. It needs two coordinate residual estimates.

That is the smallest possible correction-control statement.

---

# 6. Exact finite audit now

The finite D4 audit is:

## Audit A — quotient basis

Compute

\[
G_Q
\]

for \([e_7],[e_9]\), and prove

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Audit B — quotient coordinates

Compute

\[
B_7=\ell_7([\mathbf R_N]),
\qquad
B_9=\ell_9([\mathbf R_N]).
\]

## Audit C — scalar principal terms

Verify

\[
\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_G}(B_7)=-\frac1{288},
\]

\[
\operatorname{Coeff}_{q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}}(B_9)=-\frac1{288},
\]

and cross coefficients are either zero or included in \(M_Q\) with

\[
|\det M_Q|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

## Audit D — scalar residuals

Prove

\[
|\epsilon_7|+|\epsilon_9|
\le
\frac1{576}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

This is now a two-scalar residual bound.

---

# 7. Final “anything extra” issue

There is one remaining subtlety: the quotient coordinates \(\ell_7,\ell_9\) may depend on \(m\). If the quotient subspace \(\mathcal C_N(m)\) varies with \(m\), then:

- D4 pointwise row conversion is unaffected;
- row mobility needs derivative bounds for \(B_7,B_9\) or for the quotient norm.

So the final extra audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{quotient frame variation is polynomially bounded on }I.
}
\]

That is,

\[
\|\partial_m G_Q\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1},
\]

and

\[
\partial_m\ell_7,\ \partial_m\ell_9
\]

are polynomially bounded.

If row mobility is applied to the quotient norm directly, we need

\[
\partial_m\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}
\]

controlled. This follows from polynomial bounds on:

\[
\partial_m \mathbf R_N,
\qquad
\partial_m P_{\mathcal C^\perp},
\]

or equivalently on the quotient frame.

So the last extra condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{row quotient frame has polynomial derivative complexity.}
}
\]

This is local bookkeeping, not a new arithmetic input.

---

# 8. Final reduced D4 package

The strongest final D4 form is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Two-Coordinate Quotient Conversion.}
}
\]

There exist quotient coordinates \(B_7,B_9\) such that

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
B_7\\
B_9
\end{pmatrix}
=
q_0u^3M_Q
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
+
\epsilon,
\]

with

\[
|\det M_Q|\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and

\[
|\epsilon|\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M_Q)|\Gamma|.
\]

Then

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}\ge Q^{-C}|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
\]

Under D4 sampling, this yields the row lower bound and, by row mobility plus quotient-frame derivative bounds, row energy.

So yes: the extra step is that D4 can be reduced from row-vector estimates to **two scalar quotient-coordinate identities** plus **quotient-frame derivative control** for mobility.

## Continued attack — freeze quotient coordinates to avoid moving-frame derivative loss

The prior endpoint was the two-coordinate quotient D4 form:

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
B_7\\
B_9
\end{pmatrix}
=
q_0u^3M_Q
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
+\epsilon,
\]

with

\[
|\det M_Q|\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and

\[
|\epsilon|\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_{\min}(M_Q)|\Gamma|.
\]

The remaining nuisance was that the quotient basis

\[
[e_7](m),\ [e_9](m)
\]

and the dual coordinates

\[
\ell_7(m),\ \ell_9(m)
\]

may vary with \(m\). Row mobility would then require derivative bounds for the quotient frame.

The next attack removes that requirement: **freeze the quotient coordinates at the D4 sample point**. This converts row mobility into scalar mobility for two fixed linear functionals.

---

# 1. D4 sample and frozen duals

Suppose D4 gives a sample point

\[
m_\ast\in I
\]

such that in the quotient row space

\[
\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)
\]

we have

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

Assume the quotient Gram at \(m_\ast\) is nondegenerate:

\[
\det G_Q(m_\ast)\ge Q^{-C_Q}.
\]

Define dual quotient coordinates at this single point:

\[
\ell_7^\ast,\ell_9^\ast:\mathcal H_N\to\mathbb C,
\]

by

\[
\ell_i^\ast(\mathcal C_N(m_\ast))=0,
\]

\[
\ell_i^\ast(e_j)=\delta_{ij},
\qquad i,j\in\{7,9\},
\]

after quotienting at \(m_\ast\).

These are now **fixed row-space functionals**, not moving functions of \(m\).

Their norms satisfy

\[
\|\ell_7^\ast\|+\|\ell_9^\ast\|
\le Q^{C_\ell}
\]

because

\[
G_Q(m_\ast)^{-1}
\]

is polynomially bounded.

---

# 2. Fixed scalar row observables

Define scalar observables on nearby \(m\):

\[
B_7^\ast(m)=\ell_7^\ast(\mathbf R_N(m)),
\]

\[
B_9^\ast(m)=\ell_9^\ast(\mathbf R_N(m)).
\]

At \(m=m_\ast\), the D4 quotient-coordinate theorem gives

\[
\left(
|B_7^\ast(m_\ast)|^2+
|B_9^\ast(m_\ast)|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

Now we only need derivative bounds for the raw row vector:

\[
\|\partial_m\mathbf R_N(m)\|
\le Q^{C_D}|I|^{-1}.
\]

Then

\[
|(B_i^\ast)'(m)|
\le
\|\ell_i^\ast\|\cdot\|\partial_m\mathbf R_N(m)\|
\le Q^{C_\ell+C_D}|I|^{-1}.
\]

So the fixed scalar observables are Lipschitz with polynomial constants.

---

# 3. Frozen-coordinate thickening theorem

Let

\[
B^\ast(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
B_7^\ast(m)\\
B_9^\ast(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

If

\[
|B^\ast(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_A},
\]

and

\[
|(B^\ast)'(m)|\le Q^{C_B}|I|^{-1},
\]

then for

\[
|m-m_\ast|
\le
\frac12Q^{-C_A-C_B}|I|,
\]

we have

\[
|B^\ast(m)|\ge \frac12Q^{-C_A}.
\]

Thus there exists an interval

\[
J_\ast\subset I,
\qquad
|J_\ast|\ge Q^{-C_A-C_B-O(1)}|I|,
\]

such that

\[
|B^\ast(m)|\ge \frac12Q^{-C_A}
\quad
(m\in J_\ast).
\]

Then

\[
\int_{J_\ast}|B^\ast(m)|^2\,dm
\ge
Q^{-2C_A-O(1)}|J_\ast|
\ge
Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

Since

\[
|B^\ast(m)|
\le
\|\ell^\ast\|\,\|\mathbf R_N(m)\|
\le
Q^{C_\ell}\|\mathbf R_N(m)\|,
\]

we get raw row energy:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I\|\mathbf R_N(m)\|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
}
\]

This proves row-energy mobility without any moving quotient-frame derivative estimates.

---

# 4. What if downstream needs quotient energy, not raw energy?

If downstream requires

\[
\int_I\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}^2\,dm,
\]

then fixed functionals alone do not automatically imply quotient energy at nearby \(m\), because

\[
\ell_i^\ast
\]

annihilate

\[
\mathcal C_N(m_\ast),
\]

not necessarily

\[
\mathcal C_N(m).
\]

There are two ways to handle this.

## Option A — raw/ambient row energy is admissible

Then the frozen-coordinate argument is enough. No moving quotient control is required.

## Option B — quotient/projected energy is required

Need a modest stability theorem:

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl(\mathcal C_N(m),\mathcal C_N(m_\ast)\bigr)
\le
Q^C\frac{|m-m_\ast|}{|I|}.
\]

On

\[
J_\ast
\]

with

\[
|m-m_\ast|\le Q^{-C'}|I|,
\]

this gives

\[
\operatorname{dist}\bigl(\mathcal C_N(m),\mathcal C_N(m_\ast)\bigr)
\le Q^{-C''}
\]

after choosing \(C'\) large.

Then the fixed duals remain approximately quotient-valid:

\[
\ell_i^\ast(\mathcal C_N(m))=O(Q^{-C''}).
\]

Consequently,

\[
|B^\ast(m)|
\le
Q^C\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}
+
\text{small error}.
\]

Thus the same thickening yields quotient row energy.

So, if projected/quotient energy is required, the last needed estimate is only **Lipschitz motion of the nuisance subspace** \(\mathcal C_N(m)\), not full moving-frame derivative control.

---

# 5. Frozen-frame row mobility theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Frozen-Frame Row Mobility.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. D4 gives at \(m_\ast\)

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

2. Quotient Gram at \(m_\ast\) is nondegenerate:

\[
\det G_Q(m_\ast)\ge Q^{-C_Q}.
\]

3. Raw row derivative bound:

\[
\|\partial_m\mathbf R_N\|\le Q^{C_D}|I|^{-1}.
\]

Then there exist fixed scalar row functionals

\[
\ell_7^\ast,\ell_9^\ast
\]

and an interval

\[
J_\ast\subset I,
\qquad
|J_\ast|\ge Q^{-C}|I|,
\]

such that

\[
\int_{J_\ast}
\left(
|\ell_7^\ast(\mathbf R_N(m))|^2+
|\ell_9^\ast(\mathbf R_N(m))|^2
\right)\,dm
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

Therefore raw row energy satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\int_I\|\mathbf R_N(m)\|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
}
\]

If additionally \(\mathcal C_N(m)\) varies Lipschitzly with \(m\), then quotient/projected row energy also satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\int_I\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
}
\]

---

# 6. Why this helps

Previously row mobility seemed to require:

\[
\partial_m G_Q,\quad
\partial_m\ell_7,\quad
\partial_m\ell_9,
\]

or derivative bounds for a moving quotient frame.

Now:

- for raw row energy, no quotient-frame derivative is needed;
- for quotient/projected row energy, only the nuisance subspace \(\mathcal C_N(m)\) needs Lipschitz motion;
- the fixed functionals \(\ell_i^\ast\) reduce everything to ordinary scalar Lipschitz estimates.

This is substantially easier.

---

# 7. Remaining finite audits after this step

The downstream row-energy path now needs:

## D4 pointwise conversion

At \(m_\ast\):

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

This comes from:

\[
\det G_Q(m_\ast)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

raw symbols,

\[
R_7\sim -\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
R_9\sim -\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

and residual control.

## Row derivative bound

\[
\|\partial_m\mathbf R_N\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

This is a local complexity/row formula estimate.

## Optional quotient-frame stability

Only if the final architecture demands quotient/projected energy:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(\mathcal C_N(m),\mathcal C_N(m_\ast))
\le Q^C\frac{|m-m_\ast|}{|I|}.
\]

---

# 8. Result of this attack

The row mobility layer can be made almost independent of moving quotient geometry.

The strongest practical statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 pointwise quotient row lower bound}
+
\text{fixed dual coordinates at }m_\ast
+
\text{raw row Lipschitz}
\Rightarrow
\text{raw row energy}.
}
\]

If raw row energy is acceptable, the row-output problem is solved.

If projected/quotient energy is mandatory, add one finite stability audit for the nuisance subspace.

This is the cleanest current form of row-energy mobility.

## Continued attack — quotient-frame stability via a moving-subspace derivative bound

The last remaining row-mobility nuisance was this:

\[
\mathcal Q_N(m)=\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N(m)
\]

may vary with \(m\). We froze dual coordinates at the D4 sample \(m_\ast\), which is enough for raw row energy. If the final downstream layer specifically requires **quotient/projected row energy**, we need to know that the nuisance space \(\mathcal C_N(m)\) does not rotate too fast.

This can be handled by a standard projection-derivative estimate. So the row-mobility layer can be closed under a finite Gram-gap condition for the constraint rows. 

---

# 1. Constraint frame

Let

\[
C(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_1(m)&\cdots&c_r(m)
\end{pmatrix}
\]

be a matrix whose columns span the nuisance subspace

\[
\mathcal C_N(m)\subset \mathcal H_N.
\]

Assume the constraint Gram matrix

\[
G_C(m)=C(m)^\ast C(m)
\]

is polynomially well-conditioned:

\[
\boxed{
\lambda_{\min}(G_C(m))\ge Q^{-C_G},
\qquad
\|G_C(m)\|\le Q^{C_G}.
}
\]

Assume also a derivative bound:

\[
\boxed{
\|C'(m)\|\le Q^{C_C}|I|^{-1}.
}
\]

Then the orthogonal projection onto the nuisance space is

\[
P_C(m)=C(m)(C(m)^\ast C(m))^{-1}C(m)^\ast.
\]

The effective quotient projection is

\[
P_Q(m)=I-P_C(m).
\]

---

# 2. Projection derivative bound

Differentiate:

\[
P_C=C G_C^{-1}C^\ast.
\]

Then

\[
P_C'
=
C'G_C^{-1}C^\ast
+
C(G_C^{-1})'C^\ast
+
CG_C^{-1}(C^\ast)'.
\]

Also,

\[
(G_C^{-1})'
=
-G_C^{-1}G_C'G_C^{-1},
\]

and

\[
G_C'=C'^\ast C+C^\ast C'.
\]

Using the polynomial bounds,

\[
\|G_C^{-1}\|\le Q^{C_G},
\]

\[
\|G_C'\|\le Q^{C_G+C_C}|I|^{-1},
\]

so

\[
\|(G_C^{-1})'\|
\le Q^{3C_G+C_C}|I|^{-1}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\|P_C'(m)\|\le Q^{C_P}|I|^{-1}
}
\]

for some polynomial exponent \(C_P\). Since \(P_Q'= -P_C'\),

\[
\boxed{
\|P_Q'(m)\|\le Q^{C_P}|I|^{-1}.
}
\]

This is exactly the moving-quotient stability needed for row mobility.

---

# 3. Quotient norm stability

For any row vector \(R(m)\),

\[
\|[R(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}
=
\|P_Q(m)R(m)\|.
\]

Let

\[
Y(m)=P_Q(m)R(m).
\]

Then

\[
Y'(m)=P_Q'(m)R(m)+P_Q(m)R'(m).
\]

Assume row-size and row-derivative complexity:

\[
\|R(m)\|\le Q^{C_R},
\]

\[
\|R'(m)\|\le Q^{C_D}|I|^{-1}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\|Y'(m)\|\le Q^{C_Y}|I|^{-1}.
}
\]

Thus quotient row norm is Lipschitz at polynomial scale.

---

# 4. Quotient row-mobility theorem

Suppose D4 gives a point

\[
m_\ast\in I
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
}
\]

Let

\[
Y(m)=P_Q(m)\mathbf R_N(m).
\]

Then

\[
|Y(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

Using

\[
|Y'(m)|\le Q^{C_Y}|I|^{-1},
\]

we get, on

\[
|m-m_\ast|
\le
\frac12Q^{-C_A-C_Y}|I|,
\]

that

\[
|Y(m)|\ge \frac12Q^{-C_A}.
\]

Therefore there exists

\[
J_\ast\subset I,
\qquad
|J_\ast|\ge Q^{-C}|I|,
\]

such that

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}
\ge Q^{-C}
\quad
(m\in J_\ast).
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
\int_I
\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C'}|I|.
}
\]

This closes row mobility in the moving quotient norm.

---

# 5. Relation to frozen dual coordinates

The frozen-dual argument gave raw row energy without moving-frame control. The moving-projection argument gives quotient/projected energy when needed.

So row mobility now has two versions:

## Raw-energy mobility

Requires only:

\[
\|\mathbf R_N'(m)\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

## Quotient-energy mobility

Requires additionally:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(C^\ast C)\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
\|C'(m)\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

Both are local finite audits.

---

# 6. Projected-frame corollary

If the projected rows define a frame

\[
\bar P_{\mathfrak f}(m):\mathcal Q_N(m)\to \mathbb C^s
\]

with polynomial lower bound

\[
\|\bar P_{\mathfrak f}(m)[R]\|
\ge Q^{-C}\|[R]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)},
\]

then quotient row energy implies projected row energy:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I
\|\bar P_{\mathfrak f}(m)[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
}
\]

So if final downstream contradiction is written in projected coordinates, it suffices to prove this frame lower bound. Again, it only needs to hold on the row-output quotient, not on all raw rows.

---

# 7. Final mobility package

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Moving-Quotient Row Mobility.}
}
\]

Assume:

1. D4 pointwise quotient lower bound:

\[
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C_A}.
\]

2. Constraint-frame conditioning:

\[
\lambda_{\min}(C^\ast C)\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

3. Constraint-frame derivative bound:

\[
\|C'\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

4. Row derivative bound:

\[
\|\mathbf R_N'\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\int_I
\|[\mathbf R_N(m)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m)}^2\,dm
\ge Q^{-C'}|I|.
}
\]

If projected coordinates have a frame lower bound, projected row energy follows.

---

# 8. New final row-output chain

The downstream row side is now:

\[
\text{Gate 2}
+
\text{Wall escape}
+
\text{D4 pointwise quotient conversion}
\Rightarrow
\exists m_\ast:
\|[\mathbf R_N(m_\ast)]\|_{\mathcal Q_N(m_\ast)}
\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Then:

\[
\text{moving-quotient row mobility}
\Rightarrow
\int_I\|[\mathbf R_N]\|_{\mathcal Q_N}^2
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

Then, if needed:

\[
\text{projected-frame lower bound}
\Rightarrow
\int_I\|\mathbf A_N^{\mathfrak f}\|^2
\ge Q^{-C}|I|.
\]

So the row-output architecture is no longer ambiguous.

---

# 9. What remains after this step

The only row-side finite audits left are:

1. **D4 quotient conversion at a point**

   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_5(R_7),\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9),
   \quad
   \det G_Q\ge Q^{-C},
   \quad
   \text{quotient residual small}.
   \]

2. **Constraint-frame stability**

   \[
   \lambda_{\min}(C^\ast C)\ge Q^{-C},
   \quad
   \|C'\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
   \]

3. **Row derivative bound**

   \[
   \|\mathbf R_N'\|\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
   \]

4. **Projected-frame lower bound**, only if projected coordinate energy is mandatory.

All are finite local checks, not new global arithmetic principles.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 13:15:39)

_Thought for 27s_

Yes, but with a split:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the analytic bounds come from zeta/completed }\Xi;
\quad
\text{the row identities come from the paper’s local row algebra.}
}
\]

You should **not** try to derive \(R_7,R_9,A_7,A_9\) directly from zeta zeros. Zeta gives the canonical source \(q''_{\can}\), the log-derivative/curvature bounds, and the identities for \(g,h\). The D4 row checks come from the paper’s finite-jet / Gram / scattering / projection construction.

## 1. What can be derived from zeta

From the completed function \(\Xi\), or whatever exact completed object the paper uses, we can derive the canonical local source:

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

Then the downstream variables are defined by

\[
g=\Gamma_G=q''-6q+2q^3=u-6q+2q^3,
\]

\[
h=\Gamma_{G,2}=2q(q')^2-\frac16g''.
\]

Those identities are analytic/local differential identities. Zeta also supplies the zero-separated local complexity estimates:

\[
\sup_I |u|+\sup_I |\mathcal D u|\le Q^C,
\]

and, with primitive normalization or completed-meromorphic formulas, bounds for

\[
q,\ q',\ q'',\ldots
\]

needed by Gate 2 and wall escape. This is the Agent 1 lane.

So from zeta we derive:

\[
u,\quad g,\quad h,\quad \text{local derivative bounds},\quad \text{ordinary zero-separated control}.
\]

We do **not** derive the row symbols

\[
A_7,\ A_9,\ R_7,\ R_9
\]

from zeta alone.

---

## 2. What must be derived from the paper’s row algebra

The checks we need are row-construction checks. They should be derived from the paper definitions of the downstream rows.

The expected architecture is:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

\[
H=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}+\mathcal R_H.
\]

If these are literal leading expansions, then substituting \(H\) gives

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
+
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G
+
\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
+
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\mathcal R_9.
\]

Thus the principal D4 matrix is

\[
L_{\rm lead}
=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
0&c_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

This is exactly the finite row-symbol check that has repeatedly appeared in the agent handoffs. The current files state this as the expected row architecture and identify D4 as finite once these row expansions and half-leading correction bounds are verified. 

So from the paper we need to derive:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

That is done by taking the exact definitions of \(A_7,A_9,H\) in the paper and expanding them in the D4 filtration.

---

## 3. Exact derivation procedure

Use this filtration:

\[
\operatorname{wt}(q_0)=1,
\qquad
\operatorname{wt}(u)=1,
\qquad
\operatorname{wt}(\Gamma_G)=1,
\qquad
\operatorname{wt}(\Gamma_{G,2})=1.
\]

Then

\[
q_0u^3\Gamma
\]

has weight \(5\).

The derivation is:

### Step A — derive \(H\)

Take the paper’s exact definition of \(H\), usually stated as the row coupling factor or obstruction prefactor, and compute its weight-\(4\) symbol:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_4(H).
\]

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_4(H)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3.
}
\]

This is not a zeta computation. It is a local Taylor/normal-form computation in the paper’s row algebra.

### Step B — derive raw row symbols

Take the exact row definitions for \(R_7,R_9\) or \(A_7,A_9\), before projection if possible.

Compute:

\[
\Pi_5(R_7)
=
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
\quad\text{projected to }
q_0u^3\Gamma_G,\ q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

and same for \(R_9\).

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

Equivalently, the directional responses are

\[
v_1=-\frac1{288}e_7,
\qquad
v_2=-\frac1{288}e_9.
\]

Then

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|
=
\frac1{288^2}.
\]

So the raw exterior rank is automatic. This is exactly why the D4 task is now finite/local rather than a zeta-arithmetic problem. 

### Step C — check projection or quotient

If the paper’s final rows are \(\mathfrak f\)-projected rows, define the nuisance subspace

\[
\mathcal C_N.
\]

Then compute whether

\[
[e_7],[e_9]
\]

survive independently in

\[
\mathcal Q_N=\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N.
\]

This is the quotient Gram check:

\[
G_Q=
\left(
\langle [e_i],[e_j]\rangle
\right)_{i,j\in\{7,9\}},
\]

with required bound

\[
\boxed{
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

If the paper gives a projection map \(P_{\mathfrak f}\), the equivalent check is:

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\mathfrak f}e_7\wedge P_{\mathfrak f}e_9|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is not a zeta calculation either. It is a finite row-space/projection calculation from the paper’s definitions.

### Step D — residuals

After subtracting the principal terms,

\[
E_7
=
R_7+\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
E_9
=
R_9+\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

we only need the residual component in the leading quotient plane:

\[
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}[E].
\]

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}[E]
\right\|
\le
\frac1{576}
|q_0||u|^3|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Residuals in the nuisance subspace vanish. Residuals quotient-orthogonal to \([e_7],[e_9]\) cannot cancel the leading D4 term. This is why we no longer need full residual norm control. 

---

## 4. Can this be derived from the current draft alone?

Only if the current draft contains the exact definitions of:

\[
H,\quad A_7,\quad A_9,\quad \mathfrak f\text{-projection},\quad \mathcal C_N.
\]

If the paper only says schematically:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G,
\qquad
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

then the draft is **not enough**. We need to go back to the definitions that produced those rows.

The current handoffs show the desired expansions and the closure condition, but they do not contain the exact raw row formula or the nuisance subspace basis. They repeatedly identify the missing step as a finite row-symbol computation: compute the weight-\(5\) symbols of \(R_7,R_9\), then check projection/coercivity or quotient Gram. turn83file10

---

## 5. Best derivation path

The most efficient derivation is not “from zeta.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{paper definitions}
\rightarrow
\text{local algebra expansion}
\rightarrow
\text{associated graded symbols}
\rightarrow
\text{quotient Gram / residual projection}.
}
\]

Concretely:

1. Extract the exact definitions of \(A_7,A_9,H\) from the paper.
2. Normalize rows so constants \(c_7,c_9\) are explicit.
3. Compute \(\operatorname{gr}_4(H)\).
4. Compute \(\operatorname{gr}_5(A_7),\operatorname{gr}_5(A_9)\).
5. Identify any same-weight cross terms:
   \[
   q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ in }A_7,
   \qquad
   q_0u^3\Gamma_G\text{ in }A_9.
   \]
   If present, include them in the \(2\times2\) matrix \(L\).
6. Check:
   \[
   \det L\neq0.
   \]
7. If projected rows are used, compute the quotient Gram for \([e_7],[e_9]\).
8. Compute only the quotient-projected residual.

---

## 6. What zeta still contributes

Zeta/completed \(\Xi\) contributes the analytic legitimacy:

\[
q''_{\can}
\]

is canonical,

\[
u,\ u',\ q,\ q'
\]

are locally bounded under zero separation,

and Gate 2 identities are valid. It does **not** decide whether \(A_7\) has principal symbol \(H\Gamma_G\); that is decided by the row construction.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zeta gives the source and bounds;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{the paper’s row algebra gives the D4 row symbols.}
}
\]

---

## 7. What to ask the next agent to compute

The next agent should not do broad strategy. It should do this exact finite derivation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Given the paper definitions of }H,A_7,A_9,
\text{ compute }
\operatorname{gr}_4(H),
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_7),
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_9).
}
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Given the projection/nuisance definition, compute }
\det G_Q
\text{ for }[e_7],[e_9].
}
\]

That is the missing finite derivation.

## Computation from the stated architecture

Given:

\[
H:=yq''_{\can},
\]

and the stated leading behavior

\[
H\sim-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\qquad u=q''_{\can},
\]

we get the D4 weight-\(4\) symbol:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_4(H)
=
-\frac{1}{288}q_0u^3.
}
\]

Given the row architecture:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=c_7H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}=c_9H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9,
\]

substitution gives:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
+
c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G
+
\mathcal R_7,
\]

\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
+
c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\mathcal R_9.
\]

Therefore, if

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7)=0,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9)=0,
\]

then:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_7^{\mathfrak f})
=
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

That gives the leading D4 matrix:

\[
\boxed{
L_{\rm lead}
=
-\frac1{288}
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
0&c_9
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

So if

\[
|c_7|,\ |c_9|\ge Q^{-C},
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L_{\rm lead})
\ge
\frac1{288}\min(|c_7|,|c_9|)
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is the exact computation available from the uploaded architecture. 

---

## If same-order correction terms exist

Write:

\[
\mathcal R_H=q_0u^3\rho_H+\widehat{\mathcal R}_H,
\]

\[
\mathcal R_7
=
q_0u^3(a_{77}\Gamma_G+a_{79}\Gamma_{G,2})
+
\widehat{\mathcal R}_7,
\]

\[
\mathcal R_9
=
q_0u^3(a_{97}\Gamma_G+a_{99}\Gamma_{G,2})
+
\widehat{\mathcal R}_9.
\]

Then the true effective matrix is:

\[
\boxed{
L=
\begin{pmatrix}
-\frac{c_7}{288}+c_7\rho_H+a_{77} & a_{79}\\
a_{97} & -\frac{c_9}{288}+c_9\rho_H+a_{99}
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

The determinant audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\det L
=
\left(-\frac{c_7}{288}+c_7\rho_H+a_{77}\right)
\left(-\frac{c_9}{288}+c_9\rho_H+a_{99}\right)
-
a_{79}a_{97}.
}
\]

D4’s principal rank is certified if:

\[
\boxed{
|\det L|\ge Q^{-C},
\qquad
\|L\|\le Q^C.
}
\]

The residual is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf R=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\widehat{\mathcal R}_H\Gamma_G+\widehat{\mathcal R}_7\\
c_9\widehat{\mathcal R}_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\widehat{\mathcal R}_9
\end{pmatrix}.
}
\]

The required half-leading bound is:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R|
\le
\frac12
|q_0|\,|u|^3\,\sigma_{\min}(L)\,|\Gamma|.
}
\]

This matches the row-conversion closure condition already isolated in the uploaded D4 notes. 

---

## What I cannot certify from the current files

The exact finite checks require data not present in the uploaded context:

### 1. Raw row formulas

Need exact formulas for:

\[
R_7,\quad R_9
\]

or exact projected formulas for:

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f},\quad A_9^{\mathfrak f}.
\]

Without them, I cannot prove:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(\mathcal R_7)=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_5(\mathcal R_9)=0,
\]

nor compute \(a_{77},a_{79},a_{97},a_{99}\).

### 2. Exact formula for \(y\) or \(H\)

The files state:

\[
H=yq''_{\can}\sim-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}.
\]

To derive rather than assume:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_4(H)=-\frac{q_0u^3}{288},
\]

we need the exact definition/expansion of \(y\). In effect, we need to show:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_3(y)=-\frac{q_0u^2}{288}.
\]

### 3. Projection/nuisance data

To compute quotient Gram:

\[
G_Q=
E^\ast P_{\mathcal C^\perp}E,
\qquad
E=(e_7,e_9),
\]

we need a basis for the nuisance subspace:

\[
\mathcal C_N.
\]

Without that, I cannot certify:

\[
\det G_Q\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

### 4. Residual projection

Need explicit residuals:

\[
\mathbf E_N
=
\mathbf R_N
+
\frac1{288}q_0u^3(\Gamma_Ge_7+\Gamma_{G,2}e_9)
\]

modulo nuisance rows. Without \(\mathbf E_N\), I cannot compute:

\[
P_{\operatorname{span}\{[e_7],[e_9]\}}[\mathbf E_N].
\]

---

## Bottom line

From the uploaded architecture, the computation is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_4(H)
=
-\frac{1}{288}q_0u^3
}
\]

and therefore

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_7^{\mathfrak f})
=
-\frac{c_7}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G
+
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7),
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(A_9^{\mathfrak f})
=
-\frac{c_9}{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9).
}
\]

So D4 closes if the row definitions prove:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_7\mathcal R_H\Gamma_G+\mathcal R_7)=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_5(c_9\mathcal R_H\Gamma_{G,2}+\mathcal R_9)=0,
}
\]

or, more generally, if all same-order terms form an effective matrix \(L\) with

\[
\boxed{
\sigma_{\min}(L)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

The missing input is not zeta. It is the exact row/projection formulas from the paper’s local algebra.

## Agent 5 / E — D4 row-span audit: raw exterior rank, projection coercivity, projected residuals

Instruction applied: D4 only. I am treating Gate 2 and wall escape as upstream inputs and auditing the finite row-span conversion layer. 

---

# 1. D4 target

Let

\[
\Gamma=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

D4 should be formulated as a finite row-span theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N,
\qquad
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

The row vector may be raw/ambient rows or projected rows:

\[
\mathbf A_N=(A_7,A_9,A_{11},A_{13},\ldots)^T.
\]

The correction condition should be projected into the leading anisotropy image:

\[
\boxed{
|P_L\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12 |q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|,
}
\]

where

\[
P_L=P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}.
\]

Full residual norm control is unnecessary.

---

# 2. Raw / ambient row audit

Define the denominator-free raw or ambient row vector

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm raw}
=
(R_7,R_9,R_{11},R_{13},\ldots)^T.
\]

Extract its D4 principal anisotropy response:

\[
\boxed{
\mathbf R_{\rm raw}
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm raw}\Gamma+\mathbf E_{\rm raw}.
}
\]

For each row \(R_j\),

\[
\Pi_5(R_j)
=
q_0u^3
\bigl(\ell_{j1}\Gamma_G+\ell_{j2}\Gamma_{G,2}\bigr),
\]

so

\[
L_{\rm raw}=(\ell_{ja})_{j,a}.
\]

The expected raw symbols are:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

Thus

\[
v_1=L_{\rm raw}e_1=-\frac1{288}e_7,
\]

\[
v_2=L_{\rm raw}e_2=-\frac1{288}e_9,
\]

and therefore

\[
\boxed{
|v_1\wedge v_2|=\frac1{288^2}.
}
\]

So the raw exterior-rank condition is closed if the raw symbol identities are literal.

---

# 3. Raw exterior-rank certificate

The denominator-free rank certificate is

\[
\boxed{
|v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{i<j}
|\ell_{i1}\ell_{j2}-\ell_{i2}\ell_{j1}|^2
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

For the expected \(R_7,R_9\) pair, the single minor already gives:

\[
|\ell_{7,1}\ell_{9,2}-\ell_{7,2}\ell_{9,1}|
=
\frac1{288^2}.
\]

So if raw/ambient row energy is acceptable downstream, D4 reduces to:

1. verify the two raw principal symbols;
2. bound the residual only in the raw leading image.

No projection denominator audit is needed.

---

# 4. Raw row-energy D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Raw Exterior Row Conversion.}
}
\]

Assume

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm raw}
=
q_0u^3L_{\rm raw}\Gamma+\mathbf E_{\rm raw},
\]

\[
\sigma_2(L_{\rm raw})\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_{\rm raw}}\mathbf E_{\rm raw}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\rm raw})|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf R_{\rm raw}|
\ge
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\rm raw})|\Gamma|.
\]

Under D4 sample hypotheses,

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

this gives

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf R_{\rm raw}|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Projected-row variant

If the final downstream architecture requires \(\mathfrak f\)-projected rows, define

\[
P_{\mathfrak f}:\mathcal R_{\rm raw}\to\mathcal R_{\mathfrak f}.
\]

D4 does not require full projection regularity. It only requires coercivity on the two-dimensional anisotropy response plane

\[
\mathcal V_{\rm aniso}
=
\operatorname{span}(v_1,v_2).
\]

The exact condition is:

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\mathfrak f}w|
\ge Q^{-C}|w|
\quad
(w\in\mathcal V_{\rm aniso}).
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\mathfrak f}v_1\wedge P_{\mathfrak f}v_2|
\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is much weaker than requiring all projection denominators to be globally regular.

If this wedge condition holds, the projected anisotropy response has rank two.

If it fails, then either:

1. projection kills one anisotropy direction;
2. projection identifies the two directions;
3. the row family must be enlarged to \(A_{11},A_{13},\ldots\);
4. or the proof must use raw/ambient/quotient row energy instead of projected coordinates.

---

# 6. Projected D4 theorem

Let

\[
L_{\mathfrak f}
=
P_{\mathfrak f}L_{\rm raw}.
\]

Assume

\[
\sigma_2(L_{\mathfrak f})\ge Q^{-C_L}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
|P_{\mathfrak f}v_1\wedge P_{\mathfrak f}v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

Projected rows satisfy

\[
\mathbf A_{\mathfrak f}
=
q_0u^3L_{\mathfrak f}\Gamma+\mathbf R_{\mathfrak f}.
\]

The residual condition is:

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_{\mathfrak f}}\mathbf R_{\mathfrak f}|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\mathfrak f})|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Then

\[
|\mathbf A_{\mathfrak f}|
\ge
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_{\mathfrak f})|\Gamma|,
\]

and D4 follows under the same sample hypotheses.

---

# 7. Denominator handling

If projection denominators exist, do **not** start with a global denominator audit.

First compute the two projected directional responses:

\[
\widetilde v_1=P_{\mathfrak f}v_1,
\]

\[
\widetilde v_2=P_{\mathfrak f}v_2.
\]

Then test:

\[
\boxed{
|\widetilde v_1\wedge \widetilde v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

Only if this fails should one do charted denominator-clearing.

If charting is needed, cover the anisotropy sphere by:

\[
\mathcal U_1=\{|\Gamma_G|\ge c|\Gamma|\},
\]

\[
\mathcal U_2=\{|\Gamma_{G,2}|\ge c|\Gamma|\}.
\]

On each chart, denominators involving the active component are polynomially controlled.

---

# 8. Residual hierarchy

Split

\[
\mathbf R_N
=
\mathbf R_{\rm high}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm can}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm proj}
+
\mathbf R_{\rm norm}.
\]

Only the component in the leading image is dangerous.

## High-order local terms

All non-perturbative \(\Gamma\)-linear terms must be absorbed into \(L_N\). If a term has form

\[
q_0u^3(\alpha\Gamma_G+\beta\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

in any row, it modifies \(L_N\), not \(\mathbf R_N\).

After extracting the full \(L_N\), require:

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_{\rm high}|
\le
\frac18
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

## Canonical residual

If rows are canonical, no \(E_2\) transfer is needed. If rows are built from a truncated/model source, require row-depth transfer:

\[
\max_{k\le k_{\rm row}}|\mathcal D^kE_2|\le Q^{-A_E}
\]

with enough margin that

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_{\rm can}|
\le
\frac18
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

## Projection residual

Only the leading-image part matters:

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_{\rm proj}|
\le
\frac18
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

Projection residuals orthogonal to the leading image cannot cancel the leading term.

## Normalization residual

Absorb constant or polynomially bounded row normalizations into \(L_N\). Remaining variation must satisfy:

\[
|P_L\mathbf R_{\rm norm}|
\le
\frac18
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

---

# 9. Final D4 theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{D4 Row-Span Conversion Theorem.}
}
\]

Let \(\mathbf A_N\) be raw/ambient rows or projected rows. Suppose

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N,
\]

with

\[
\sigma_2(L_N)\ge Q^{-C_L},
\]

and

\[
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12
|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
\]

Then, under

\[
|q_0|\ge Q^{-C_q},
\]

\[
|u|^2|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

and either

\[
|u|\ge Q^{-C_u}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma|\le Q^{C_\Gamma},
\]

one gets:

\[
\boxed{
|\mathbf A_N|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

---

# 10. Exact finite audit checklist

To close D4, execute:

1. Compute

\[
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7),
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9).
\]

Expected:

\[
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
\qquad
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

2. Compute

\[
v_1=L_{\rm raw}e_1,
\qquad
v_2=L_{\rm raw}e_2.
\]

3. Verify raw exterior rank:

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

4. Decide row-energy target:

   * raw/ambient row energy;
   * quotient row energy;
   * projected row energy.

5. If projected row energy is required, compute:

\[
P_{\mathfrak f}v_1,
\qquad
P_{\mathfrak f}v_2,
\]

then check:

\[
|P_{\mathfrak f}v_1\wedge P_{\mathfrak f}v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

6. Extract the full effective \(L_N\), absorbing all same-scale \(\Gamma\)-linear terms.

7. Bound only the projected residual:

\[
P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}\mathbf R_N.
\]

---

# 11. Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is a finite row-span audit.}
}
\]

The shortest path is:

\[
\boxed{
R_7\sim H\Gamma_G,\qquad
R_9\sim H\Gamma_{G,2},\qquad
H\sim-\frac{q_0u^3}{288}.
}
\]

This gives raw exterior rank immediately.

Then either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raw/ambient row energy is accepted}
}
\]

or:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\mathfrak f}\text{ is coercive on }\operatorname{span}(v_1,v_2).
}
\]

Only the residual component in the leading anisotropy image needs to be bounded.

## Final audit — downstream route status

Current verdict:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Downstream is no longer a broad D1/D2/D3/D4 black-box stack.}
}
\]

It has reduced to two concrete chains:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 1}
+
|u'|_\infty\text{ source thickening}
+
L^2\text{-input integral rigidity}
\Rightarrow
\text{Gate 2}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2}
+
\text{wall escape}
+
\text{D4 row-span conversion}
+
\text{row mobility}
\Rightarrow
\text{row energy}.
}
\]

This is a real simplification. It does **not** prove RH, but it substantially localizes the remaining downstream work. 

---

# 1. Gate 2 audit

## G2-A′ — source thickening from one large \(u\) point

From Gate 1,

\[
\int_I |u|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-B}|I|,
\]

there exists

\[
m_\ast\in I
\]

with

\[
|u(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-B/2}.
\]

If

\[
|u'|_\infty\le Q^{C_u}|I|^{-1},
\]

then \(m_\ast\) thickens to an interval \(J\subset I\) with

\[
|u|\ge \tau\asymp Q^{-B/2}
\quad\text{on }J,
\]

and

\[
|J|\ge Q^{-B/2-C_u-O(1)}|I|.
\]

Then Gate 2 failure,

\[
\int_I |u|^2(g^2+h^2)\,dm\le Q^{-A}|I|,
\]

implies

\[
\frac1{|J|}\int_J(g^2+h^2)\,dm
\le
Q^{-A+\frac32B+C_u+O(1)}.
\]

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Near-Green.}}
\]

It only needs the local bound on \(u'\).

---

## G2-B′ — \(L^2\)-input integral rigidity

Target lemma:

\[
\boxed{
\|g\|_{L^2(J)}+\|h\|_{L^2(J)}
\le
\varepsilon |J|^{1/2}
\Rightarrow
\int_{J'}|u|^2
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^{2/5}|J'|.
}
\]

where

\[
g=q''-6q+2q^3,
\]

\[
h=2q(q')^2-\frac16g'',
\]

\[
u=q''=6q-2q^3+g.
\]

The current best proof uses the multiplier

\[
a_\delta(q)=q\chi_\delta(q),
\]

with

\[
a_\delta'(q)=O(1),
\qquad
a_\delta''(q)=O(\delta^{-1}).
\]

This should give the kinetic estimate

\[
\int_{|q|\ge2\delta}\phi(q')^2
\le
Q^C\varepsilon\delta^{-3}|J|,
\]

then the potential estimate, then

\[
\delta=\varepsilon^{1/5},
\]

yielding the \(2/5\) exponent.

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Near-Green, but still the main technical proof to write cleanly.}}
\]

The remaining work is cutoff bookkeeping: integration by parts, transition-annulus terms, and the residual-stable version.

---

## Gate 2 exponent flow

With

\[
\varepsilon
=
Q^{-A/2+\frac34B+\frac12C_u+O(1)},
\]

integral rigidity gives

\[
\int_{J'}|u|^2
\le
Q^{C_R}\varepsilon^{2/5}|J'|.
\]

But source thickening gives

\[
\int_{J'}|u|^2
\ge
Q^{-B-O(1)}|J'|.
\]

Contradiction if

\[
\boxed{
A>\frac{13}{2}B+C_u+5C_R+O(1).
}
\]

### Gate 2 verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate 2 is locally reduced to } |u'|\text{ control plus the }L^2\text{-rigidity lemma.}
}
\]

Old D1/D2 should now be treated as historical fallback, not active mainline.

---

# 2. Local complexity audit

The current Gate 2 route needs less than the previous package.

Minimum needed:

\[
|u|_\infty\le Q^C,
\qquad
|u'|_\infty\le Q^C|I|^{-1}.
\]

For rigidity, also need polynomial local bounds for

\[
q,\quad q',\quad q''.
\]

The clean formal approach is either:

1. prove direct completed-meromorphic bounds for \(q,q'\), or  
2. assume basepoint primitive admissibility:

\[
|q(m_b)|+|q'(m_b)|\le Q^C,
\]

then integrate \(u=q''\).

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Near-Green under ordinary zero-separated transfer, but primitive normalization must be fixed.}}
\]

This is now a local analytic-complexity item, not a broad arithmetic input.

---

# 3. Wall escape audit

Let

\[
q=q_0,
\qquad
u=q''_{\can}=q'',
\]

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\qquad
G=|u|^2|\Gamma|^2.
\]

Given Gate 2,

\[
\int_I G\ge Q^{-A}|I|,
\]

split

\[
I_{\rm nd}=\{|q|\ge \varepsilon\},
\qquad
I_{\rm wall}=\{|q|<\varepsilon\}.
\]

If non-wall mass is present, sample there and apply D4.

If wall mass dominates, discard low-source points \(|u|<\tau\). On the live wall strip,

\[
|q|<\varepsilon,\qquad |u|\ge\tau,
\]

the identity

\[
u=q''
\]

forces first exit to

\[
|q(m_1)|=\varepsilon,
\qquad
|u(m_1)|\ge \tau/2,
\]

provided

\[
C_\varepsilon\ge 3C_\tau+2C_{u'}+O(1).
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q+2q^3,
\]

and \(\varepsilon\ll \tau\) gives

\[
|\Gamma_G(m_1)|\ge c\tau.
\]

So

\[
G(m_1)\ge c\tau^4.
\]

Thus \(m_1\) is a non-wall D4 sample.

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Near-Green for row-energy / existential architectures.}}
\]

If the final contradiction requires exact-point row conversion, wall escape is insufficient and the \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil remains fallback.

---

# 4. Row mobility audit

If D4 gives

\[
|\mathbf A_N(m_\ast)|\ge Q^{-C_A},
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf A_N'|\le Q^{C_D}|I|^{-1},
\]

then there is \(J_\ast\subset I\) with

\[
|J_\ast|\ge Q^{-C}|I|,
\]

and

\[
|\mathbf A_N|\ge \frac12Q^{-C_A}
\quad\text{on }J_\ast.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\int_I|\mathbf A_N|^2\,dm\ge Q^{-C'}|I|.
}
\]

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Green under row derivative bounds.}}
\]

For moving quotient norms, add constraint-frame stability. For raw row energy, no moving quotient frame is needed.

---

# 5. D4 row-span audit

D4 is now best stated as row-span conversion.

Let

\[
\mathbf R_{\rm raw}
=
(R_7,R_9,R_{11},R_{13},\ldots)^T.
\]

Expected raw principal symbols:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_7)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_G,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_5(R_9)
=
-\frac1{288}q_0u^3\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

Thus

\[
v_1=-\frac1{288}e_7,
\qquad
v_2=-\frac1{288}e_9,
\]

and

\[
|v_1\wedge v_2|
=
\frac1{288^2}.
\]

So raw exterior rank is immediate if the raw symbol identities are verified.

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Finite audit.}}
\]

Need exact row formulas to certify the two symbols.

---

## D4 residual control

Normal form:

\[
\mathbf A_N=q_0u^3L_N\Gamma+\mathbf R_N.
\]

Need only projected residual control:

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\operatorname{im}L_N}\mathbf R_N|
\le
\frac12|q_0||u|^3\sigma_2(L_N)|\Gamma|.
}
\]

Full residual norm control is unnecessary.

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Finite audit.}}
\]

Need exact residual formulas after extracting the principal row response.

---

## Projected row issue

If raw/ambient/quotient row energy is acceptable, no projection audit is needed.

If projected row energy is required, need only coercivity on the anisotropy plane:

\[
\boxed{
|P_{\mathfrak f}v_1\wedge P_{\mathfrak f}v_2|\ge Q^{-C}.
}
\]

This is much weaker than global projection regularity.

### Status

\[
\boxed{\text{Optional finite audit, depending on final row-energy target.}}
\]

---

| Module | Status | Remaining work |
|---|---|---|
| Source thickening from Gate 1 | Green / near-Green | needs \(|u'|\) bound |

| Gate 2 theorem | Near-Green | combine exponent flow cleanly |

| Wall escape | Near-Green | boundary layer and exponent bookkeeping |
| Row mobility | Green | row derivative bounds |

| D4 projected residual | Finite audit | compute \(P_L\mathbf R_N\) |
| Projection coercivity | Optional finite audit | only if projected rows are mandatory |
| \(q_0\)-adic row-pencil | Fallback | only if exact-point row architecture is required |

+
|u'|_\infty\text{ source thickening}

\text{Gate 2}.

\Rightarrow

| type | condition | disposition |
|---|---|---|
| same-scale | \((a,b,c)=(1,b_\ast,1)\) | absorb into \(L_\ast\) or \(M_Q\) |
| higher valuation | \(a>1\) or \(b>b_\ast\) or \(c>1\) | controlled by valuation, if thresholds permit |
| absolute residual | independent of \(q_0,u,\Gamma\) enough to gain valuation | needs explicit threshold-corridor bound |
| quotient-null | lies in \(\mathcal C_N\) | vanishes in quotient |
| quotient-orthogonal | orthogonal to leading anisotropy plane | cannot cancel leading term |

C_\varepsilon+(b_\ast+1)C_\tau+C_L+O(1).

X_0N_7X_0 & \text{n/a} & \text{skew, projected away} & 0\\
E_{\rm GateA/B}^{(7)} & \text{n/a} & \text{closed} & 0\\
E_{\rm conn}^{(7)} & \text{n/a} & \text{absorbed} & 0\\
E_{\rm shadow/pure}^{(7)} & \text{n/a} & \text{projected/vanishes} & 0\\
R_{\rm line}^{(7)} & \text{not guaranteed} & \text{line nuisance} & quotient or include coordinate\\
R_{\rm bg/core}^{(7)} & \text{not certified} & \text{baseline/correction} & quotient or include coordinate\\
R_{\rm norm/sub}^{(7)} & \text{not certified} & \text{normalization correction} & quotient or include coordinate\\
\hline
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3} & \text{yes} & \text{live order-9 channel} & H\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ coordinate}\\
R_{\rm line}^{I,(9)} & \text{not certified} & \text{line/reference-row term} & must vanish, quotient, or include\\
R_{\rm bg/core}^{I,(9)} & \text{not certified} & \text{baseline/core term} & must vanish, quotient, or include\\
R_{\rm norm/sub}^{I,(9)} & \text{not certified} & \text{normalization/subtraction term} & must vanish, quotient, or include\\
E_{\rm conn}^{I,(9)} & \text{absorbed} & \text{covariant connection} & 0\\
E_{\rm GateA/B}^{I,(9)} & \text{closed} & \text{Gate closure} & 0\\
E_{\rm shadow/pure}^{I,(9)} & \text{projected/vanishes} & \text{not fixed-sector live} & 0
\end{array}
\]

---

# 7. D4 status

D4 is **not closed**.

It is closed only under the additional correction theorem:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}\in H\mathcal O
\quad\text{or}\quad
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}\text{ is quotiented/absorbed.}
}
\]

Equivalently, if the row-output norm is a quotient norm, we need the nuisance space \(\mathcal C_N\) to include the line/background/normalization correction directions and preserve the independent classes of the live \(A_7,A_9\) channels.

That quotient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
[e_7]\wedge[e_9]\neq0
\quad
\text{in }
\wedge^2(\mathcal H_N/\mathcal C_N).
}
\]

But the current files do not define \(\mathcal C_N\) or prove that it absorbs \(R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}\).

---

# 8. Exact blocker

The exact D4 blocker is:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}
=
R_{\rm line}^{I,(9)}
+
R_{\rm bg/core}^{I,(9)}
+
R_{\rm norm/sub}^{I,(9)}.
}
\]

We need one of the following finite outcomes:

1. **Vanishing**

\[
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}=0.
\]

2. **Divisibility**

\[
R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}\in H\mathcal O.
\]

3. **Absorption / quotient**

A defined nuisance space \(\mathcal C_N\) contains the correction directions, while \([e_7],[e_9]\) survive independently.

4. **Source-robust row**

A non-\(H\)-divisible correction is proved packet-visible and RH-obstructive.

At present, none is established. The available audits explicitly warn that non-\(H\)-divisible corrections are not automatically useful source-robust rows; they may just be line/background/normalization nuisances. 

---

# 9. Final answer

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is not closed from the current paper algebra.}
}
\]

What is closed:

\[
\boxed{
\text{order-7 live row is }H\text{-divisible and detects }\Gamma_G.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{order-9 live row is }H\text{-divisible and detects }\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

What blocks closure:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the order-9 correction package }R_{\rm corr}^{I,(9)}
\text{ is not proved zero, absorbed, or }H\text{-divisible.}
}
\]

Next exact action:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute or eliminate }
R_{\rm line}^{I,(9)},\quad
R_{\rm bg/core}^{I,(9)},\quad
R_{\rm norm/sub}^{I,(9)}.
}
\]

Without that, the clean D4 quotient/projective row conversion remains conditional.

## Agent 5 / E — D4 finite row audit result

I audited the available row-table material. The result is sharper than before:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The order-7 and clean order-9 live channels are real.}
}
\]

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is not fully closed unless we either quotient/avoid or control the order-9 cross-channel rows.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Order-7 row: confirmed live \(\Gamma_G\) channel

The order-7 fixed-sector row is

\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}T_7^{(1)}
=
-2yBr\,I,
\]

where

\[
r=\frac12(g^{-1/2}-h^{-1/2}).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}T_7^{(1)}
=
-y\frac{q''(h-g)}
{4\pi gh(\sqrt g+\sqrt h)^2}I.
\]

Since

\[
g-h=-\frac{\Gamma_G}{6\pi},
\]

this row is controlled by

\[
yq''\Gamma_G.
\]

Thus the order-7 channel gives the first anisotropy direction:

\[
\boxed{
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\sim H\Gamma_G.
}
\]

This is valid away from the source walls

\[
yq''=0
\]

and denominator walls such as \(g=0,h=0\). 

---

# 2. Order-9 candidate: confirmed live \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel

On the first Gram-isotropy wall \(g=h\), the order-9 candidate row is

\[
T_9^{(1,2)}
=
X_{-,1}YX_{+,2}+X_{-,2}YX_{+,1}.
\]

With

\[
X_1=BS,\qquad Y=yJ,\qquad X_2=AI+CD,
\]

centered parity gives

\[
T_9^{(1,2)}
=
X_1YX_2-X_2YX_1.
\]

Using

\[
SJ=-D,\qquad JS=D,
\]

one obtains

\[
T_9^{(1,2)}
=
-2yB(AD+CI),
\]

and the fixed-sector projection keeps only the \(I\)-component:

\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\mathfrak f}T_9^{(1,2)}
=
-2yBC\,I.
}
\]

With

\[
C=(X_2)_D
=
-\frac1{32\pi}g^{-3/2}\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

this becomes

\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\mathfrak f}T_9^{(1,2)}
=
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3}I.
}
\]

So the order-9 clean candidate gives the second anisotropy direction:

\[
\boxed{
A_9^{\rm clean}\sim H\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

This is a genuine new channel, replacing the order-7 \(X_{0,D}\) anisotropy by \(X_{2,D}\). 

---

# 3. Direct \(N_9\) raw carrier: harmless

The direct raw order-9 carrier satisfies

\[
N_9^I=0
\]

at centered parity. More precisely,

\[
N_9=F_9J,
\]

so

\[
X_0N_9X_0=g^{-1}F_9J,
\]

and

\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(J)=0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_0N_9X_0)=0.
}
\]

So direct \(N_9\) cannot cancel the order-9 candidate

\[
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3}I.
\]

This part is clean. 

---

# 4. \(N_7\)-family: cross-channel relevant, not structurally fatal

The centered order-9 companion from the \(N_7\)-family is

\[
\mathcal R_{2,7}
=
X_2N_7X_0-X_1N_7X_1+X_0N_7X_2.
\]

With

\[
X_0=pI,\qquad X_1=BS,\qquad X_2=aI+dD,
\]

its \(I\)-sector component is

\[
\boxed{
\pi_I(\mathcal R_{2,7})
=
(2pa-B^2)N_7^I+2pd\,N_7^D.
}
\]

The \(N_7^S\) and \(N_7^J\) sectors do not contribute to the \(I\)-coordinate.

So \(N_7^{I,D}\) can contribute to the same \(I\)-coordinate as the order-9 candidate, but not through the same carrier channel. Exact cancellation would require the analytic identity

\[
(2pa-B^2)N_7^I+2pd\,N_7^D
+
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3}
=0
\]

after including all other order-9 \(I\)-sector companions. This is a cross-channel incidence condition, not a sector-forced cancellation. 

---

# 5. \(N_5\)-family: cross-channel relevant, not structurally fatal

The \(N_5\)-family can also produce order-9 \(I\)-sector terms.

For \(N_5^I\):

\[
\boxed{
\pi_I(\mathcal R_5^{(9)}[N_5^I])
=
N_5^I(2pe-2bc+a^2+d^2)I.
}
\]

For \(N_5^D\):

\[
\boxed{
\pi_I(\mathcal R_5^{(9)}[N_5^D])
=
N_5^D(2pf+2ad)I.
}
\]

But

\[
N_5^S,\quad N_5^J
\]

do not contribute to the \(I\)-coordinate.

So the \(N_5^{I,D}\)-terms can affect the full order-9 \(I\)-component, but they are different raw-carrier channels. They enlarge the cross-channel incidence wall; they do not structurally kill the candidate \(Y_JX_{1,S}X_{2,D}\) row. 

---

# 6. Source-side nonvanishing: positive

The order-9 source factor is

\[
yq''\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

For a centered off-line packet, the source-side audit confirms that this is generically nonzero. In particular,

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
=
(1-q_0^2)q''-\frac16q^{(4)}
\]

is not forced to vanish by the first wall \(\Gamma_G=0\). An explicit clean packet example gives

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
=
-\frac{12449792}{243}\ne0.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_G=0\not\Rightarrow \Gamma_{G,2}=0.
}
\]

So the order-9 channel is a genuine local improvement over order 7. 

---

# 7. Cross-channel incidence wall

The full order-9 \(I\)-component is

\[
(A_9^{\mathfrak f})_I
=
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3}
+
R_9^I,
\]

where \(R_9^I\) is the sum of all other order-9 \(I\)-sector rows, including \(N_7^{I,D}\), \(N_5^{I,D}\), and any lower-carrier / high-whitening terms.

Define the source wall:

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm source}^{(9)}
=
\{y=0\}\cup\{q''=0\}\cup\{\Gamma_{G,2}=0\}\cup\{g=0\}.
\]

On \(g>0\), omit \(\{g=0\}\).

Define the genuine cross-channel wall:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}
=
\left\{
R_9^I
=
-\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3},
\quad
yq''\Gamma_{G,2}\ne0
\right\}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
F_9^I
:=
R_9^I+
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3},
\]

and

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}
=
\{F_9^I=0\}\setminus \mathcal E_{\rm source}^{(9)}.
\]

This is a proper analytic wall if

\[
\boxed{
F_9^I\not\equiv0.
}
\]

The available row-table audits support that cancellation is not sector-forced, but they do not yet prove the non-identity globally. 

---

# 8. D4 status after audit

## What is closed

\[
\boxed{
\text{Order 7 detects }\Gamma_G\text{ through }H\Gamma_G.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Order 9 clean channel detects }\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ through }H\Gamma_{G,2}.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
N_9\text{ is harmless by centered parity.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
N_7^{I,D}\text{ and }N_5^{I,D}\text{ are cross-channel companions, not structural killers.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ is a genuine second anisotropy, not forced by }\Gamma_G.
}
\]

## What remains open

\[
\boxed{
F_9^I
=
R_9^I+
\frac{yq''\Gamma_{G,2}}{128\pi^2g^3}
\not\equiv0
}
\]

must still be proved, or the theorem must explicitly exclude

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}.
\]

Thus D4 is locally closed **outside** the order-9 cross-channel incidence wall. It is not globally closed without either:

1. proving \(\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}\) is a proper wall and avoiding it quantitatively; or  
2. computing \(R_9^I\) fully and proving the candidate cannot be cancelled; or  
3. enlarging to order 11 / ambient rows if order 9 is structurally cancelled.

---

# 9. Final theorem-level statement

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Layered fixed-sector visibility through order 9.}
}
\]

On a centered reduced good patch with

\[
g>0,\qquad h>0,\qquad yq''\ne0,
\]

define

\[
\Gamma_G=q''-6q_0+2q_0^3,
\]

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=(1-q_0^2)q''-\frac16q^{(4)}.
\]

Outside the order-7 and order-9 cross-channel incidence walls:

\[
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(7)}\cup\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)},
\]

one has:

\[
\Gamma_G\ne0
\Longrightarrow
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\ne0,
\]

and, on \(\Gamma_G=0\),

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}\ne0
\Longrightarrow
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\ne0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
yq''\ne0,\quad
\theta\notin
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(7)}\cup\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)},
\quad
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\ne(0,0)
\]

implies

\[
\boxed{
(A_7^{\mathfrak f},A_9^{\mathfrak f})\ne0.
}
\]

This is a local visibility theorem. It is not yet the quantitative D4 row-energy lower bound because quantitative avoidance of \(\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}\) remains open.

---

# 10. Final verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is locally viable through order 9, but not globally closed.}
}
\]

The exact remaining D4 blocker is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{quantitative control or avoidance of the order-9 cross-channel wall }
\mathcal E_{\rm cc}^{(9)}.
}
\]

Next finite action:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute the full }R_9^I\text{ row table or prove }
F_9^I\not\equiv0
\text{ with a witness.}
}
\]

\[

=
